Beyond 15 feet, overhand gives you the carry needed. Matching the throw to the distance is the mark of a skilled pivot man.

Always throwing overhand adds unnecessary arm movement on close plays. Within 15 feet, the underhand flip reaches SS in half the time with more control. Overhand at close range creates looping throws that arrive late.
The backhand shovel is a desperation move for balls hit to your glove side when you're off-balance. On a ball hit right at you, use the standard flip or overhand based on distance.

Waiting for SS to set defeats the purpose of the DP. The timing is choreographed — SS starts moving to the bag as you field the ball. Your job is to deliver immediately so he arrives just as the throw does.
scenarios.concept: DP feed to SS: underhand flip close in, overhand throw from deep

scenarios.title: The Bunt — Who Takes First?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, nobody out. Batter squares to bunt. First baseman charges hard. Where do you go?
options: Cover first base — 1B is charging, you take the bag
Stay at your position and let first handle it
Cover second in case the runner advances
Back up the catcher
explanations: Correct. When first base charges a bunt, second base covers first. This is the standard bunt defense rule — it's in every level's defensive coverage scheme. You sprint to the bag, receive the throw from the charging 1B or pitcher, and record the out. If you don't cover, first base is a ghost bag.

'Let first handle it' works only if first base isn't charging. On a hard charge play, 1B physically cannot get back to the bag. Second base must fill it — this is a non-negotiable coverage assignment.
Covering second when the runner is already committed to advancing there makes sense only if there's a play on the lead runner — not on a standard sac bunt with just a runner at first.
Backing up the catcher on a bunt? The catcher fields the ball or directs traffic. You are needed at first base. Backup catcher duty on bunts belongs to the pitcher on certain coverage schemes.
scenarios.concept: On bunt defense: 2B covers first when 1B charges

scenarios.title: Hit and Run — Your Read
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st takes off on the pitch. It's a hit and run. Ball is hit sharply up the middle. What do you do?
options: Abandon covering 2nd — field the ball up the middle
Keep going to cover 2nd — SS will field the ball
Stop and let SS take everything up the middle
Wave the runner through and focus on the next hitter
explanations: Correct. A sharply hit ball up the middle on a hit-and-run is YOUR ball if it's in your range. The offense is trying to exploit the hole created when you leave for 2B — recognize this and attack the ball. Fielding the sharp grounder prevents the hit, possibly catches the runner off base, and kills the hit-and-run play. If you can reach it, get it.
Going to cover 2nd when a ball is hit sharply in your zone is exactly what the offense wants. The hit-and-run is designed to exploit the hole your movement creates. Read the batted ball and react.
Stopping mid-coverage to let SS handle everything up the middle is indecisive. The read is simple: if the ball is in your zone (right side of middle), take it. If it's to SS's side, cover 2nd.
'Wave the runner through' makes no sense on defense. Your job is to field the ball or cover the base — not signal anything to baserunners.
scenarios.concept: Hit and run: 2B must read the ball — field if in range, cover if not

scenarios.title: Relay from Right Field — Who Takes It?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, fly ball drops in right field for a double. Deep in the RF corner. Who takes the relay and where do you go?
options: SS takes the relay — you back up second base
You take the relay — SS covers second
Both you and SS go out for a double-cut
You cover third — SS takes relay, 2B covers second
explanations: Correct. On balls to right field, the shortstop is the primary relay man because his arm angle to third base is optimal. Second base backs up the SS and covers second base. If SS's relay throw is cut, you're in position to relay to second. This is the standard RF relay assignment at every level.
On RF balls, second base is NOT the primary relay man. Your arm angle to third base is awkward — you'd have to turn completely around. SS handles this relay; your role is backup and bag coverage.
Double-cut relay (both 2B and SS going out) is used on extra-base hits to the outfield gaps — not a standard RF corner double. Double-cuts add personnel but create a hole at second base if misused.
Covering third on a RF ball makes no sense for second base. Third base covers third. Your responsibilities are: back up SS relay, cover second base.
scenarios.concept: RF relay: SS is primary relay man, 2B backs up and covers second

scenarios.title: First and Third — Your Role
scenarios.description: Runners on 1st and 3rd, 1 out. The runner on 1st breaks for 2nd. The catcher fires down. You're the fielder covering 2nd. What's your read as the throw comes in?
options: Fake a tag, then check the runner at 3rd before throwing home
Always apply the tag and throw home immediately
Let the runner have 2nd — don't tag, hold the ball
Fire to third base to trap the runner
explanations: Correct. The fake tag (or bluff tag) on a 1st-and-3rd steal is a classic defender's tool. Show the tag to stop the runner at 2B from reversing, then immediately read the runner at 3rd. If he hasn't committed to home, hold the ball — you've just stopped the run-scoring threat. If he goes, fire home. Blind throws home give up free bases and often wild pitch situations.

Always throwing home gives the offense exactly what they want. Many 1st-and-3rd steals are designed to get the catcher to throw so the 3rd-base runner can score. Make the runner earn it — read first, throw second.
Letting the runner have 2nd with no contest is too passive in a tie game in the 7th. Use the fake tag to control the baserunner — you get information for free.
Throwing to 3rd makes no sense — the runner is already standing there. You'd be giving up 2nd base and making a cross-diamond throw with a runner baiting you at home.
scenarios.concept: 1st and 3rd: fake the tag at 2nd, read the runner at 3rd before throwing

scenarios.title: Cutoff Decision
scenarios.description: You're the SS cutoff. Deep hit to left-center, runner from 1st trying to score. Throw coming to you.
options: Cut it off and throw home
Let the throw go through
Cut and hold
Cut and throw to 3rd
explanations: Your relay is faster and more accurate than a bouncing outfield throw.
Letting throws through only works on perfect one-hoppers.
Holding lets the tying run score with no play.
The runner scoring is the main threat — throw home because that's where the action is.
scenarios.concept: The cutoff man relays accurate throws home — catch and throw, don't let it go

scenarios.title: Ground Ball to Short — Runner on First
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, runner on 1st, nobody out. You're at shortstop. Ground ball hit right at you. The runner isn't very fast.
options: Throw to second to start the double play
Throw to first for the sure out
Tag the runner yourself
Hold the ball and check the runner
explanations: With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is the best outcome. Flip to second base, the middle infielder turns it, and you get two outs on one play. That's how you get out of innings fast!
Throwing to first gets one out but advances the runner to second — now he's in scoring position. The double play clears the bases.
Tagging the runner as a shortstop would require chasing him. Throw to second for the force.
Holding the ball with a runner advancing means nobody is out. Make a play!
scenarios.concept: With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is always the first thought

scenarios.title: First and Second — Start the DP
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, runners on 1st and 2nd, nobody out, tied 4-4. You're at shortstop. Hard ground ball right at you. Both runners are going.
options: Step on second and throw to first — double play
Throw to third for the force out
Throw to first for the sure out
Tag the runner coming from first
explanations: With runners on 1st and 2nd, stepping on second starts the double play — the runner from first is forced at second, then a quick throw to first gets the batter. Two outs!
Throwing to third gets one out but lets the batter reach first. The double play is worth more.
Throwing to first gets one out but advances both runners into scoring position.
Tagging one runner still leaves the other runners advancing. The double play is the best play.
scenarios.concept: With runners on 1st and 2nd, step on second and throw to first for the double play

scenarios.title: Runner Breaks from Third
scenarios.description: Bot 7th, runner on 3rd, 1 out, down 3-4. You're at shortstop playing normal depth. Slow ground ball to your left — you field it, but the runner on 3rd breaks for home!
options: Throw home to get the runner
Throw to first — get the sure out
Hold the ball — check if the runner commits
Throw to third — maybe he'll go back
explanations: On a slow roller from normal depth, you had to range to your left and take time to field it. The runner broke on contact — by now he's almost home. A long throw across your body to the plate will likely be late or wild. Know when you don't have the play.

This is the smart play. On a slow grounder from normal depth, the runner breaking on contact will beat your throw home. Take the sure out at first — don't let the batter reach base too. Give up the run you can't stop, get the out you can.

While reading the runner is good instinct, on a slow roller you took too long to field it. The runner is already committed and almost home. Holding the ball means no out anywhere — now you've given up the run AND the out.
The runner already committed to home — throwing to an empty third base is a wasted throw. You need to get an out somewhere.
scenarios.concept: On a slow roller from normal depth, the runner from third will beat your throw home — take the sure out at first

scenarios.title: Cutoff Man Position on a Relay
scenarios.description: Top of the 6th, runner on 1st, nobody out. Double to left-center gap. You're the shortstop going out as the relay man. Where do you position yourself?
options: In a straight line between the outfielder and home plate
In a straight line between the outfielder and third base
Halfway between the outfielder and the infield
As close to the outfielder as possible
explanations: Line yourself up directly between the outfielder and home plate. Raise your arms so the outfielder can see you and has a target. The throw from the outfielder should come to you chest-high, and you catch-and-fire to home in one motion. The relay man is always an infielder — the pitcher is sprinting to back up a base, not cut off a throw.
Lining up toward third only works if you know the throw is going to third. Default to home — you can always redirect to third after catching it.
Halfway is too vague. You need to be in the optimal relay distance — close enough to receive a strong throw but far enough to get momentum on your throw home.
Being too close to the outfielder makes your relay throw longer. Position yourself about 100-130 feet from home.
scenarios.concept: As the relay man, line up between the outfielder and home plate — give the outfielder a clear target

scenarios.title: Double Play Depth
scenarios.description: Top of the 4th, runner on 1st, nobody out, tied 2-2. You're the shortstop. Where should you position yourself?
options: Double play depth — a few steps closer to 2nd base
Normal depth — play your regular spot
Deep in the hole — maximize range
Shift toward the pull side
explanations: With a runner on first and nobody out, move to double play depth. This means moving a few steps toward second base and a step or two in (closer to home). This shorter path to second base lets you start the double play faster. You sacrifice a little range in the hole, but the DP is worth it.
Normal depth makes the double play harder — you have to cover more ground to get to second.
Deep in the hole maximizes your range but makes the throw to second for the DP much longer.
Shifting without knowing the hitter's tendencies is guessing.
scenarios.concept: Double play depth means a few steps toward 2nd and a step in — shorter path to start the DP

scenarios.title: Fast Runner at the Plate — Adjust Depth
scenarios.description: Top of the 8th, nobody on, 1 out, up 3-2. Their fastest player steps in — he can beat out routine ground balls if you play too deep. You're the shortstop.
options: Play a step or two in — shorten the throw to first
Play normal depth — don't change for one batter
Play deep — get more range
Shade toward the hole — fast guys pull the ball
explanations: Against fast runners, cheat in a step or two. A shorter throw to first means the ball gets there faster. You're trading a tiny bit of range for a quicker throw — which matters against speed. The difference between safe and out against a fast runner is often 0.1 seconds.
Normal depth against a speedster means routine grounders become infield singles. Adjust!
Playing deep gives you more range but a longer throw. Against a fast runner, the long throw means he beats it out.
There's no evidence this fast player is a pull hitter. Don't guess — adjust your depth.
scenarios.concept: Against fast runners, play a step in — a shorter throw to first compensates for their speed

scenarios.title: Relay from Deep Center — Who Goes Where?
scenarios.description: Bot 8th, runner on 1st, nobody out. Ball hit to deep center — it's a triple. The center fielder is chasing it to the wall. Who goes out for the relay?
options: Shortstop goes out for the relay, second baseman covers second
Second baseman goes out, shortstop covers second
Both middle infielders go out — one for each possible target
The third baseman goes out as the relay man
explanations: On balls to center and left-center, the shortstop goes out as the relay man. He lines up between the outfielder and home plate, arms raised as a target. The second baseman stays and covers second base. This is the standard relay alignment.
The second baseman goes out for relay throws to the right side (right-center and right field). On balls to center and left, it's the shortstop's job.
Having both go out leaves second base uncovered. One goes, one stays.
The third baseman stays at third to take a possible throw. The shortstop is the relay man on balls to center.
scenarios.concept: On balls to center/left, the shortstop is the relay man — the second baseman covers second base

scenarios.title: Deep in the Hole
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. No outs, nobody on base. The batter hits a sharp ground ball deep in the hole between short and third. You range far to your right, backhand the ball, and now you're deep behind third base. What's the play?
options: Plant your back foot hard, set your feet, and make a strong overhand throw across the diamond to first
Flip the ball to the third baseman and let him throw to first
Lob a soft throw to first to make sure it's accurate
Run toward first base to shorten the throw before letting it go
explanations: This is the signature shortstop play! After a deep backhand, you plant your back foot hard to stop your momentum, set your feet, and fire a strong throw across the diamond. It takes a cannon arm and great footwork — that's why shortstop is a premium position.
The third baseman isn't expecting a flip, and by the time he catches it and throws, the runner is safe. You have to make this throw yourself — it's one of the most important plays a shortstop makes.
A lob throw from deep in the hole will never beat the runner. You're far from first base and the batter is sprinting. You need your strongest, most accurate throw to get the out in time.
Running toward first eats up valuable time. The batter is faster than you running toward first, so every step you take closer costs you. Plant and throw — your arm strength is what gets this out.
scenarios.concept: On a deep backhand in the hole, plant hard, set your feet, and make a strong throw across the diamond — this is the shortstop's signature play.

scenarios.title: Starting the Double Play
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first, one out, and the batter hits a ground ball right at you. You field it cleanly. You need to start the double play by getting the ball to second base. What's the best way to feed the second baseman?
options: Make a quick, chest-high throw to the second baseman at the bag — firm but easy to handle
Shovel a hard underhand flip right at his feet
Throw the ball as hard as you possibly can to save time
Run the ball over to second base yourself and then throw to first
explanations: A firm, chest-high throw right to the second baseman's glove is the perfect double play feed. It gives him a clean catch and lets him turn the play quickly. Accuracy matters more than speed on the feed — help your teammate make the turn!
An underhand flip at his feet is hard to handle and slows down the turn. The second baseman has to reach down, adjust, and then throw — that extra time lets the runner reach first safely.
Throwing as hard as you can might be wild or hard for the second baseman to catch. A firm, accurate throw is much better than a bullet that sails into center field. Control your throw!
Running the ball to second wastes way too much time. By the time you get there, the runner from first is already at second, and the batter is almost to first. Use your arm — that's what it's for!
scenarios.concept: On a double play feed to second, make a firm chest-high throw the second baseman can handle — accuracy beats raw speed every time.

scenarios.title: Covering on the Steal
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first with good speed, and you suspect he's going to steal second. The pitcher delivers, and the runner takes off! The catcher pops up and fires to second. You're covering the bag. How should you set up?
options: Get to the bag early, straddle it with your left foot on the outfield side, and give the catcher a clear target with your glove
Stand in front of the bag blocking the base path so the runner can't reach it
Get to the bag and jump out of the way as the throw arrives to avoid getting hit by the runner
Wait at your normal position until you see the throw, then run to the bag
explanations: Perfect steal coverage! Getting to the bag early gives the catcher a clear target. Straddling with your left foot on the outfield side keeps you out of the runner's path while letting you sweep a tag down in front of the base. Quick glove, quick tag, out!
Blocking the base path without the ball is obstruction — the umpire will award the runner the base! You need to straddle the bag and leave a lane for the runner to slide, then apply the tag.

Jumping out of the way means you won't be in position to make the tag. Yes, the runner is coming hard, but you need to be strong at the bag. Straddle it so you can apply the tag and let the runner slide past you on the other side.

Waiting at your normal position means the catcher has no target and you won't get to the bag in time. You need to anticipate the steal and get to the bag as the pitch is delivered, not after the throw.
scenarios.concept: On steal coverage, get to the bag early, straddle it to give the catcher a target, and sweep the tag down in front of the base.

scenarios.title: Pop Fly in Shallow Left
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Bases empty, two outs. The batter pops the ball up into shallow left field. It's a tweener — the left fielder is charging in and you're drifting back. The third baseman is nearby too. Who takes this ball?
options: Call for it yourself — you're an infielder going back on the ball
Let the left fielder take it — he's charging in with the ball in front of him
Let the third baseman call for it since he's closer to that side
Nobody calls it — whoever gets there first just catches it
explanations: Going back on a fly ball is one of the hardest plays in baseball — the ball drifts AWAY from you and you're running blind. The left fielder charging in has it in front of him the whole way, which is a much easier catch. Let the outfielder take this one.

Correct! The outfielder coming in always has priority over the infielder going back. Running forward, the left fielder can see the ball in front of him and has momentum toward the infield for a throw. Going back, you're chasing a ball drifting away from you — that's the toughest catch in baseball. Call him off? Never. Let him call YOU off.

The third baseman would have to range a long way to his left for this ball. Even if he got there, he'd be going back too — same problem as the shortstop. The outfielder coming in has the best angle by far.
Never leave a pop fly uncalled! When nobody calls it, players crash into each other or the ball drops between them. Someone MUST take charge — and on tweeners, the outfielder is the one who calls it.
scenarios.concept: Outfielders coming in have priority over infielders going back — running forward with the ball in front of you is always easier than chasing it over your shoulder.

scenarios.title: Relay Man to Left-Center
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first, one out. The batter hits a deep fly ball to left-center field that drops and rolls to the wall. The runner from first is rounding second and heading for third — and he might keep going. You need to be the relay man. Where do you go?
options: Run out to line up between the left-center fielder and third base, arms up high so the outfielder can see you
Line up between the outfielder and home plate, arms up
Stay at shortstop position and let the third baseman handle it
Go to second base in case the batter tries to stretch it to a double
explanations: Lining up to third can work if the runner clearly stops, but your default should always be toward home. Preventing runs is the priority, and your teammates can yell to redirect you if needed.

Perfect relay positioning! Your default is always to line up between the outfielder and home plate — preventing runs is the #1 priority. Put your arms up high so the outfielder has a target, and listen to your teammates. They'll tell you where to throw. If the runner stops at third, you can cut it and hold.
The third baseman needs to be at third base to receive the throw! He can't be out doing the relay. The shortstop is always the relay man on balls hit to the left side of the outfield.

The second baseman or another infielder can cover second. Your priority as shortstop on a deep left-center hit is to get out there as the relay man. Without a relay, the outfielder has to make an impossible long throw.
scenarios.concept: On relay plays, the default alignment is toward home plate — preventing runs is always the priority.

scenarios.title: Do-Or-Die With Runner on Third
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on third, one out, tie game in the bottom of the last inning. The batter hits a slow chopper toward you — not hit hard, and the runner on third is breaking for home the moment the ball is hit. This is a do-or-die play. What do you do?
options: Charge the ball hard, field it with your bare hand if needed, and fire home to try to get the runner
Play it safe — field the ball cleanly and throw to first for the sure out
Wait for the ball to come to you, then decide where to throw
Let the third baseman field it since he's closer to home
explanations: This is the do-or-die play! With the game on the line and the runner going home, you MUST charge hard and throw home. Even a bare-hand pickup and throw gives you a chance to save the game. If you throw to first, the runner scores and the game is over.

Throwing to first gets an out, but the runner from third scores and the game is over — you lose! In a tie game with a runner breaking from third, you have to try to get the runner at home. The out at first doesn't matter if the winning run scores.

Waiting for the ball to come to you wastes precious time. The runner is sprinting home — every fraction of a second matters. You have to charge aggressively and make a quick play. There's no time to be patient here.

The ball is hit toward you, not the third baseman. Even if the third baseman could get it, he'd have to field it and then spin to throw home. You're already facing home plate as you charge in — it's your play to make.
scenarios.concept: On a do-or-die play with a runner scoring from third, charge hard and throw home — the out at first doesn't matter if the winning run scores.

scenarios.title: Positioning for a Pull Hitter
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Nobody on base, two outs. A big right-handed pull hitter is stepping up to bat. Your coach tells you to shade toward the third base side. Where should you position yourself?
options: Move a few steps toward third base from your normal position — right-handed pull hitters tend to hit toward the left side of the infield
Stay in your normal shortstop position because you don't want to leave gaps
Move a few steps toward second base to cover the middle
Move way over right next to the third baseman
explanations: Smart positioning! Right-handed pull hitters hit the ball to the left side of the field most of the time. By shading a few steps toward third, you put yourself right where the ball is most likely to be hit. Good defense is about being in the right spot BEFORE the ball is hit.

Your normal position is designed for an average hitter. But this is a known pull hitter, so the ball is more likely to be hit toward third. Adjusting your position a few steps gives you a much better chance at making the play.
Moving toward second takes you AWAY from where a pull hitter is most likely to hit the ball. That's the opposite of what you want. You'd be leaving the hot zone unprotected.

Moving right next to the third baseman is too extreme. You'd leave a huge gap between you and second base. A few steps toward third is enough — you still need to cover your zone. Subtle adjustments make a big difference.
scenarios.concept: Against pull hitters, shade a few steps toward the side they tend to hit — positioning yourself where the ball is most likely to go is smart defense.

scenarios.title: Bare-Hand the Swinging Bunt
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on second, two outs, one-run game. The batter barely makes contact — a weak dribbler rolling slowly up the third base line. It's dying in the grass between the mound and third. The third baseman is playing deep. Can you make the play?
options: Charge full speed, bare-hand the ball on the run, and make a quick throw to first in one motion
Let the third baseman come in for it since it's on his side
Wait for the ball to stop rolling, then pick it up with your glove and throw
Charge in but field it with your glove first, then transfer and throw
explanations: This is the ultimate athletic play! The ball is dying in the grass, so you have to charge full speed. Bare-handing lets you grab it and throw in one motion — no glove transfer needed. It's risky but it's the only way to get the runner at first in time. Elite shortstop play!

The third baseman is playing deep and has to cover more ground than you. Plus, he'd be running in and to his left, which is a harder angle. You can charge straight in — you have the better path to this ball.

If you wait for the ball to stop, the batter is safe at first by a mile. On a slow dribbler like this, every millisecond counts. You can't play it safe — you have to be aggressive and make a bang-bang play.

Fielding with your glove means you have to transfer the ball to your throwing hand, and that extra step takes too long on a slow roller. The bare-hand scoop-and-throw is one motion. When the ball is rolling that slowly, you need the bare hand.
scenarios.concept: On a dying swinging bunt, charge hard and bare-hand it — the scoop-and-throw in one motion is the only way to beat the runner on a slow roller.

scenarios.title: Ranging Deep in the Hole
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first, two outs. The batter hits a hard ground ball deep in the hole between short and third. You range far to your right, backhand the ball cleanly, and now you're almost behind third base. The runner on first is moving. What's your best play?
options: Plant your back foot hard, set up, and fire a strong overhand throw across the diamond to first base
Flip the ball to the third baseman and let him make the throw to first
Lob a high, arching throw to first to make sure it gets there
Spin toward second base and try to get the force out on the lead runner
explanations: This is the shortstop's money play! After ranging deep into the hole and backhanding the ball, you plant your back foot hard to stop your momentum, get your feet set, and fire across the diamond. It takes a strong arm and great footwork — you're throwing from far away, so you need everything behind it. This is the play that separates great shortstops from good ones.

The third baseman isn't positioned to receive your flip — he's not expecting it and may not even be near third base. By the time he catches your flip, adjusts, and throws, the runner is safe at first. You have to make this throw yourself.

A lob from deep in the hole will never beat the runner. You're already far from first, and a slow, arching throw gives the batter extra time. You need a hard, low throw — a line drive to the first baseman's glove. Lobs are for Sunday catch, not game situations.

Spinning toward second takes time and the throw to second is awkward from this angle. With two outs, you don't need the force at second — just get the batter at first. One out ends the inning. Keep it simple.
scenarios.concept: On a deep backhand in the hole, plant hard and fire across the diamond — your arm strength and footwork are what make this signature shortstop play possible.

scenarios.title: Relay on a Left-Center Gap Shot
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first, nobody out, and the batter rips a ball into the left-center field gap. It rolls all the way to the warning track. The runner from first is rounding second and heading for third — he might try to score. You need to get out as the relay man. How do you set up?
options: Sprint out and line up between the outfielder and home plate, hands raised high so the outfielder can see you — listen to your teammates for where to throw
Line up between the outfielder and third base since that's where the lead runner is heading
Stay at shortstop position and let the outfielder make the throw himself
Sprint all the way to the outfielder to shorten his throw as much as possible
explanations: Perfect relay setup! On balls to left-center, the shortstop is the relay man. Your default alignment is always toward home plate because preventing runs is the #1 priority. Put your hands up high so the outfielder has a clear target. Your teammates behind you will yell directions — if they say 'cut three!' you cut it and throw to third. If they say 'let it go!' the throw is on line to home. Trust your ears.

Lining up toward third can work if the runner clearly stops, but your default should always be home plate. If you line up to third and the runner keeps going, you've already lost. Start with home as your target — you can always redirect to third after you catch the ball.

The outfielder cannot make a 250-foot throw to home plate on the fly — that's nearly impossible. The relay man exists to break that long throw into two shorter, faster throws. Without you out there, the runner scores easily.

Going too far toward the outfielder defeats the purpose of a relay. You want to position yourself so you can catch the outfielder's throw and make a strong relay throw home. If you're too deep in the outfield, your throw home is still really long. Find the sweet spot — roughly in line with the edge of the outfield grass.
scenarios.concept: On relay plays to left-center, the shortstop lines up between the outfielder and home plate — preventing runs is always the default priority. Hands up, ears open.

scenarios.title: Steal Coverage With a Righty Batting
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runner on first with speed, and a right-handed batter is at the plate. The runner takes off for second on the pitch. The catcher fires the throw. On this play, who covers second base — you or the second baseman?
options: You cover second base — with a right-handed batter up, the ball is more likely hit to the left side, so the second baseman stays in his fielding position
The second baseman covers — he's closer to the bag
Both of you run to the bag so someone is definitely there
Neither of you — the pitcher covers second on a steal
explanations: Exactly right! With a right-handed batter, the ball is more likely to be pulled to the left side of the field. That means the second baseman needs to stay in his position to field a possible ground ball hit to the right side. So the shortstop covers second on the steal. It's all about protecting the side of the field where the ball is most likely NOT to go.

The second baseman might be physically closer, but with a right-handed batter up, he needs to stay in his fielding position. If he breaks toward second to cover the steal and the batter hits a ground ball to the right side, there's nobody there. The coverage depends on the batter, not the distance.

If both of you run to the bag, you leave the entire middle of the infield wide open. What if the batter swings and hits a ground ball? It rolls through for an easy hit because nobody is in position. Only one middle infielder covers — the other stays home.

The pitcher has to deliver the pitch and then be ready to field a batted ball. He can't cover second base on a steal — that's the middle infielders' job. The shortstop and second baseman divide this responsibility based on the handedness of the batter.
scenarios.concept: With a right-handed batter, the shortstop covers second on a steal because the second baseman needs to protect the right side where a righty is more likely to hit.

scenarios.title: Double Play Depth or Normal Depth?
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. It's a tie game in the 6th inning. Runner on first, nobody out. Your coach tells you to get into double play depth. What does that mean, and why does it matter?
options: Move a few steps toward second base and a step or two closer to home plate — shorter distance to second base means you can start the double play faster
Move several steps toward third to increase your range in the hole
Stay at your normal depth but move closer to second base only
Move way in on the grass, almost behind the pitcher's mound
explanations: That's double play depth! You move a couple of steps toward second base (shorter throw or quicker feed to start the DP) and a step or two in toward home (so the ball gets to you faster and you have more time to turn two). You sacrifice a little range deep in the hole, but the double play is worth it — two outs changes the whole inning.

Moving toward third gives you more range in the hole, but it makes the double play much harder. You're farther from second base, so the feed takes longer. With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is your priority — position for it.

Moving laterally toward second without coming in a step means the ball takes longer to reach you. Coming in slightly means you get the ball a fraction of a second sooner, and on a double play, every fraction counts. Both adjustments matter.

Moving way in on the grass is too aggressive. You'd be so close to home that hard ground balls get through before you can react. Double play depth is a subtle adjustment — a few steps, not a radical change. You still need to be able to field normal ground balls.
scenarios.concept: Double play depth means a few steps toward second base and a step in toward home — shorter distance to the bag and quicker reactions make turning two possible.

scenarios.title: Pop Fly in Shallow Left
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop. Runners on first and third, one out. The batter pops the ball up into shallow left field. You're going back on the ball and the left fielder is charging in. The third baseman has pulled off to cover his base. Who should catch this?
options: Let the left fielder take it — he's charging in and can see the ball in front of him the whole way
Call for it yourself — you're the shortstop and you're closer
Let the third baseman come over since it's on the left side
Nobody calls for it — just see who gets there first
explanations: Smart play! The left fielder charging in has the ball in front of him the entire way — that's a much easier catch than you going back over your shoulder. Outfielders coming in always have priority over infielders going back. Plus, with a runner on third, the left fielder's forward momentum gives him a better position to throw home if the runner tags up. Let the outfielder have it!

Going back on a fly ball is the hardest catch in baseball — the ball drifts away from you and you're running almost blind. The left fielder running forward can track the ball the whole way and make a much easier catch. Don't let pride get in the way of good defense.

The third baseman has to cover third base with a runner there. He can't leave the bag to chase a pop fly. This is between you and the left fielder, and the left fielder wins that priority battle every time.

Never leave a fly ball uncalled! When nobody communicates, players crash into each other or the ball drops. The left fielder should call for it loudly, and you should peel off. Communication prevents disasters.
scenarios.concept: Outfielders running in always have priority over infielders going back — the ball is easier to see and catch when it's in front of you, not over your shoulder.

scenarios.title: Throwing on the Run — Backhand Side
scenarios.description: You're the shortstop in a one-run game. Runner on second, two outs. The batter hits a ground ball to your backhand side — not deep in the hole, but far enough that you have to range to your right and field it on the move. You don't have time to plant and set your feet. How do you make this throw?
options: Field it on the run, skip the plant, and throw sidearm to first using your momentum — your body is already going that direction so let it work for you
Stop completely, set your feet, then make a perfect overhand throw to first
Field it and flip underhand to the second baseman to relay to first
Hold the ball — with two outs and a runner on second, wait for a better play
explanations: Elite play! When you're ranging to your backhand side and don't have time to plant, you throw on the run with a sidearm or three-quarter arm slot. Your momentum is already carrying you toward the first base side of the field, so you use that energy in your throw. It's not as strong as a planted throw, but it's FAST — and fast is what you need when the runner is flying down the line. This is a big-league skill.

Stopping to set your feet is ideal in most situations, but here you don't have time. The ball was hit hard enough to your right that if you stop, plant, and throw overhand, the batter beats it out. Sometimes you have to sacrifice a little accuracy for speed. Trust your arm and let it fly on the move.

Flipping to the second baseman adds an extra exchange — your flip, his catch, his throw. Two throws take longer than one. And the second baseman may not even be in position to receive a flip. Make the throw yourself and get the inning-ending out.

Holding the ball with two outs means the inning continues and the runner on second stays in scoring position. You need this out to end the inning and protect your one-run lead. Every out matters — make the throw!
scenarios.concept: When ranging to the backhand side without time to plant, throw sidearm on the run — use your momentum toward first base to power the throw instead of fighting it.

scenarios.title: Double Play Pivot — Inside or Outside?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, 1 out. Ground ball to second. You're covering 2nd for the pivot. The throw is coming to your left. Do you take it inside the bag or outside?
options: Take it inside the bag — step across
Take it outside the bag — drag the back foot
Camp on the bag and wait for the throw
Cut it off and flip to first directly
explanations: Correct. Inside pivot: you catch on the infield side, step across the bag, and release toward first in one fluid motion. You clear the runner's path and gain arm angle. MLB second-to-first DP conversion rate is 72% using the inside pivot.

The outside pivot (dragging the back foot on the outfield side) works on throws to your right, not your left. On a throw to your left, going outside puts your arm behind your body — you lose 15+ feet of carry.
Camping on the bag and waiting kills your momentum. The pivot is a catch-and-release movement — dwelling costs you the DP because the runner reaches first before your throw does.
Cutting it off only makes sense if the throw is badly offline and a DP is already dead. On a catchable throw, always complete the relay to first.
scenarios.concept: Double play pivot: inside vs. outside depends on throw direction

scenarios.title: Relay Positioning — Where Do You Line Up?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, nobody out. Deep single to right-center. Where do you set up as the relay man?
options: Shallow outfield grass, inline between CF and 3rd base
Deep in the outfield to save the outfielder a long throw
On the outfield side of 2nd base
At the pitching mound — split the distance
explanations: Correct. The SS relay man sets up on the shallow outfield grass, directly in line between the outfielder and the base being targeted. 'Shallow' means 30-40 feet into the outfield — not so deep you can't re-relay, not so shallow the outfielder's throw dies before reaching you. Inline alignment is the #1 priority: any angle creates carry loss.
Deep setups waste the relay's purpose. You're absorbing a long throw only to make another long throw. Proper depth is 30-40 feet past the infield — close enough to fire quickly to any base.
Setting up at 2nd base means you're too close — the outfielder's throw sails over you. The relay man must be out in the grass to take cut-down throws.
The mound is a common youth mistake. It's too shallow for deep singles — the outfielder's throw lands before it reaches you. Relay depth scales with the depth of the hit.
scenarios.concept: Relay positioning: shallow outfield grass, inline with target base

scenarios.title: Steal Coverage — Who Has Second?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st. Left-handed batter at the plate. Runner breaks for 2nd. Who covers the bag?
options: Second baseman covers — SS stays in position
Shortstop covers — 2B stays in position
Whoever gets there first
Both break for the bag and the first one there takes it
explanations: Correct. Against a left-handed batter, the ball tends to be pulled toward the right side. The second baseman must guard against the pull — so the shortstop covers 2nd on stolen base attempts. This is the universal steal coverage rule for LHB: SS covers 2nd.
When a left-handed batter is up, the shortstop does NOT cover 2nd. The shortstop needs to be in position for pull grounders to the left side. Second baseman takes the steal throw here.
'Whoever gets there first' creates communication breakdowns — the classic 'I've got it'/'you've got it' collision scenario. Stolen base coverage is pre-determined by batter handedness, every time.
Two players breaking for the bag creates a hole on both sides of the infield. Pre-set communication rules prevent this — one player covers, the other holds position.
scenarios.concept: Stolen base coverage: SS covers on LHB, 2B covers on RHB

scenarios.title: Communication on the Pop-Up
scenarios.description: Short popup between you and the third baseman. Both of you break for it. You're calling for it. What do you do?
options: Call loudly 'I got it! I got it!' and take the ball
Wave off the third baseman and back off
Let third base take it — he has better angle
Yell for center field to come take it
explanations: Correct. The shortstop has priority over third base on short pops in this zone. When you call loudly and early, third base must yield — verbal communication wins. Early, loud, repeated calls ('I got it! I got it!') eliminate collisions. SS is moving toward the ball, which improves catch probability vs. standing still.
Waving off and backing away contradicts taking responsibility. If you call it, take it. Backing off after calling creates hesitation from third base and increases drop probability.
Third base has a harder angle on balls drifting toward SS territory. The SS moving forward has better momentum and a cleaner look than 3B backpedaling.
Center field handles deep flies. A short pop between SS and 3B is never a CF ball — calling CF creates confusion and leaves the other zones uncovered.
scenarios.concept: Shortstop has priority over third base on shared popup zone

scenarios.title: The 5-4-3 Double Play — Your Throw
scenarios.description: Bases loaded, 1 out. Hard grounder to third. Third fires to you at second for the force. You catch it, step, and see the runner from first is trucking. Do you hurry the throw?
options: Take the extra half-second to set your feet — accuracy beats speed
Rush the throw — the runner is close
Eat the ball — just get the out at second
Lob it to avoid throwing into the runner
explanations: Correct. A half-second to set your feet is almost always worth it at the pro level. An offline throw to first in this spot scores two runs and puts runners on the corners. MLB data: rushed pivot throws are 3x more likely to be wild. Get the out at second, set your feet, and deliver a strike to first. Runner contact is part of the pivot — absorb it and throw.
Rushing the relay when you have time is the #1 cause of DP-killing wild throws. The 'hurry up and miss' play is a baserunner's best friend. Take your half-second.
Eating the ball gets one out when you might get two. With the force, you always complete the relay unless the throw to 2nd was truly off-line and you bobbled it.
Lobbing is never the answer on a double play relay. You lose velocity, the throw becomes hittable for the runner, and you almost certainly won't beat the batter to first.
scenarios.concept: On the DP pivot, accuracy beats speed — set your feet

scenarios.title: Pop Fly Priority
scenarios.description: You're center field. High pop between you, the right fielder, and the 2nd baseman.
options: Call 'I got it!' and wave off others
Let the infielder take it
Stay quiet and see who gets there
Back off for the right fielder
explanations: CF has priority on ALL fly balls they can reach. Call early and loud!
You're running in (easier). Outfielder coming in has priority over infielder going back.
NEVER stay quiet! Collisions happen when nobody communicates.
Center fielders have better angles — back off for the CF because he has the clearest view of the ball.
scenarios.concept: Center fielder has priority on all fly balls — call it early and loud!

scenarios.title: Do-or-Die Play
scenarios.description: Bottom 9th, tie game, runner on 2nd, 2 outs. Single to you in center. Runner going home.
options: Field cleanly, set feet, strong throw home
Charge and throw on the run
Field and hit cutoff
Dive for the ball
explanations: This is it. Accuracy beats speed. Set your feet and make your best throw.
Throwing on the run sacrifices accuracy in the biggest moment.
Cutoff adds time on a game-ending throw.
Diving forces you to get up slowly, losing time to throw. Clean field beats risky diving here.
scenarios.concept: In do-or-die plays: field cleanly, set your feet, throw accurately

scenarios.title: Fly Ball Caught — Throw Home or Cutoff?
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, runner on 3rd, 1 out, tied 3-3. Fly ball caught by you in center field — medium depth. The runner is tagging up.
options: Throw directly home — gun him down
Hit the cutoff man with a strong throw
Throw to second — the batter isn't going anywhere
Hold the ball — save your arm
explanations: On a tag-up play from center field at medium depth, you have a realistic chance to throw the runner out at home. Crow-hop, set your feet, and fire a strong throw home. This is one of the few times you throw through the cutoff.
The cutoff man is great on base hits, but on a tag play you need the ball home as fast as possible. A relay adds time.
Throwing to second ignores the run scoring. The runner on third is the priority.
Holding the ball lets the tying run score. Make a play! Think about what gives your team the best result here.
scenarios.concept: On tag-up plays, throw directly home — speed matters more than accuracy on this play

scenarios.title: Extra Base Hit to the Gap
scenarios.description: Top of the 7th, runner on 1st, 1 out, tied 4-4. Ball hit into the right-center gap. Runner is sprinting for home. You're the center fielder — you get to the ball first.
options: Throw home — try to get the runner at the plate
Hit the relay man between you and home
Throw to third to stop the batter
Hold the ball and run it in
explanations: Throwing home from the gap is 300+ feet — way too far for an accurate throw. It'll bounce multiple times and the batter will advance.

On extra-base hits to the gap, the relay throw is essential. Hit the relay man (usually the shortstop or second baseman) who is positioned in a straight line between you and home plate. He catches your throw and fires home in one motion. Two short throws beat one long throw.
Throwing to third ignores the scoring runner. Stop the run first.
Running the ball in is way too slow. The runner scores easily and the batter takes an extra base.
scenarios.concept: On balls in the gap, use the relay man — two short throws beat one long throw

scenarios.title: Two Runners Going — Which Throw?
scenarios.description: Bot 9th, runners on 1st and 2nd, 1 out, down 5-4. Single to center field. Runner from 2nd is scoring. Runner from 1st is trying for third. You're the center fielder.
options: Throw home — stop the tying run
Throw to third — get the batter trying to advance
Hit the cutoff and let him decide
Throw to second — hold the batter to a single
explanations: With a runner scoring the tying run in the 9th, preventing that run is everything. The runner from second to home is the throw you must make. If you get him, the game stays in your favor.
Getting the runner at third is nice, but the tying run scoring from second is far more important. Stop the lead runner first.
The cutoff is usually smart, but in a do-or-die situation where the tying run is scoring, you throw home. The cutoff can catch the throw if the runner stops.
Throwing to second is the lowest-priority play. The tying run is about to score!
scenarios.concept: When the tying or go-ahead run is scoring, throw home — stop the most important runner first

scenarios.title: Routine Fly — Catch It Right
scenarios.description: Bot 3rd, nobody on, 1 out, tied 1-1. Fly ball hit right to you in center field. It's a routine catch. How should you catch it?
options: Catch it above your head with both hands — the right way
Basket catch at your waist — it looks cool
One-hand snag — show off your skills
Catch it on the run — charge in
explanations: Catch fly balls above your head with two hands. Your glove hand catches the ball and your throwing hand covers it immediately. This is the safest, most reliable way to catch a fly ball. You can also transition to a throw quickly from this position.
Basket catches look cool but are much riskier. If you misjudge the ball, it hits you in the face. There's no margin for error at your waist.
One-handed catches increase the chance of the ball popping out of your glove. Use two hands.
The ball is coming to you — don't charge in on a routine fly. Get under it and make the catch.
scenarios.concept: Catch fly balls above your head with two hands — safe, reliable, and ready to throw

scenarios.title: Ball Between Two Outfielders — Who Gets It?
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th, nobody on, 2 outs. Fly ball hit to left-center — both the left fielder and center fielder are running for it. Who calls it?
options: Center fielder calls it — he has priority
Left fielder calls it — he called it first
Whoever is closer calls it
They should both try to catch it
explanations: The center fielder is the captain of the outfield. On any ball between two outfielders, the center fielder has priority. He calls 'I got it!' or 'Mine!' and the other fielder peels off. This prevents collisions and dropped balls. The CF has priority because he's running toward the ball and has a better angle.
The left fielder should defer to the center fielder even if he called it first.
Whoever is closer is a recipe for collisions. There must be a clear priority system.
Both going for the ball is how outfielders get hurt. One person catches it, the other backs up.
scenarios.concept: The center fielder has priority on fly balls between outfielders — he calls it and everyone else defers

scenarios.title: Ball Deep to the Warning Track
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, runner on 2nd, 1 out, tied 3-3. Deep fly ball hit toward the warning track in right-center. You're the center fielder — it's going over your head.
options: Sprint back, turn your back to the infield, and run to the spot
Drift back while keeping your eyes on the ball
Sprint to the wall and wait for it
Take an angle route — the banana route to the ball
explanations: Sprinting straight back while watching over your shoulder is the old-school method but it's inefficient.
Drifting back is too slow for a ball going over your head. You won't get there.
Sprinting to the wall and waiting doesn't work — the ball might land short of the wall.

Take an angle route (the 'banana route') — turn and run at an angle to where the ball will land, then curve back to catch it. This is faster than running straight back because you run in a more efficient path. You also end up facing the right direction to make a throw after the catch. Elite outfielders always use angle routes on deep fly balls.
scenarios.concept: On deep fly balls, use an angle route — turn and run to the spot, don't drift back or run straight

scenarios.title: Take Charge on the Pop Fly
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder. A high pop fly is hit to short left-center field. The left fielder is running over and the shortstop is drifting back. You have a clear path to the ball and can get there easily.
options: Call 'I got it!' loudly and wave off both players
Let the left fielder take it since it's closer to his side
Stay quiet and just run to the ball
Yell for the shortstop to catch it
explanations: As the center fielder you're the outfield captain. You have priority over corner outfielders and infielders on fly balls, and calling loudly prevents collisions.
The left fielder can catch it, but center fielders have priority on balls they can reach because you're coming in and can see the whole field better.
Running to the ball without calling it is dangerous. Two players could collide at full speed trying to make the same catch.
The shortstop is running with his back to the infield, making it a harder catch. Outfielders have priority over infielders on fly balls.
scenarios.concept: The center fielder is the outfield captain and has priority on any fly ball they can reach.

scenarios.title: Shade the Gap
scenarios.description: You're in center field. A left-handed pull hitter steps up with nobody on base and one out. Your coach taught you to watch the batter's stance. This batter has an open stance and likes to drive the ball to right-center.
options: Shade a few steps toward right-center field
Shade a few steps toward left-center field
Stay in straightaway center
Move way over to the right field line
explanations: A left-handed pull hitter with an open stance tends to drive the ball to right-center. Shading that direction puts you in the best spot to cut off a gap hit.
Left-center is the opposite of where this batter likes to hit. You'd be leaving the right-center gap wide open for a double.
Staying in straightaway center is okay, but you're giving up a step or two toward where this hitter is most likely to drive the ball.
Moving all the way to the right field line is way too extreme. You'd leave a huge gap in left-center and couldn't recover on an opposite-field hit.
scenarios.concept: Study the batter's stance and tendencies to shade your position toward where they're most likely to hit.

scenarios.title: Hit the Right Base
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder. A line drive single is hit to you on one hop. There's a runner on first who was running on the pitch and is already rounding second base heading for third. No other runners are on base with one out.
options: Throw to third base to try to get the lead runner
Throw to second base to hold the batter at first
Throw home just in case the runner tries to score
Throw to first base to keep the batter honest
explanations: The lead runner is the most dangerous baserunner. If you can gun him down at third, you get the second out and take away a scoring threat. That's the high-value play.
The batter already has a single and is unlikely to try for second on a ball hit right at you. Throwing to second base wastes time while the lead runner takes third easily.
The runner is only going to third, not home. Throwing home lets the batter move up to second and gives nobody a chance at an out.
Throwing to first makes no sense since the batter is already safe on the hit. You'd be ignoring the advancing lead runner completely.
scenarios.concept: Always target the lead runner — getting the out on the most advanced baserunner takes away the biggest scoring threat.

scenarios.title: Back Up the Play
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder. With a runner on second and one out, a ground ball is hit to the shortstop. The shortstop fields it cleanly and throws to first base for the out. You notice nobody is behind the left fielder's area.
options: Sprint toward left field to back up any overthrow or bobble at third
Stay in center field and watch the play
Run toward second base to back up the throw
Jog toward the infield to congratulate the shortstop
explanations: The runner on second may try to advance to third on the groundout. If the third baseman bobbles a throw or an overthrow gets past, you need to be backing up in left field to prevent the runner from scoring.
Standing still in center field means if anything goes wrong near third base, the ball rolls to the fence and the runner scores easily. Always be moving to back up.
The throw is going to first, not second. Running to second base doesn't help anyone on this play.
Never jog during a play! The ball is still live and runners are moving. Hustling to back up is how great center fielders prevent extra bases.
scenarios.concept: Great center fielders are always moving to back up throws, even on routine plays — that's how you prevent disaster.

scenarios.title: Go Back on the Deep Ball
scenarios.description: You're in center field playing at normal depth. The batter crushes a towering fly ball that's hit well over your head toward the warning track. You need to turn and run. The ball is drifting slightly to your right.
options: Turn your body to the right, sprint back at an angle, and look for the ball over your right shoulder
Backpedal straight back while keeping your eyes on the ball the whole time
Turn completely around and sprint straight back, then look up at the last second
Drift back slowly while watching the ball so you don't lose it
explanations: Turning to the side the ball is drifting and sprinting at an angle is the fastest way to cover ground while still tracking the ball over your shoulder. This is textbook deep-ball technique.
Backpedaling is the slowest way to move backward. On a ball hit well over your head, you'll never catch up by going backward — you need to turn and run.
Sprinting straight back without looking risks running to the wrong spot. You need to peek over your shoulder to track the ball's flight and adjust your path.
Drifting slowly won't get you to a ball that's over your head. You need full speed immediately or the ball lands behind you for extra bases.
scenarios.concept: On balls over your head, drop-step to the side the ball is drifting, sprint at an angle, and track it over your shoulder.

scenarios.title: Direct the Outfield
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder and the outfield captain. A pinch hitter comes up that nobody on your team has seen before. He's a right-handed batter in a tie game. Your left fielder is shading toward the line and your right fielder is playing straightaway. The scouting report on your lineup card says this hitter is a dead pull hitter.
options: Wave the right fielder to shade toward the right field line and have the left fielder move toward center
Tell both outfielders to stay where they are since you don't know the hitter
Wave the left fielder closer to the left field line to protect against a hit down the line
Move yourself toward right-center but don't direct the other outfielders
explanations: A dead pull right-handed hitter drives the ball to left field and left-center. Moving RF toward the line and LF toward center creates an outfield shift that puts all three fielders where the ball is most likely to go. Great captainship!
Doing nothing wastes the scouting report you were given. The center fielder's job is to use information and position the outfield before each batter.
Moving LF toward the left field line leaves the biggest gap in left-center, exactly where a right-handed pull hitter drives the ball most. That's the opposite of what you want.
Moving yourself is good, but part of being outfield captain is directing all three outfielders. The team defense is only as good as its positioning.
scenarios.concept: The center fielder uses scouting reports and batter tendencies to position the entire outfield before each pitch.

scenarios.title: Relay Throw From the Gap
scenarios.description: You're in center field. A line drive is hit into the left-center gap and rolls to the wall. The left fielder gets to the ball first. You see a runner from first base rounding third and heading home. The shortstop is running out to be the relay man.
options: Line up behind the shortstop and yell directions — 'Home! Home!' — so the relay throw goes to the plate
Run to the ball to help the left fielder field it
Stand at second base to take a throw from the left fielder
Run toward home plate to help the catcher
explanations: On relay plays, the center fielder lines up behind the relay man and yells where to throw. Your voice and positioning help the shortstop make an accurate relay throw home. This is your most important job on gap hits.
The left fielder already has the ball. Two outfielders at the wall means nobody is directing the relay, and the throw home will be late and inaccurate.
Standing at second base doesn't help when the play is at the plate. The batter's base doesn't matter right now — the run scoring does.
The catcher handles home plate. Your job is behind the relay man, giving directions and backing up in case the relay throw is off-line.
scenarios.concept: On relay plays, the center fielder lines up behind the cutoff man and calls out where to throw — you're the eyes of the relay.

scenarios.title: Shallow or Deep?
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder. It's the bottom of the last inning and your team leads by one run. There's a runner on third with one out. A fly ball to you would tie the game on a sacrifice fly. The batter is a contact hitter who rarely hits deep fly balls.
options: Play shallow to have a better chance of throwing the runner out at home on a sac fly
Play extra deep to make sure nothing gets over your head
Stay at normal depth since you don't want to change anything
Move to left-center because the runner is on third
explanations: Playing shallow gives you momentum going forward and a shorter throw home. With a contact hitter who doesn't hit deep, you can afford to come in. A strong throw might nail the runner at the plate and save the game.
Playing extra deep makes your throw home longer, almost guaranteeing the runner scores. Against a contact hitter who doesn't hit deep, there's no reason to play back.
Normal depth is okay, but with the game on the line and a runner on third, you should adjust. Good fielders change their positioning based on the situation.
Moving left or right doesn't help you throw the runner out at home. The issue is depth — how close or far from home plate you are.
scenarios.concept: In late-game situations with a runner on third, play shallow to give yourself a shorter throw home and a chance to save the run.

scenarios.title: Line Drive Read
scenarios.description: The batter swings hard and the ball jumps off the bat heading your way. It's coming fast and you need to decide instantly — is this a line drive that's going to sink or a fly ball that will carry?
options: Take a step back first, then adjust forward if needed
Charge in aggressively right away
Stay frozen and wait to see what happens
Turn and run straight back to the wall
explanations: Taking a drop step back first is the golden rule of outfield reads. It's much easier to come forward on a ball than to recover after charging in on a line drive that carries over your head. This protects against the worst outcome — a ball getting behind you for extra bases.
Charging in is tempting, but if this ball has any carry at all, it sails over your head and rolls to the wall. A single becomes a triple. Only charge when you're 100% sure it's a short fly.
Staying frozen wastes your most precious resource — reaction time. Every fraction of a second matters on a hard-hit ball. You need to move immediately, and your first step should be back.
Turning and sprinting to the wall only makes sense on an obvious deep fly. On a ball you're still reading, you'll lose sight of it completely and have no chance to adjust.
scenarios.concept: On a hard-hit ball, your first step should always be back — it's easier to come in than go back.

scenarios.title: Backing Up Second
scenarios.description: Runner on first takes off stealing. The catcher fires a strong throw to second base. You're in center field about 150 feet behind the bag. The shortstop is covering the base.
options: Sprint in behind second base in a direct line with the catcher's throw
Stay in your normal center field position and watch
Move toward second but angle toward the right field side
Drift slowly toward the bag, staying shallow
explanations: This is your job on every steal attempt. Sprint directly behind second base, lined up with the catcher's throw. If the ball gets past the shortstop or bounces away, you're right there to keep the runner from advancing to third. Great center fielders do this automatically.
Staying put is a missed responsibility. If the throw skips past the infielder, the runner jogs to third with nobody backing up. That turns a stolen base into a runner 90 feet from scoring.

Angling toward right field takes you off the line of the throw. A backup should always be directly behind the base in line with the throw — that way a missed ball comes right to you instead of past you at an angle.
Drifting slowly won't get you close enough. You need to sprint hard to get within 30-40 feet of the bag. A lazy backup is almost as bad as no backup at all.
scenarios.concept: Center fielders back up second base on every steal attempt and infield throw — sprint in directly behind the bag, in line with the throw.

scenarios.title: Ball Off the Wall
scenarios.description: Deep fly ball to center field — you turn and chase it but it's going to reach the wall. The ball hits the padded wall about 8 feet high and bounces back. Runner on first is sprinting hard around second. You need to play this carom cleanly.
options: Set up a few feet off the wall and field the carom with two hands, then hit your cutoff man
Run to the wall, grab the ball, and throw it as hard as you can to third base
Let the ball bounce to you wherever you end up, then look for a play
Go to the wall and try to catch it on the rebound before it hits the ground
explanations: Setting up off the wall is the smart play. Position yourself 5-8 feet from the wall so the ball bounces to you at a playable height. Field it with two hands, turn, and hit your cutoff man. This is the fastest, most reliable way to keep the runner from taking an extra base.

Reaching the wall first and trying to throw from there puts you in a terrible position. Your body is facing the wall, you have no momentum toward the infield, and a wild throw from deep center with no cutoff man means the runner might score.

Letting the ball come to you passively means it might bounce past you or die at the base of the wall. You need to be aggressive and set up in the right spot — not just react to wherever the ball ends up.

Trying to catch the carom before it hits the ground is flashy but risky. The ball bounces unpredictably off the wall. If you miss the snag, the ball rolls away and the runner scores easily. Play it safe with a controlled two-hand pickup.
scenarios.concept: On balls off the wall, set up a few feet away from the wall to play the carom cleanly — don't chase the ball to the wall.

scenarios.title: Gap Communication
scenarios.description: A fly ball is hit into the right-center gap. Both you and the right fielder are running hard toward the same ball. You're the center fielder and you can see you'll get there at about the same time. Nobody has called for it yet.
options: Call 'I got it! I got it!' loudly and wave off the right fielder
Stay quiet and let the right fielder take it since it's closer to right
Both go for it and whoever gets there first catches it
Slow down and point at the right fielder to take it
explanations: The center fielder is the captain of the outfield. On any ball you can reach, you have priority and should call for it loudly and clearly — at least twice. The right fielder is trained to peel off when they hear you call. This prevents collisions and dropped balls.

Staying quiet is the worst thing you can do. Silence causes collisions. Two outfielders running full speed into each other can cause serious injuries. Someone must communicate, and center field has the priority.

'Whoever gets there first' is how collisions happen. In youth baseball and the pros alike, this is exactly the kind of play that leads to injuries. There must be one clear call and one player backing off.
Slowing down and pointing gives up the play. Hand signals are hard to see when both players are sprinting and focused on the ball. Use your voice — it's louder and clearer than a gesture.
scenarios.concept: The center fielder is the captain of the outfield — call for every ball you can reach, loudly and more than once.

scenarios.title: Dive or Play the Hop
scenarios.description: Tie game, bottom of the 8th. Runner on second, one out. A sinking line drive is hit to your right in center field. You're sprinting and the ball is dropping fast. If you dive, you might catch it — or it might skip past you to the wall. If you play it on a hop, the runner scores but you keep the batter to a single.
options: Play it on a hop — keep the ball in front of you and limit the damage
Dive for the catch — a spectacular play ends the threat
Slide feet-first to knock the ball down
Pull up and let it bounce to you, then throw home
explanations: With one out in a tie game, the risk of diving and missing is too high. If the ball gets past you, both the runner on second AND the batter score — you go from a tie to down two. Playing it on a hop gives up one run but keeps the batter at first with two outs coming. Smart center fielders know when the risk isn't worth the reward.

The highlight-reel play feels right, but the math is brutal. If you miss, the ball rolls to the wall and two runs score instead of one. In a tie game with one out, you can't afford to gamble. Diving is the right call with two outs or a bigger lead — not here.

Feet-first slides in the outfield are dangerous and unpredictable. You can't control where the ball goes, and you're likely to kick it past you. This technique doesn't belong in an outfielder's toolbox.
Pulling up and letting it bounce is too passive — the ball may scoot past you or take a bad hop. You still need to charge and play it aggressively on the short hop, just without leaving your feet.
scenarios.concept: In a close game with less than two outs, don't dive unless you're sure — a ball past the center fielder is a disaster.

scenarios.title: Crow-Hop and Throw
scenarios.description: Fly ball hit to medium-depth center field. You camp under it and make the catch. Runner on third is tagging up to score. Runner on first is holding. Your arm is decent but the throw home is about 250 feet. The cutoff man is lined up at the mound area.
options: Crow-hop and throw a strong one-hopper to the cutoff man, letting him decide to cut or let it go
Throw directly home on the fly, skipping the cutoff man
Lob it to second base to keep the runner on first from advancing
Hold the ball since the runner will score easily anyway
explanations: The crow-hop gives your throw power and accuracy. Hitting the cutoff man lets him make the final decision — if the runner at third left early or stumbles, the cutoff lets the throw through. If the run is going to score, the cutoff can redirect to keep the runner on first from advancing. Two plays for the price of one.

A 250-foot throw on the fly from medium center is extremely difficult. If the throw sails or bounces wide without a cutoff, the runner on first moves up to second or third. You've given up an extra base trying to be a hero. Trust your cutoff man.

Throwing to second base completely ignores the runner scoring. Your primary job is to give your team a chance at the plate. Even if the runner is likely to score, you have to make the throw home through the cutoff — that's the play that matters.
Holding the ball gives away the play and lets the runner on first advance for free. Even if the run will likely score, your throw through the cutoff man keeps the other runner honest at first base.
scenarios.concept: Always hit your cutoff man with a crow-hop throw — it gives your team two chances to make a play instead of one.

scenarios.title: Wind Out — How Deep Do You Play?
scenarios.description: You're playing center field. Top 3rd, nobody on, tied 1-1. The wind is blowing straight out at about 15 mph — you've already seen two fly balls carry farther than expected in the first two innings. The cleanup hitter is up with a 1-0 count. Where do you set up?
options: Play 10-15 feet deeper than normal — the wind is carrying everything
Play normal depth — adjust on each ball as it comes
Play a few steps shallower — the wind will push the ball back toward you
Stay where you are but shade toward the pull side
explanations: With 15 mph wind blowing out, fly balls carry 10-15 feet farther than normal. You've already seen evidence of this in innings 1-2. Playing deeper gives you the cushion to run in on short flies, but prevents balls from sailing over your head. Against the cleanup hitter in a hitter's count, depth is critical.
Adjusting 'on each ball' means you're constantly reacting late. Set your depth BEFORE the pitch based on known conditions. Two innings of evidence tells you the wind is real.
Shallower is exactly backwards. Wind OUT means balls carry FARTHER, not closer. Playing shallow means routine fly balls become extra-base hits over your head.
Shading to the pull side is a reasonable secondary adjustment, but it doesn't address the primary issue — you need to be DEEPER. Wind direction trumps pull tendency when the wind is this strong.
scenarios.concept: When the wind blows out at 10+ mph, play 7-15 feet deeper than normal — fly balls carry farther and you need the extra cushion

scenarios.title: Reading the Swing — Bat Angle Predicts Direction
scenarios.description: You're in center field. The batter is a left-handed hitter with a slightly uppercut swing. The pitcher delivers a fastball up in the zone. You see the batter's bat angle as he starts his swing — the bat is tilting upward and his hands are rolling over early. Where is this ball most likely going?
options: Dead pull to right field — rolled hands on a high fastball = pull side
Straight to center — lefty hitters hit center on fastballs
Opposite field to left — the uppercut creates loft the other way
Foul ball — that bat angle means he's late on the pitch
explanations: Correct! When a left-handed hitter rolls his hands over early on a high fastball, the bat angle is closing. This produces a pulled ball to the right side. The uppercut swing combined with early hand roll creates topspin pull-side contact. As a center fielder, you should be shading a step toward right-center as soon as you read the hand roll. Elite outfielders read the swing in the first milliseconds of contact to get a jump.
Straight to center would require the bat to be square at contact. Rolled hands and an uppercut on a high pitch produce pull-side contact, not center.
Opposite field from a lefty rolling his hands over is extremely unlikely. Opposite-field hits come from staying inside the ball with hands back — the opposite of what you're seeing.
The bat angle you described is consistent with contact, not a foul ball. Early hand roll on a high fastball produces a hard pull-side hit, likely in the air.
scenarios.concept: Read the bat angle at contact — rolled hands and an uppercut on high fastballs produce pull-side fly balls. Shade early for a jump.

scenarios.title: Catchable or Uncatchable — The Split-Second Decision
scenarios.description: You're in center field. A deep fly ball is hit to right-center gap. You're sprinting at full speed and the ball is drifting farther away. You have about 2 seconds to decide: you MIGHT catch it at full extension with a dive, or you can pull up and play the carom off the wall. Runner on first, 1 out, close game. What's your decision framework?
options: Always dive — making the catch ends the threat
Pull up and play the carom — a missed dive means the ball rolls to the wall and both runners score
Dive only if you're 80%+ sure you can catch it — below that, play the carom
Sprint to the wall and try to play it off the bounce, don't dive at all
explanations: 'Always dive' ignores risk. A missed dive with a runner on first and 1 out turns a double into an inside-the-park home run. The ball rolls past you, both runners score, and the batter ends up at third. The risk is catastrophic.
'Never dive' is too conservative. If you're 90% sure you can catch it, the expected value of the dive is much higher than conceding a double. You need a threshold, not a blanket rule.

Correct! The 80% rule: if you're 80% or more confident you can make the catch, dive. Below that, pull up and play the carom. Here's why — with a runner on first and 1 out, a catch ends the at-bat and keeps the runner at first (2 outs now). A missed dive lets both runners score and the batter reaches third. The downside of missing is 3x worse than the upside of catching. So you need high confidence before committing. Game situation matters: if it's 2 outs, lower your threshold because a catch ends the inning.
