Sprinting to the wall without attempting the catch concedes extra bases. If the ball is catchable at full extension, you should at least evaluate the catch probability before giving up on it.
scenarios.concept: Use the 80% rule on dive decisions: dive if you're 80%+ confident, play the carom below that. The threshold changes with outs and game situation.

scenarios.title: Getting Behind the Ball — Never Let It Go Over Your Head
scenarios.description: You're in center field. A fly ball is hit right at you — medium depth, not deep. It's a routine catch. But you notice you're drifting forward toward the ball as it rises. By the time it starts coming down, you realize it's going farther than you thought. What's the fundamental mistake?
options: You should have charged hard to catch it earlier
You should have taken your first step BACK, then come forward if needed
You should have stood still and waited to read the ball's full arc
You should have called for help from the corner outfielders
explanations: Charging hard is the opposite of what you need. You already misjudged the ball as shorter than it was. Charging forward when the ball is deeper makes the problem worse.

Correct! The golden rule of fly ball reads: your first step should always be BACK. Take a drop step backward, read the ball's trajectory, then come forward if it's short. It's much easier to run forward to a ball in front of you than to turn and sprint backward for a ball over your head. A ball over your head is a triple. A ball you run in on is a routine catch. Always give yourself the cushion first, then adjust forward.
Standing still wastes your reaction time. By the time you read the full arc, you've lost 1-2 seconds of running time. The drop step back gives you a head start while you read.
Calling for help on a ball hit right at you means you've already made a positioning error. The fix is your first step, not communication.
scenarios.concept: First step back on every fly ball — it's easier to run forward than to turn and chase a ball over your head

scenarios.title: Directing the Outfield — You're the Captain
scenarios.description: You're the center fielder. Between innings, you notice the left fielder is playing too shallow for the power hitter coming up, and the right fielder is shading too far toward the line against a hitter who goes opposite field. What do you do?
options: Mind your own business — each outfielder positions himself
Wave the left fielder back and the right fielder toward the gap — you're the captain
Yell instructions but stay in your own position
Tell the coach between innings and let him fix it
explanations: 'Mind your own business' ignores the center fielder's primary leadership role. CF is the captain of the outfield because you can see both corners and the whole field. Staying silent when you see a positioning error costs your team.

Correct! The center fielder is the outfield captain. You can see both corners, you know the hitter's tendencies, and you're responsible for the whole outfield alignment. Wave the LF back to prevent extra-base hits over his head, and wave the RF toward the gap to cover the opposite-field hitter's likely spray direction. Do this BEFORE the pitch. Good communication prevents runs before they happen.
Yelling without moving them into position is half the job. Use hand signals and direct them to specific spots — don't just shout general instructions.

Waiting for the coach wastes an at-bat of poor positioning. The center fielder has the authority and the view to adjust positioning in real time. The coach may not see the same angles you do from center field.
scenarios.concept: Center fielder is the outfield captain — direct both corner outfielders into position before every pitch based on the hitter and situation

scenarios.title: Hit the Cutoff
scenarios.description: You're in left. Single to you, runners on 1st and 2nd. Lead runner rounding 3rd.
options: Throw home directly
Throw to 3rd
Throw to 2nd
Hit the cutoff man
explanations: A direct throw from left is long. If offline, everyone advances.
3rd isn't the priority — the run matters.
Throwing to second puts focus on the trailing runner, not stopping the runner scoring from third.
The cutoff man can redirect to home or hold to prevent extras.
scenarios.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — he makes the best decision for the team

scenarios.title: Ball in the Sun
scenarios.description: High fly ball headed right into the sun. You lose sight of it.
options: Glove up to shade your eyes
Keep running where you think it's going
Stop and call for help
Drop to one knee
explanations: The glove-up technique is standard sun defense. Practice it every spring.
Running blind is how you misplay balls badly.
By the time help arrives, the ball has landed.
Dropping to one knee blocks even less sun — hold your glove high to actually block the glare.
scenarios.concept: Use your glove to shade the sun — fundamental outfield technique

scenarios.title: Hit the Cutoff Man
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th, runner on 2nd, 1 out. Single to left field — the runner is rounding third heading home. You're the left fielder. The third baseman is your cutoff man.
options: Throw directly home — skip the cutoff
Hit the cutoff man with a strong, low throw
Hold the ball — the runner will score anyway
Throw to third to get the batter
explanations: Throwing over the cutoff man often results in a wild throw that bounces past the catcher. The runner scores AND the batter advances. Always hit the cutoff!

Hitting the cutoff man is fundamental outfield play. A strong throw to the cutoff gives your team options — the cutoff can relay home, cut it off if the runner stops, or redirect to another base. It's the smart play every time.
Giving up before making a play is never the right answer. Make a throw and let the cutoff man decide.
The batter is your secondary concern. Stop the run from scoring first, then worry about the batter.
scenarios.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — it gives your team the most options and prevents wild throws

scenarios.title: Runner Trying to Score from First on a Double
scenarios.description: Bot 5th, runner on 1st, nobody out, down 3-2. Ball bounces off the left field wall — it's a double. Runner from first is trying to score all the way from first base.
options: Throw home — he started from first, it's a long run
Hit the relay man lined up between you and home
Throw to third — stop the batter from getting to third
Hold the ball — the runner will score anyway
explanations: A throw from the left field wall to home is 350+ feet. Even if you have a cannon arm, that throw bounces 3 times and arrives too late.

The relay man is your best friend on balls off the wall. He should be positioned between you and home. Fire a strong, chest-high throw to him. He catches and fires home. This is how runners get thrown out from the outfield — through relay throws, not one long heave.
The run scoring is more important than holding the batter at second. Stop the run first.
Never give up on a play. Even if the runner probably scores, a strong relay throw can save a run.
scenarios.concept: On doubles off the wall, the relay throw is essential — no arm is strong enough to throw 350 feet accurately

scenarios.title: Ball Off the Wall — Play It Quick
scenarios.description: Top of the 8th, nobody on, 1 out, up 5-4. Ball slices toward the left field corner and hits the wall. You're the left fielder — the ball caroms off at an angle.
options: Chase the ball and play the carom off the wall
Wait for the ball to settle on the ground
Sprint to the ball but be cautious of the wall
Let the center fielder back you up and play it
explanations: Play the carom! When a ball hits the wall, read the angle it bounces and position yourself to field it quickly. Getting the ball back to the infield fast is the difference between holding the batter to a double or letting him reach third.
Waiting for the ball to settle lets the runner take an extra base. Every second counts.
Being cautious of the wall is important for safety, but you should know where the wall is before the ball is hit. Attack the carom.
Waiting for backup wastes time. You're the closest player — play it yourself and get the ball back in.
scenarios.concept: Play the carom off the wall quickly — reading the angle and fielding fast prevents extra bases

scenarios.title: Line Drive Sinking — Dive or Play It Safe?
scenarios.description: Top of the 7th, runner on 1st, 2 outs, up 4-3. Line drive sinking in front of you in left field. If you dive and catch it, the inning is over. If you miss, the ball rolls past you.
options: Dive for it — catch it and end the inning
Play it on a hop — keep the damage to a single
Slide feet-first to catch it low
Let it bounce and field it quickly
explanations: With 2 outs and a lead, dive for it! If you catch it, the inning is over. If you miss, the worst case is the ball gets past you and the tying run scores. But with 2 outs, you want to end the inning NOW. The risk of diving is worth the reward of ending the threat.
Playing it on a hop concedes a single and keeps the inning alive with the tying run at the plate.
Sliding feet-first in the outfield is not a standard technique for catching a sinking liner.
Letting it bounce guarantees a base hit and extends the inning. End it here.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs and a lead, dive for sinking liners — ending the inning is worth the risk

scenarios.title: Wind Blowing Out — Play Deeper
scenarios.description: Bot 5th, nobody on, tied 2-2. The wind is blowing hard toward the outfield today — straight out. A routine fly ball in the 2nd inning carried 20 feet farther than expected.
options: Play deeper than normal — give yourself more room
Play normal depth — adjust on each ball
Play shallow — the wind won't affect line drives
It doesn't matter — just react to each ball
explanations: When the wind is blowing out, play 10-15 feet deeper than normal. Fly balls will carry farther, and balls that would normally be warning track fly outs become home runs or extra-base hits if you're playing too shallow. Depth is your friend with the wind at your back.
Adjusting on each ball means you're constantly behind. Set your depth before the pitch.
Playing shallow is the OPPOSITE of what you should do. Wind blowing out means balls carry — give yourself room.
It absolutely matters. A ball you would normally catch at the warning track now sails over the fence if you're too shallow.
scenarios.concept: When the wind blows out, play deeper — fly balls carry farther and you need the extra room

scenarios.title: Hit the Cutoff
scenarios.description: You're the left fielder. Bottom of the 4th, runner on second, one out. The batter singles to your left. You field it cleanly about 20 feet from the foul line. The runner is rounding third and heading home. Your third baseman is lined up as the cutoff man.
options: Throw a strong one-hopper to the cutoff man's chest
Throw it directly to home plate, skipping the cutoff
Throw it to third base to keep the batter from advancing
Hold the ball and jog it in
explanations: Hitting the cutoff man gives your team options — he can let it go through to the plate or redirect it. A one-hopper to his chest is a throw he can handle cleanly.
Trying to skip the cutoff from deep left field often leads to wild throws that miss everyone. Even strong arms should use the relay to keep throws accurate.
Throwing to third ignores the runner scoring. The priority is always the lead runner, and right now he's heading home.
Holding the ball lets the runner score easily and the batter take extra bases. You must get the ball in quickly.
scenarios.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — it keeps your throw accurate and gives your team options on where to send the ball.

scenarios.title: Ball Off the Wall
scenarios.description: You're in left field. Top of the 6th, runner on first, no outs. The batter drives a ball to the left field corner. It hits the wall and caroms back at a sharp angle toward center field. You're running toward the corner to play the bounce.
options: Round the ball so your momentum carries toward the infield as you field it
Run straight to the ball, pick it up, then spin and throw
Let the ball come to you off the wall and wait for it to settle
Call for the center fielder to get it since it bounced toward him
explanations: Rounding the ball lets you field it with your momentum already going toward the infield. You save a full second compared to fielding and spinning, which can be the difference between the runner being safe or out.
Running straight at the ball means you have to stop, field, spin 180 degrees, then throw. That wasted motion gives runners extra bases.
Waiting for the ball to settle is way too passive. Every second you wait, runners are advancing. Attack the ball off the wall.
The ball is in your territory — calling off to the center fielder creates confusion and delays. You're closer and it's your wall to play.
scenarios.concept: Round the ball off the wall so your momentum is already heading toward the infield — never field and spin.

scenarios.title: Shallow Fly Communication
scenarios.description: You're the left fielder. Bottom of the 3rd, nobody on, two outs. The batter hits a soft fly ball that's dropping into short left field. You're running in hard, and you can see the shortstop backpedaling toward the same spot. You're going to get there at the same moment.
options: Call 'I got it!' loudly and early so the shortstop peels off
Stay quiet and let the shortstop take it since he's an infielder
Both go for it and whoever gets there first catches it
Pull up and let the shortstop have it — infielders have priority on shallow flies
explanations: As the outfielder coming in, you have the ball in front of you the whole way. You have the better angle and can see it clearly. Call it loud and early so the shortstop knows to peel off.

Wrong — outfielders have priority over infielders on balls in front of them. You're running IN with the ball in front of you the whole way. The shortstop is running BACK and can't see the ball as well. Priority goes to the player running toward the ball. Center field has priority because they're always running IN — corner outfielders back off when CF calls it. Call it loud, call it early.
Both going for it without talking is how players get hurt. Collisions between fielders are one of the most dangerous plays in baseball.
Actually, on balls in between, the outfielder has priority because they're running in and can see the ball better. The shortstop is running with his back to the infield, making it a harder catch.
scenarios.concept: Outfielders have priority on shallow flies because they're running forward with the ball in front of them — call it loud and early.

scenarios.title: Back Up Third
scenarios.description: You're in left field. Top of the 5th, runner on first, no outs. The batter hits a ground ball to second base. The second baseman fields it and throws to the shortstop at second for the force out. The shortstop now throws to first for the double play. Where should you be?
options: Backing up third base in case of an overthrow from the outfield later
Stay in your normal left field position — the play is on the infield
Move toward the infield to back up any throw to third base
Back up second base in case the throw from the shortstop is wild
explanations: You're thinking ahead, but the immediate play might involve a throw to third if the double play doesn't turn. Moving toward third to back up is the smart positioning right now.
Standing in your normal spot means you're too far away to help if a throw gets away. Left fielders should always be moving toward a backup position on ground balls.
Correct! On ground balls with a runner on first, the left fielder backs up third base. If the throw from second goes wild, or if a runner advances to third, you're there to prevent extra bases.
Second base is the center fielder's backup responsibility, not yours. As the left fielder, third base is your backup assignment.
scenarios.concept: On ground balls, the left fielder always moves to back up third base — every throw needs a backup behind it.

scenarios.title: Two Runners Moving
scenarios.description: You're the left fielder. Bottom of the 7th, runners on first and second, one out, tie game. The batter singles hard into left field. The runner from second is scoring easily. The runner from first is rounding second and heading to third. The batter is taking a wide turn at first.
options: Throw to third base to try to get the runner coming from first
Throw home to try to get the runner scoring from second
Hit the cutoff man and let him decide where to throw
Throw to second base to get the batter trying to stretch it
explanations: The runner from first has a huge head start to third. By the time you field and throw, he's likely safe. You might also throw it away and let the batter advance.
The runner from second is scoring easily — there's no play at home. Throwing there wastes your time and lets other runners advance.
With multiple runners moving, hitting the cutoff man is the smartest play. He can see all the runners and redirect the throw to wherever the best play is. This prevents wild throws.
The batter is just rounding first. Throwing behind him to second is a low-value play when you have a runner going to third. Get the ball to the cutoff.
scenarios.concept: With multiple runners moving, always hit the cutoff man — he can see the whole field and redirect to the best play.

scenarios.title: Reading Off the Bat
scenarios.description: You're in left field. Top of the 1st, nobody on, no outs. A right-handed batter swings and you hear a loud crack. The ball comes off the bat high in the air, hooking toward you. Your first step will determine if you catch it or it drops.
options: Take a drop step back and to your right, then read the ball in the air
Take a step forward to charge the ball
Stand still and watch the trajectory for a moment before moving
Break hard toward center field since right-handed hitters usually pull
explanations: A drop step back opens your hips and lets you cover ground quickly. A high fly ball hooking toward left from a right-hander usually carries more than you think. Getting back first is safer — you can always come in.
Charging forward on a high fly ball is dangerous. If it carries over your head, you can't recover. It's much easier to come in on a ball than to go back.
Standing still wastes your best reaction time. Your first step in the first half-second determines whether you reach the ball. Read and react immediately.
Breaking toward center ignores where the ball actually is — it's hooking toward you in left, not going to center. React to the ball, not a guess about the hitter.
scenarios.concept: On fly balls, your first step determines everything — a drop step back gives you time to read the carry and adjust.

scenarios.title: Sun in Your Eyes
scenarios.description: You're the left fielder. Bottom of the 2nd in an afternoon game. The sun is low and directly in your eyes when you look toward home plate. A high fly ball is hit your way and you immediately lose it in the sun. You know it's somewhere above you.
options: Use your glove to shade the sun while tracking the ball
Turn your head sideways and try to pick up the ball's shadow on the grass
Put your sunglasses down, shift your head to find the ball out of the sun's glare, and get under it
Close your eyes and put your glove up where you think it will land
explanations: Using your glove to shade works and is a solid backup technique. Every outfielder should know the glove-shield method — raise your glove to block the sun while tracking the ball. If you don't have sunglasses, this is your go-to move.
Looking for the ball's shadow is creative but unreliable — shadows shift depending on the sun angle and the grass, and it doesn't help you judge the ball's height.

Dropping your sunglasses, shifting your angle to find the ball outside the sun's glare, and repositioning is the best technique when you have sunglasses. But the glove-shield is a strong backup — use both together if needed.
Closing your eyes and guessing is never a good idea. The ball could hit you in the face. You need to find a way to track it.
scenarios.concept: In the sun, use sunglasses AND the glove-shield technique — sunglasses are primary, glove-up is your always-available backup.

scenarios.title: Dive or Play Safe
scenarios.description: You're in left field. Top of the 8th, your team leads 6-3. Runner on first, two outs. The batter hits a sinking line drive to your left. You can probably catch it with a full dive, but if you miss, the ball rolls to the wall and at least one run scores — maybe two.
options: Play it safe on a hop — keep the ball in front of you and hold the runner to second
Dive for the catch — with two outs, the inning is over if you catch it
Sprint and try a sliding catch to stay in control
Dive but angle your body so if you miss, the ball stays in front of you
explanations: With a 3-run lead in the 8th and two outs, playing it safe is the percentage play. If the ball gets past you, you turn a 3-run lead into a 1-run game. Keeping it in front means runners on first and second with two outs — your pitcher can get the next guy.
A diving catch ends the inning, but if you miss, the ball rolls to the wall and suddenly it's a one-run game. The risk isn't worth the reward when you're protecting a lead.
A sliding catch sounds controlled, but on a sinking liner to your side, a slide is hard to execute. If you misjudge it, you're on the ground and the ball is rolling away.
Angling your dive is good in theory, but it's very hard to control where your body goes mid-dive. If you're going to play it safe, commit to playing it safe.
scenarios.concept: With a comfortable lead and two outs, play it safe — keeping the ball in front of you protects your lead better than a risky dive.

scenarios.title: Charging a Ground Single
scenarios.description: Ground ball single slices through the left side of the infield. Runner on first is rounding second and heading for third. The ball is rolling on the grass toward you in left field. You've got a chance to throw the runner out if you charge hard and field it cleanly.
options: Charge hard, field the ball outside your glove foot, and throw to third in one motion
Charge, pick it up, set your feet, and make a strong throw to third
Jog in, field it safely, and hold the runner at third with the threat of a throw
Run hard to the ball, barehand it, and fire to third
explanations: Charging hard and fielding outside your glove-side foot puts your body in perfect throwing position. You can field and throw in one fluid motion without wasting time setting your feet. This is the outfielder's version of a shortstop's charging play — momentum toward the target creates a fast, accurate throw.

Stopping to set your feet costs you a full second, and that's the difference between out and safe. By the time you plant and throw, the runner has already slid into third. The charge-and-throw in one motion is what separates great outfielders from average ones.

Jogging in concedes the base. If you don't challenge the runner, they take third every time. With one out, a runner on third scores on a fly ball, a wild pitch, or a passed ball. You have to at least try to get them.

Barehanding a ground ball in the outfield is extremely risky. If you bobble it, the ball rolls behind you and the runner might score. Use your glove — that's what it's there for. Barehand plays are for infielders on slow rollers, not outfielders.
scenarios.concept: Charge ground balls aggressively and field outside your glove foot — this lets you throw in one smooth motion without stopping.

scenarios.title: Backing Up Third Base
scenarios.description: Runner on second base tries to steal third. The catcher jumps up and fires a throw to the third baseman. You're the left fielder standing in your normal position. The throw looks a little high and might get past the third baseman.
options: Sprint toward the area behind third base in line with the catcher's throw
Stay in your position — that's the third baseman's play
Run toward the foul line to back up any ball that goes into the stands
Move toward shortstop position to help relay
explanations: This is left field's responsibility on every throw to third. Sprint to a spot about 30 feet behind third base, directly in line with the catcher's throw. If the ball gets past the third baseman, you're right there to grab it and keep the runner from scoring. This is a fundamental play that youth coaches love to see.

Staying put is a mistake. If that high throw gets past the third baseman and nobody is backing up, the runner scores on what should have been a simple steal attempt. Left fielders who don't back up third base cost their team runs.

Running to the foul line takes you out of the line of the throw. A backup should always be directly behind the base in line with the throw. The ball won't bounce sideways toward the stands — it'll skip straight back.
Moving to shortstop position doesn't help on this play at all. The throw is going from the catcher to third base, and you need to be behind third base, not beside it.
scenarios.concept: The left fielder always backs up third base on throws from the catcher — sprint behind the bag, in line with the throw.

scenarios.title: Lost in the Sun
scenarios.description: Afternoon game and the sun is brutal in left field. A high fly ball is hit your way and as you look up, you lose the ball completely in the blinding sun. You had a read on it for a second — it's coming down somewhere near you. The crowd is yelling.
options: Use your glove to shade the sun, shift your angle slightly, and try to pick the ball back up
Keep staring straight up into the sun — you'll find it eventually
Turn away from the sun and listen for your teammates to guide you to the ball
Drop to one knee and let the ball bounce in front of you to play it safe
explanations: Using your glove as a sun shield is the textbook move. Hold it up above your eyes to block the sun while shifting your angle a step or two to either side. This often lets you reacquire the ball against a different part of the sky. Sunglasses help, but the glove shade is every outfielder's best friend on sun-field plays.

Staring directly into the sun is dangerous for your eyes and won't help you find the ball. The longer you stare, the worse the spots in your vision become. You need to shade your eyes and change your viewing angle.

Turning away from the ball completely means you have zero chance of catching it. Your teammates can yell, but they can't guide you precisely enough in the 2-3 seconds a fly ball is in the air. You need to keep your eyes in the general area of the ball.

Dropping to a knee is giving up on the play. The ball could land anywhere, and if it bounces over you, runners advance freely. You should always try to track the ball — shade your eyes and fight to find it.
scenarios.concept: When you lose a ball in the sun, use your glove to shade your eyes and shift your angle — never stare directly into the sun.

scenarios.title: Shallow Fly Communication
scenarios.description: A soft, looping fly ball is hit into shallow left field. You're running in hard. The shortstop is running out with their back to the infield. You can see the ball clearly the whole way. The shortstop is waving their glove but hasn't called for it.
options: Call 'I got it!' loudly — you have the better angle coming in on the ball
Stay quiet and let the shortstop take it since they're closer to the infield
Both go for it and figure it out at the last second
Peel off since infielders always have priority
explanations: You have the huge advantage here — you're running in toward the infield and can see the ball the entire way. The shortstop is running with their back to the play, which makes catching much harder. An outfielder coming in always has a better angle than an infielder going out. Call for it loudly and take charge.

Staying quiet causes confusion and collisions. On a ball like this, someone must call for it. Since you have the better angle — running in with your eyes on the ball — it should be you. Silence is the enemy.

Going for it without communicating is how players get hurt. Two players colliding at full speed on a shallow fly is one of the most common and dangerous plays in baseball. Call for it or get out of the way.

While infielders generally have priority on balls they can reach, that rule doesn't apply when the outfielder has a clearly better angle. Running in is always easier than running out. An outfielder coming forward should take this ball every time and call off the infielder.
scenarios.concept: An outfielder running in has a better angle than an infielder running out — call for the ball and take charge.

scenarios.title: Reading the Wall Carom
scenarios.description: Scoreless game, runner on first, one out. A screaming line drive hooks toward the left field corner and hits the angled wall where the foul line meets the outfield fence. The ball ricochets hard and shoots back toward center field. You chased it to the corner and now the carom is bouncing away from you.
options: Let the center fielder grab the carom — redirect and become the relay man between CF and the infield
Chase the ball down yourself even though it's moving away from you
Run to the wall where the ball hit and wait for it to come back
Yell for the third baseman to come get it
explanations: This is elite-level awareness. When the carom shoots away from you toward center, the center fielder is in a better position to field it. Your job now changes — become the relay man. Position yourself between the center fielder and the infield so the throw gets home as fast as possible. This team play can save a run.

Chasing a ball that's bouncing away from you toward center field is a losing race. You're moving in the wrong direction relative to the infield, and every second you chase, the runners advance. Let the center fielder take the shorter path to the ball.

Going back to the wall where the ball originally hit makes no sense — the ball isn't there anymore. It's already bouncing toward center. You need to react to where the ball IS, not where it WAS. In a scoreless game, every second matters.

The third baseman should be covering third base for a possible play on the lead runner. Asking them to leave their position to chase a ball in the outfield leaves third base empty and gives the runners a free base.
scenarios.concept: When a carom takes the ball away from you, let a teammate field it and switch to being the relay man — smart positioning beats foot speed.

scenarios.title: Sac Fly Positioning
scenarios.description: Runner on third, less than two outs. The batter is a right-handed pull hitter who likes to lift the ball. Your coach signals for you to adjust your positioning. A sacrifice fly will score the run, but if the ball drops for a hit, it's even worse.
options: Move in a few steps from normal depth — catch the fly and give yourself a shorter throw home
Play extra deep to make sure nothing gets over your head
Move to your normal depth but shade toward the line since he's a pull hitter
Charge in very shallow, almost to the dirt, to have the shortest possible throw
explanations: Moving in a few steps shortens your throw home by 20-30 feet, which could be the difference between the runner being safe or out. You're still deep enough to catch most fly balls, but now your throw has a real chance. This is the smart compromise — you give yourself a chance at both the catch AND the throw.

Playing extra deep guarantees the runner scores easily on any fly ball. Sure, nothing gets over your head, but the run scores on any catchable fly ball because your throw is too long. In a one-run game, you need to give yourself a chance to throw the runner out.

Shading toward the line is a reasonable idea, but depth matters more than angle here. The main issue isn't where the ball is hit laterally — it's how long your throw to the plate will be. Moving in a few steps helps more than moving sideways.

Playing extremely shallow is too aggressive. A ball hit over your head in this situation scores the runner AND gives the batter extra bases. You'd turn a sacrifice fly into a run-scoring double or triple. Move in a few steps, not halfway to the infield.
scenarios.concept: With a runner on third and fewer than two outs, move in a few steps — shorten your throw home without giving up catchable fly balls.

scenarios.title: Reading the Bat Angle — Pull or Opposite
scenarios.description: You're in left field. A right-handed power hitter is up with a 2-0 count. He's a dead-pull hitter — 70% of his balls in play go to left field. But you notice he's opened his stance slightly and moved his hands lower. Where do you position?
options: Shade toward the line — he's a pull hitter, play the tendency
Stay in your normal gap position — one stance change doesn't override 70% pull rate
Move a step toward center — the open stance and low hands suggest opposite field
Charge in shallow — 2-0 count means he's swinging hard, expect a line drive
explanations: Correct! A 70% pull rate is a strong tendency. One slight stance adjustment doesn't override a season of data. The 2-0 count is a hitter's count — pull hitters are MOST likely to pull on counts where they can sit on a fastball. Shade toward the line and play his tendency.
Normal gap position ignores a 70% pull rate. That's a massive tendency — you should be shading to take advantage of it.
One stance change can suggest an opposite-field approach, but 70% pull rate means his muscle memory will win. Moving toward center leaves the line wide open.
Charging in shallow on a power hitter in a hitter's count is dangerous. A 2-0 fastball from a pull hitter can be a home run or a deep fly — you need depth, not shallow positioning.
scenarios.concept: Trust pull-rate data over single at-bat stance changes — a 70% pull hitter pulls even more on hitter's counts

scenarios.title: Hard Carom Off the Wall — Line Drive
scenarios.description: You're in left field. A screaming line drive hits the wall 8 feet up on the fly and caroms straight back toward you at chest height. The batter-runner is sprinting for second. A runner on first is rounding second heading for third. How do you handle the carom?
options: Catch the carom on the fly and throw directly to third to get the lead runner
Field the carom off the ground after it bounces, then hit the cutoff man
Set up off the wall — let the ball come to you, field it cleanly, and hit the cutoff at shortstop
Turn and chase the ball to the wall, then relay to the infield
explanations: Trying to catch a hard carom on the fly is risky — line drive caroms are unpredictable and can change direction off the wall padding or seams. If you miss it, both runners advance.
Waiting for the ball to bounce off the ground after the carom adds time. A hard line drive off the wall comes back fast — you should be set up to field it in the air or on a short hop.

Correct! On hard caroms, set up 10-15 feet off the wall so the ball comes to YOU rather than you running to the wall. A line drive that hits 8 feet up will come back hard and fast — you need space to read the angle. Field it cleanly, then hit the cutoff man at shortstop who can redirect to third or home.
The ball is coming BACK toward you, not away from you. Turning to chase it to the wall means you're running away from a ball that's already coming your way.
scenarios.concept: On hard wall caroms, set up 10-15 feet off the wall — let the ball come to you, field it cleanly, and use the cutoff system

scenarios.title: Do-or-Die — Runner Tagging from Third
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 9th, runner on 3rd, 1 out, tied game. Fly ball hit to medium-depth left field — catchable but not routine. If you catch it, the runner will tag and try to score the winning run. Your arm is average. The throw home is about 200 feet. What's your plan BEFORE the ball arrives?
options: Catch the ball and throw home as hard as you can — it's do or die
Catch on the move with momentum toward home, crow-hop, and hit the cutoff
Let the ball drop — if it's a single, the runner scores but you don't risk a bad throw
Position yourself so you catch moving toward the infield, then hit the cutoff man at third-base side
explanations: 'As hard as you can' without a plan leads to wild throws. An average arm from 200 feet is unlikely to reach home on a line. A wild throw lets the batter-runner advance.
Crow-hopping to the cutoff is close, but you need to catch the ball moving TOWARD the infield so your momentum is already going the right direction.
Letting a catchable fly drop concedes the run AND puts the batter on base. Never voluntarily give up outs.

Correct! With an average arm and a 200-foot throw, position yourself so you catch the ball moving toward the infield. Set up slightly behind the ball's landing spot so your momentum carries you toward home. Then hit the cutoff man at third-base side, who redirects to home. The relay system turns your average arm into an effective throw home. Pre-positioning is the key.
scenarios.concept: With an average arm on a do-or-die play, pre-position to catch moving toward the infield and use the cutoff relay

scenarios.title: Sprint-to-Throw Transition — The Crow Hop
scenarios.description: You're in left field. A single hits the gap between you and center field. You sprint to your right to cut it off. You field the ball on the run while moving away from the infield. A runner is trying to go from first to third. How do you transition from sprinting to throwing?
options: Plant your back foot hard, spin, and throw to third as fast as possible
Round the ball — take a curved path so your momentum points toward the infield when you field it
Field it and throw on the run without stopping — save time by not setting your feet
Stop completely, set your feet square to third base, then throw
explanations: Planting hard and spinning while your momentum is going the wrong direction leads to off-balance throws. Your body is moving away from the infield — a hard plant and spin fights your own momentum.

Correct! Rounding the ball means taking a banana-route path so that by the time you field it, your body is facing the infield. This converts your sprinting momentum into throwing momentum. The curve route adds 1-2 steps but gains 10-15 mph on your throw.
Throwing on the run without setting your feet is inaccurate at distances over 100 feet. You lose half your arm strength when your lower body isn't involved.
Stopping completely kills all your momentum. You've just sprinted 30 feet — that energy should transfer into your throw. A crow-hop from momentum beats a dead-stop throw.
scenarios.concept: Round the ball on gap hits — curve your path so your momentum faces the infield when you field it, then crow-hop for a strong, accurate throw

scenarios.title: First Base Fundamentals
scenarios.description: You're at 1st. Grounder to the shortstop, nobody on. What do you do BEFORE the throw?
options: Get to the bag and give a target
Move toward the ball
Stay back from the bag
Cover home
explanations: Get to the bag, face the thrower, stretch toward the ball. Textbook.
SS has it. Your job is to receive the throw at the bag.
Staying back means you can't stretch for offline throws.
Nobody on base — no need to cover home.
scenarios.concept: First baseman: get to the bag early, face the thrower, stretch toward the throw

scenarios.title: First Baseman's DP — Bases Loaded
scenarios.description: Top of the 4th, bases loaded, 1 out, down 4-2. You're the first baseman. Hard ground ball hit right at you, 15 feet from the bag.
options: Step on first, throw home for the double play
Step on first, throw to second for the double play
Throw home first, then cover first after
Step on first — one out is enough
explanations: With bases loaded, step on first (that's out #2), then fire home where the catcher steps on home plate for the force out (out #3 if he hasn't scored). This is the 3-2 double play — two outs AND you prevent a run.
Throwing to second after stepping on first gets two outs but lets the runner from 3rd score.
Throwing home first requires the catcher to step on the plate for a force. Stepping on first is the easy first out, then throw home.
One out is never enough when you can get two. With bases loaded and you near the bag, the double play is right there.
scenarios.concept: Bases loaded, ball hit to the first baseman — step on first, then throw home to prevent the run

scenarios.title: Let It Through or Cut It Off?
scenarios.description: Bot 8th, runner on 2nd, 1 out, up 6-5. Single to right. Runner from 2nd is heading home. You're the cutoff man. The throw from right field is coming — the runner is going to be close at home.
options: Let the throw go through to home — it's on line
Cut it off — the throw is off-line and won't get there
Listen for the catcher's call — he can see the play
Catch it and throw to third to get the batter
explanations: You can't see the runner and the throw at the same time. Letting it through when it's off-line means it gets past the catcher.
Cutting it off when it's on-line means the runner scores for free. You need more information.

The catcher can see the runner AND the throw. He'll yell 'Cut!' if the throw is off-line or the runner has already scored, or 'Let it go!' if the throw is on-target. Listen to your catcher — he's the quarterback of the defense.
Getting the batter at third is secondary to preventing the tying run from scoring.
scenarios.concept: As the cutoff man, listen to the catcher — he can see the play and tells you to cut or let it go

scenarios.title: Pull Hitter — Shade or Stay?
scenarios.description: Top of the 6th, nobody on, 2 outs, up 4-2. A left-handed pull hitter steps in. He's hit every ball to the right side today — 3 ground balls to second and first.
options: Shade the whole infield toward the right side
Play straight up — he might adjust
Only shade the second baseman
Shift the shortstop all the way to the right side
explanations: When a hitter pulls everything, shade your infield toward the pull side. Move the second baseman toward first, the shortstop toward second, and the first baseman closer to the line. You're positioning where the ball is most likely to go based on the data you have.
Playing straight up ignores three at-bats of evidence. Use the information!
Only shading one player is a half-measure. Move the whole infield together.
A full shift with the shortstop on the right side is too extreme for most situations. Shading is enough.
scenarios.concept: Against pull hitters, shade the infield toward the pull side — position where the ball is going

scenarios.title: Pop-Up Between Three Players
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Bot 3rd, nobody on, 1 out. A pop-up is hit between you, the pitcher, and the catcher. All three of you are drifting toward it. Nobody has called it yet.
options: Call for it loudly — you have priority as the first baseman
Let the pitcher catch it — he's closest
Wait for the catcher to call it — he sees the whole field
Wait and see who gets there first
explanations: On pop-ups in the infield, there's a priority system: the fielder behind the play has priority because the ball is moving toward them (easier catch). The first baseman has priority over the pitcher, and the pitcher has priority over the catcher. Someone MUST call 'I got it!' — the worst outcome is nobody calling it.
The pitcher should defer to the first baseman, who is a better fielder and has the ball coming toward him.
The catcher CAN call it, but the first baseman has priority on pop-ups in front of home plate.
Waiting is how easy pop-ups drop for hits. Someone must take charge immediately.
scenarios.concept: On pop-ups, the priority is: infielder behind the play > pitcher > catcher. CALL IT!

scenarios.title: Bunt Defense — Who Covers What?
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, runners on 1st and 2nd, nobody out. Bunt is expected. Your team calls the bunt defense. Who covers what?
options: 1B and 3B charge, SS covers 3rd, 2B covers 1st, pitcher fields near the mound
Everyone holds their position and reacts
1B charges, 3B holds, pitcher covers the bunt area
Only the pitcher and catcher handle the bunt
explanations: Standard bunt defense with runners on 1st and 2nd: the first baseman and third baseman charge toward home as the pitch is delivered. The shortstop breaks to cover third base. The second baseman breaks to cover first base. The pitcher is ready to field the bunt and throw to the base where the lead runner is going. Everyone has a job.
Holding position means slow reaction time. The bunt dies before anyone gets to it.
Only the 1B charging leaves the third base line bunt uncovered if the 3B holds.
The pitcher and catcher alone can't cover all the bases. The whole infield must shift.
scenarios.concept: Bunt defense is a team play — corners charge, SS covers 3rd, 2B covers 1st, pitcher fields

scenarios.title: Holding the Runner On
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. There's a speedy runner on first with one out. The pitcher is in the stretch position and looking back at the runner. The runner is taking a big lead — almost four steps off the bag.
options: Stay on the bag with your foot touching it and give the pitcher a target with your glove for the pickoff throw
Play behind the runner in case the batter hits a ground ball to the right side
Cheat toward second base to get a head start on covering the hole between first and second
Bluff like you're going to the bag, then back off to play deep for a ground ball
explanations: Exactly right! With a fast runner taking a big lead, you need to hold him on the bag. Keep your foot on first and show the pitcher your glove as a target. This keeps the runner honest and close to the base.
Playing behind the runner means he can extend his lead even more, making a stolen base almost automatic. Your job right now is to keep that runner close to first base.
Cheating toward second leaves first base uncovered. If the pitcher tries a pickoff throw, no one is there to catch it and the runner advances easily — or even scores.
Bluffing can work sometimes, but with an aggressive runner already taking four steps, you need to be ON the bag. A bluff won't hold this runner — only your physical presence on the base will.
scenarios.concept: With a fast runner on first, the first baseman's primary job is to hold the runner on by staying on the bag and giving the pitcher a pickoff target.

scenarios.title: Scooping a Short-Hop Throw
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Routine ground ball to shortstop, runner sprinting down the line. The shortstop's throw is low — it's going to bounce about three feet in front of you. The runner is fast and it'll be close.
options: Keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and scoop it by keeping your glove low with the pocket facing up
Pull your foot off the bag and catch the ball cleanly with two hands, then try to tag the runner
Stay upright on the bag and try to catch the ball chest-high after it bounces up
Move off the bag entirely to block the ball like a hockey goalie
explanations: Textbook first baseman play! Keep your foot on the base for the force out, stretch toward the throw to catch it sooner, and scoop with your glove low and open. Great first basemen save errors by picking these short hops every day.

If you pull your foot off the bag, it's not a force out anymore. You'd have to tag the runner, and he's already sprinting past you. Keep your foot on the bag — that's the whole point of a force play at first.
Short-hop throws don't bounce up to chest height at first base — they stay low. Waiting for a big hop means the ball scoots under your glove or past you. Get down to where the ball is.
Leaving the bag means no out even if you stop the ball. First base is all about keeping your foot on the bag while making the catch. That's what makes the position special.
scenarios.concept: First basemen save infielders' errors by scooping short hops — keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and get your glove down low.

scenarios.title: Fielding a Bunt as the First Baseman
scenarios.description: You're at first base. Runner on first, no outs, and the batter squares to bunt. The pitch is bunted hard right at you, about 20 feet down the first base line. The second baseman is sprinting to cover first.
options: Charge the ball, field it, and throw to second base to get the lead runner if you have time
Charge the ball, field it, and flip to the second baseman covering first for the sure out
Let the pitcher field it since bunts are always the pitcher's job
Field the ball and run to first base yourself to get the easy out
explanations: Going to second for the lead runner sounds great, but the ball was bunted hard right at you — by the time you field it, the runner from first has already gotten a good jump. The safe play is to get the sure out at first.
Smart play! The ball is right in front of you, so you can field it quickly. The second baseman is covering first, so flip it to him for the guaranteed out. Getting the sure out prevents a big inning.
Bunts are NOT always the pitcher's job. When a bunt comes right at the first baseman, you field it. The pitcher covers his zone, and you cover yours. This one is yours all the way.
Running to first yourself takes too long and you might not beat the batter. The second baseman is already sprinting to cover first — flip it to him for a much faster out.
scenarios.concept: On bunt plays, the first baseman fields balls in his zone and flips to the second baseman covering first for the sure out.

scenarios.title: Cover First or Play the Ball?
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runner on second, one out. The batter hits a sharp ground ball right in the hole between you and second base — about 15 feet to your right. The pitcher is breaking toward first base to cover. The second baseman is also moving toward the ball.
options: Let the second baseman field it — sprint back to first base so someone is there to take the throw
Go after the ball yourself since you're closer, and let the pitcher cover first
Freeze and see what happens — wait to see if the second baseman or pitcher gets to it first
Go after the ball and then try to beat the runner back to first base yourself
explanations: This is the right call! When the ball is in the hole, the second baseman has a better angle to field it and throw to first. Your job is to get back to the bag so there's someone to receive the throw. If nobody's on first, the out is impossible.

You might be closer, but the second baseman has a much better throwing angle to first base from that position. If you field it, you have to spin and throw across your body or run to the bag yourself — both are harder plays.

Freezing is the worst thing a first baseman can do. Every ground ball requires an instant decision — either go get the ball or get back to the bag. Hesitation means nobody covers first and the runner is safe.

You cannot field a ball 15 feet to your right and then beat a sprinting batter back to first base. Even the fastest first baseman can't outrun a thrown ball. Get to the bag and let your teammate make the throw.
scenarios.concept: First basemen must decide instantly: go after the ball or get back to the bag. When the second baseman has a better angle, get to first and be the target.

scenarios.title: Cutoff Positioning on a Single to Right
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runner on second base, and the batter singles to right field. The runner is rounding third and heading home. The right fielder picks up the ball and is ready to throw. You need to get in the cutoff position.
options: Line up between the right fielder and home plate, about 45 feet from home, with your arms up so the fielder can see you
Run to home plate to back up the catcher in case the throw gets past him
Stay at first base in case the batter tries to stretch his single into a double
Move halfway between first and second base to cover the cutoff from right-center field
explanations: Perfect cutoff positioning! Line up between the outfielder and home plate so the throw comes right through you. Arms up so the fielder can see your target. You can cut the throw and redirect it or let it go through to the catcher.

Backing up the catcher sounds helpful, but who's going to cut the throw off? If the throw is offline or the catcher has no play, nobody is there to redirect the ball. The cutoff man is the most important player on this play.
Staying at first base ignores the bigger play — a run is about to score! The batter advancing to second doesn't matter as much as cutting down the runner at home. Cutoff plays save runs.
The cutoff position for a throw from right field to home plate is between the fielder and home, not between first and second. You need to be in a straight line from the right fielder to the catcher.
scenarios.concept: On throws to home from right field, the first baseman is the cutoff man — line up between the fielder and home plate with your arms raised.

scenarios.title: Turning the 3-6-3 Double Play
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runner on first, one out, ground ball hit right at you. The runner is going on the pitch. You field the ball cleanly about eight feet in front of the bag. The shortstop is covering second base.
options: Throw to the shortstop at second base for the force out, then sprint back to first and get ready for the return throw
Tag first base yourself first, then throw to second to try to get the runner
Hold the ball and just tag first base for the one sure out
Throw to second base and let the pitcher cover first for the return throw
explanations: That's the 3-6-3 double play! Throw to the shortstop at second for the first out, then hustle back to the bag for the return throw from the shortstop. It's one of the hardest double plays in baseball, but it ends the inning in a tie game.

Tagging first base first removes the force at second — without the force, it becomes a TAG play, which is fundamentally harder. The shortstop now has to tag the runner instead of just touching the base. Get the lead runner first while the force is still on.
One out is okay, but in a tie game in the 7th inning, a double play ends the inning and keeps the score tied. You have a chance for two outs — take it.

The pitcher covering first is less reliable than you getting back to the bag yourself. The 3-6-3 is your play to make — you know where the bag is and you're the best at receiving throws at first base.
scenarios.concept: The 3-6-3 double play requires quick feet — throw to the shortstop at second, then sprint back to first for the return throw.

scenarios.title: Foul Ball Communication Near First
scenarios.description: You're at first base. The batter pops a foul ball high in the air between first base and the dugout on the home team side. The second baseman is running over, the pitcher is drifting toward the area, and the ball is drifting toward the stands.
options: Call 'I got it! I got it!' loudly and wave off your teammates since you have the best angle as the first baseman
Let the second baseman take it since he's running toward the ball
Wait to see who gets there first and then the closest player catches it
Stay quiet and just try to catch it without saying anything
explanations: Take charge! Foul balls on the first base side are your territory. You're closest to the stands, you know how much room you have, and you face these pop-ups all the time. Call it loud and clear so nobody crashes into each other.

The second baseman is running a longer distance and doesn't know how close the stands are. The first baseman plays near the dugout every day and knows exactly how much room is available. This is your ball.
Waiting to see who gets there first causes collisions. Somebody has to call for the ball EARLY so the other players can get out of the way. Hesitation leads to dropped balls and banged-up teammates.

Never try to catch a fly ball silently! Without calling it, two or three players might converge on the same spot. Collisions cause injuries and dropped balls. Always communicate — be loud and be early.
scenarios.concept: On foul balls near your position, call it loudly and early — communication prevents collisions and dropped balls.

scenarios.title: First-and-Third Pickoff Play
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runners on first and third, one out. The runner on first takes off for second base on the pitch. Your catcher catches the ball and fires a throw to second base — but it's actually a designed play where the shortstop will cut the throw and look at third.
options: Sprint to cover home plate in case the runner on third tries to score during the chaos of the play
Stay near first base in case the throw from the shortstop comes back to you for a pickoff on the runner retreating
Get behind the mound to back up any throw that goes to second base or back to the pitcher
Run toward the runner going to second to get in a rundown between first and second
explanations: The catcher covers home plate — that's his position. If you leave first base to go home, nobody is covering first and you create a gap the offense can exploit. Stay at your position and trust your catcher.

Smart positioning! In a first-and-third situation, the shortstop might cut the throw and fire back to first if the runner on first hesitates or retreats. If you're on the bag, you can catch the throw and get a surprise out.

Backing up behind the mound takes you away from first base, which is your responsibility. Every player has a specific job on this play, and yours is to stay at first base ready for a possible return throw.

Running toward the baserunner creates confusion and leaves first base completely uncovered. Rundowns are for when the ball is already in play between two fielders — let the middle infielders handle this.
scenarios.concept: In first-and-third pickoff plays, the first baseman must stay on the bag — the throw might come right back to you for a surprise out.

scenarios.title: Hold Runner, Lefty Batter
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Tie game, runner on first with decent speed. A left-handed batter steps in — his swing naturally pulls toward right field, right where you normally stand. The pitcher is looking back at the runner, who has a three-step lead.
options: Hold the runner on the bag like normal — stay with your foot on first and give the pitcher a target
Play behind the runner in your normal defensive position since lefties pull the ball to you
Cheat halfway — stand two steps off the bag toward your regular position to split the difference
Hold the runner, but after the pitcher starts his motion, quickly peel off toward your defensive position
explanations: Holding the runner on the bag is solid, but with a left-handed pull hitter, staying glued to the bag leaves a massive hole on the right side. A pulled ground ball goes right through where you should be standing.
Playing full depth behind the runner gives him a huge lead and a free stolen base. In a tie game, letting a runner reach scoring position for free is a gift.
Splitting the difference means you're too far off the bag to hold the runner AND too close to the bag to field a pulled grounder. You end up bad at both jobs.

This is the advanced first baseman move. Hold the runner until the pitcher commits home, then quickly release to your fielding position. You keep the runner honest AND cover the hole against the lefty pull hitter.
scenarios.concept: Against left-handed hitters, hold the runner until the pitcher delivers, then peel off to cover the right side — you need to do both jobs.

scenarios.title: Bunt in Front of the Mound
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runner on first, no outs. The batter bunts the ball and it dribbles to a stop about 30 feet in front of home plate — right between the mound and the first base line. The pitcher, catcher, and you are all looking at it. Nobody has called for it yet.
options: Sprint toward the ball and call 'I got it!' — the first baseman should take charge on bunts
Hang back and let the pitcher or catcher field it since they're closer to the ball
Charge toward the ball but stay quiet and see who gets there first
Sprint toward the ball and call 'I got it!' while pointing at where you want the pitcher to go cover first
explanations: Charging and calling for it is great, but if you field the ball, who covers first base? You need someone there to throw to. Communication is everything on bunt plays.
Hanging back means nobody takes charge. The bunt sits there while three players stare at each other and the runners advance. Somebody has to go get the ball.
Charging silently is a recipe for a collision. The pitcher and catcher are also moving toward the ball. Without calling it, two players might crash into each other.

This is the complete play! You call for the ball — the first baseman owns bunts in his zone on bunt defense — AND you direct the pitcher to cover first base. On a bunt, 2B covers first if 1B charges; the pitcher backs up by sprinting to the bag. Call it loud, field it clean, direct your teammates.
scenarios.concept: On bunts, call for the ball AND direct your teammates — tell the pitcher to cover first so you have someone to throw to.

scenarios.title: Scoop the Short-Hop From Third
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Two outs, runner on second. The batter hits a sharp grounder to the third baseman who backhands it deep behind the bag. He throws hard across the diamond but the ball is going to short-hop you about two feet in front of first base. This is a tougher scoop than a throw from shortstop because the ball has more velocity and a different angle.
options: Keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and turn your glove to get a forehand scoop on the ball
Stretch out but keep your glove in the backhand position since the ball is coming from the left side
Pull your foot off the bag and use two hands to block the ball and keep it in front of you
Stay upright on the bag and try to short-hop it with your glove at waist height
explanations: Textbook! On throws from third base, stretch toward the throw and turn your glove for a forehand scoop. The forehand position keeps your glove pocket open and facing the ball, giving you the best chance to pick the short hop cleanly.
A backhand scoop is harder to control and the ball can kick off the back of your glove. On throws from the left side, rotating to a forehand position gives you a much bigger scooping surface.
Pulling your foot off the bag means even if you catch it, the runner is safe. With two outs you need this out — keep your foot on the base and make the scoop.
Short hops don't bounce up to waist height from two feet away. The ball stays low. You need to get your glove down to the dirt to scoop it, not wait for it to come up to you.
scenarios.concept: On short-hop throws from third base, stretch and rotate your glove to the forehand position — the bigger scooping surface gives you the best chance.

scenarios.title: 3-6-3 Double Play — Quick Feet
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman in a tied game. Runner on first, no outs. The batter hits a hard grounder right at you. You field it cleanly just in front of the bag. The shortstop is already breaking for second base to cover. The runner from first has only taken two steps — he got a bad jump. You have time, but you need to execute perfectly.
options: Throw to the shortstop covering second, then sprint back to first base for the return throw
Step on first base to get the batter out, then throw to second for the runner
Hold the ball, tag first base, and let the team settle for one out
Flip to the pitcher covering first, then let the pitcher throw to second
explanations: This is the 3-6-3 double play executed perfectly! Throw to the shortstop for the force at second, then hustle back to first for the return throw. Getting the lead runner first keeps the force play alive at both bases. Two outs, inning over.

Stepping on first REMOVES the force at second base. Now instead of the shortstop just touching second, he has to tag the runner — which is much harder and slower. Always get the lead runner first when turning two.
Settling for one out when you have a chance to end the inning with a double play wastes an opportunity. In a tie game with no outs, a double play completely changes the momentum.
The pitcher covering first adds an unnecessary relay. You're RIGHT THERE at first base — you can get back to the bag faster than the pitcher can get there. The 3-6-3 is your play.
scenarios.concept: On the 3-6-3 double play, always throw to second FIRST to keep the force play alive — stepping on first first kills the force at second.

scenarios.title: Play Back or Hold — Tie Game
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Bottom of the 7th in a tie game. Runner on first, no outs, and the batter is the opposing team's best power hitter. The runner on first is slow — a catcher who's only stolen two bases all season. Your pitcher has a good pickoff move.
options: Hold the runner on the bag like you always do when there's a runner on first
Play behind the runner at your normal depth — the power hitter is the bigger threat
Play in closer to home plate to cut off a ground ball through the right side
Shade toward the foul line to prevent an extra-base hit down the line
explanations: With a slow runner who almost never steals and a pitcher with a good pickoff move, holding the runner on the bag sacrifices your defensive range for nothing. The runner isn't going anywhere.

Smart! A slow runner with only two steals isn't a threat. Playing at your normal depth gives you much better range to field ground balls from the power hitter. Your pitcher's pickoff move keeps the runner honest without your help.
Playing in makes sense for bunt defense, but a power hitter isn't bunting. Moving in just makes hard ground balls reach you faster, giving you less reaction time.

Shading the line could work late in a game, but with no outs and a runner on first, taking away the right-side hole is more valuable. A ground ball through the right side advances the runner to third — a single down the line does too, but it's less likely.
scenarios.concept: When the runner on first is slow and not a stolen base threat, play at normal depth to maximize your defensive range against the batter.

scenarios.title: Picking the Ball in the Dirt
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Routine ground ball to the second baseman with a runner on first and one out. The second baseman makes the throw but it bounces in the dirt about five feet in front of you — a classic short hop. You can see the ball clearly and the batter is still a few steps from first base.
options: Keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, bend your knees, and scoop your glove along the ground like you're sweeping the dirt
Stay upright and try to catch the ball after it bounces up to you
Pull your foot off the bag so you can move freely to block the ball
Close your eyes and stab at where you think the ball will be
explanations: Perfect form! Keeping your foot on the bag maintains the force out. Stretching toward the throw catches the ball sooner, before the hop gets unpredictable. Bending low and sweeping the glove along the ground gives you the best chance to scoop it cleanly.
Staying upright means the ball could bounce under your glove or skip past you. Short hops stay low — you need to get down to the ball's level, not wait for it to come up to yours.
Pulling your foot off the bag means there's no force out even if you catch the ball perfectly. The whole point of first base is keeping your foot on the bag while making the catch.
Never close your eyes! You need to watch the ball all the way into your glove. Closing your eyes and guessing leads to errors and potential injuries from getting hit by the ball.
scenarios.concept: Scooping throws in the dirt is a first baseman's signature skill — keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and sweep your glove low along the ground.

scenarios.title: The Cutoff — RF to Home
scenarios.description: Runner on 2nd, nobody out. Single to right field. RF is throwing home. You're first base. What's your job?
options: Line up as the cutoff between RF and home plate
Stay at first base — this isn't your cutoff
Become the relay to second base
Back up third base on the throw
explanations: Correct. First base is the cutoff man on throws from right field to home plate. You position yourself in a direct line between the RF and home, approximately 45 feet in front of the plate. The catcher reads the play and either calls 'cut' (redirect to another base) or says nothing (let it go through). Without you, a bad RF throw has no second chance.

Staying at first base on an RF throw home leaves a critical gap. The cutoff man's job is to give the catcher a redirect option and cut off throws that can't reach home in time. First base owns this responsibility.
The relay to second on RF-to-home throws belongs to the shortstop or second baseman, not first base. First base's lane is the direct RF-to-home line.
Backing up third base on this play belongs to the pitcher. Your lane is RF-to-home as cutoff. Leave third base backup to the pitcher.
scenarios.concept: First base is the cutoff man on throws from right field to home

scenarios.title: Scooping the Short-Hop
scenarios.description: Ground ball to shortstop. His throw is in the dirt, 3 feet in front of the bag. What's your technique?
options: Keep your foot on the bag, drop your glove to the dirt, scoop up through the ball
Jump off the bag and block it like a catcher
Pull your foot off the bag early and catch it on the short-hop
Ignore the bag and just knock it down to keep it in the infield
explanations: Correct. The scoop technique: maintain bag contact with your foot (either heel or toe depending on your stretch), drop your glove below the ball, and scoop up through the bounce. Eyes stay on the ball through the entire movement. MLB first basemen convert short-hop scoops at an 87% success rate using this technique — the key is trusting the glove angle rather than trying to time the bounce.
Jumping off the bag turns a potential out into a sure hit. You lose the out the moment your foot leaves the base before catching the ball. Stay connected.
Pulling your foot early to 'catch it cleanly' is the most common first-base mistake at youth level. The foot must stay until the ball is in the glove. Umpires watch the foot, not the glove.
Knocking it down may save a run in a desperation scenario but it's not technique — and you give up the out. If there's any chance to scoop it, scoop it.
scenarios.concept: Short-hop scoop: keep foot on bag, drop glove to dirt, scoop through

scenarios.title: Pickoff Tag — Swipe or Sweep?
scenarios.description: Runner takes a big lead at first. Pitcher fires over for a pickoff. The throw is on time. Do you swipe tag or sweep tag?
options: Swipe tag — apply quickly at the front corner of the bag where the runner dives
Sweep tag — bring the glove across the runner's body in a wide arc
Block the bag with your body and let him run into the tag
Catch and hold — wait for the umpire to look
explanations: Correct. The swipe tag at the front corner of the bag is the fastest and safest technique. Your glove meets the runner's hand or foot at the earliest contact point — the front edge of the bag where he dives back. Place the back of the glove down so the ball doesn't get knocked loose on contact. MLB first basemen apply this tag in under 0.15 seconds from catch to tag completion.

The sweep tag (wide arc across the body) takes 3-4x longer than the swipe tag and exposes the ball to being knocked loose. On a close pickoff, the sweep will be too slow. Reserve the sweep for runners already committed past the bag.
Blocking the bag creates obstruction risk. You must present the tag, not your body. Letting a runner run into you is Type A obstruction — automatic safe, possibly advancing other runners.
Catching and holding confuses the umpire and delays the tag. Apply it immediately upon catching — don't wait. Delayed tags are never called out.
scenarios.concept: Pickoff tag: swipe at front bag corner, back of glove down

scenarios.title: Bunt Defense — Your Crash
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, nobody out. Batter shows bunt early. You're playing at normal depth. When do you break for the bunt?
options: Break hard on the pitch — commit to the bunt as the pitcher delivers
Wait until you see the ball hit the ground
Creep forward but don't commit until contact
Stay back — let the pitcher handle bunts down the line
explanations: Correct. On a bunt defense, first base crashes hard on the pitch. The timing key is 'go when the pitcher's arm comes forward.' Committing on delivery gives you the speed to field a bunt and still have a play. Hesitation — waiting to see where the ball goes — means arriving late and having no play anywhere. Hard crash = options. Hesitation = no play.
Waiting until the ball hits the ground means you arrive 1-2 seconds late. By then, the batter is halfway to first and you have no throw. Reaction-based charging kills all bunt defense plays.
Creeping forward is half-charging — you're slow to the ball and you've already left first base uncovered without gaining the speed to field it effectively. Commit fully or hold your position.

Letting the pitcher handle first-base-side bunts alone is too passive. Pitchers field bunts down the first base line, but you crash to give a second fielder option and to take a quick throw back from the catcher.
scenarios.concept: Bunt defense: 1B crashes hard on the pitch, not after contact

scenarios.title: Runner on Second — Hold or Play Back?
scenarios.description: Runner on 2nd base only, 1 out, tie game in the 7th. Do you hold the runner at second or play at normal depth?
options: Play at normal depth — no need to hold a runner on second
Hold the runner tight at second base
Play halfway — between hold and normal depth
Shade toward the line to guard against the double
explanations: Correct. There is never a standard reason to hold a runner at second base. Holding a runner at second gains you almost nothing — the runner can score from second on a single regardless of his lead. Normal depth at first base keeps you in the play for grounders, gives you better range, and maintains your ability to cover first on bunts. Holding at second base is textbook positional waste.

Holding at second base is one of the most common strategic mistakes in youth baseball. The runner scores from second on a hit anyway — your two extra feet of lead inhibition buys nothing. You surrender your range and your coverage duty at first.

'Halfway' positioning combines the weaknesses of both — you're not covering first effectively and you're not helping at second. Position yourself for your primary job: fielding ground balls and receiving throws at first.
Shading toward the line in a 1-out, tie game is reasonable late-game positioning — guarding against an extra-base hit. But the core answer is: play normal depth. No hold at second base.
scenarios.concept: Never hold a runner at second base — no strategic value

scenarios.title: Relay Position on Extra-Base Hit to Right
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. A ball is hit to the right-center gap and it's rolling to the wall. Runner on first is rounding second and heading for third. Where do you position yourself?
options: Line up between the outfielder and home plate as the cutoff man
Line up between the outfielder and third base
Stay at first base in case the batter tries to stretch to a double
Run to back up the throw at third base
explanations: On extra-base hits to right-center, the first baseman is the cutoff man. Line up in a direct line between where the outfielder fields the ball and home plate, about 45 feet in front of home. Listen for the catcher's call to cut it or let it through.
Lining up toward third is the shortstop's or second baseman's job on relay plays. The first baseman's lane is always toward home plate on throws from the right side of the outfield.
Staying at first base takes you out of the most important play happening on the field. Nobody is going back to first — the action is at home. Get into cutoff position.
Backing up third is the pitcher's responsibility. Your job as first baseman is the cutoff lane between the outfield and home plate.
scenarios.concept: On extra-base hits to right field, the first baseman is the cutoff man between the outfielder and home plate

scenarios.title: Bunt With Runner on Second — Where to Throw?
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Runner on 2nd, nobody out. The batter drops a sacrifice bunt right in front of you. You charge and field it cleanly. The runner on second is going to third. Where do you throw?
options: Throw to first — get the easy out on the batter
Throw to third — try to get the lead runner
Hold the ball and look at both runners
Throw home — the runner might try to score
explanations: Get the sure out at first! When you charge a bunt with a runner on second, the lead runner usually gets a great jump to third. Trying to throw behind a runner going to third is risky and often results in nobody being out. Take the guaranteed out at first base.
The runner on second had a big head start. Throwing to third is a bang-bang play where an error means everyone is safe and the batter reaches first. The safe play is the out you can get.
Holding and looking takes time, and now both runners are safe. On bunt plays, you have to decide quickly. In this situation, take the out at first.
The runner on second is going to third, not home. There's no play at home plate on a sacrifice bunt from second to third.
scenarios.concept: On sacrifice bunts, take the sure out — don't try to be a hero by getting the lead runner when the throw is risky

scenarios.title: 3-1 Putout — Unassisted
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Ground ball hit right to you. Nobody on base, 1 out. You field it cleanly. The pitcher isn't covering first yet. What do you do?
options: Take it to the bag yourself — step on first for the unassisted putout
Throw to the pitcher covering first
Hold the ball and wait for the pitcher to get there
Throw to second base
explanations: If you're close enough to the bag, take it yourself! A 3-1 unassisted putout (first baseman to first base) is the safest play when the pitcher hasn't gotten over yet. No throw means no chance of a throwing error.
The pitcher isn't covering first yet. Waiting for him and then throwing adds time and risk. If you can reach the bag yourself, that's always the safer play.
Holding and waiting lets the batter get closer to first. If you're near the bag, just go step on it yourself.
Nobody is running to second base. First base is where the out is — take the ball there.
scenarios.concept: When you field the ball near first base and can reach the bag, take the unassisted putout — no throw means no error

scenarios.title: Pop-Up Near the Dugout
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Foul ball popped up high on the first base side, drifting toward the dugout. The second baseman is also running over. Who takes it?
options: You take it — call everyone off and track the ball
