Let the second baseman take it since he's running toward the ball
Let whoever gets there first catch it
Back off and let the catcher handle it
explanations: Foul pop-ups on the first base side are your ball as the first baseman. You're closer, you have a better angle, and the second baseman would be running away from the play. Call 'I got it!' loudly and repeatedly, track the ball, and watch for the dugout railing.
The second baseman would be running toward the dugout — away from the infield and toward an obstacle. You're already facing the right direction and are closer to the play. Take charge.
Letting whoever gets there first is how collisions happen. Someone must call for the ball, and on the first base side, that's the first baseman. Take charge and call everyone off.
The catcher handles pop-ups directly behind home plate. This one is on the first base side — it's your territory as the first baseman.
scenarios.concept: Pop-ups on the first base side belong to the first baseman — call for the ball and watch for the dugout railing

scenarios.title: Wide Throw From the Shortstop
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Routine grounder to shortstop, but his throw is pulling you off the bag toward the home plate side. The batter-runner is about three steps from first. What do you do?
options: Stretch toward the throw with your glove foot on the bag — reach as far as you can while maintaining contact
Come off the bag entirely and catch the ball cleanly — you can tag the runner
Switch feet and stretch with your throwing-side foot on the bag
Stay flat-footed on the bag and wait for the ball to come to you
explanations: Stretching with your glove foot on the bag gives you maximum reach toward the throw while keeping the force play alive. This is the fundamental first baseman stretch — every inch of reach can be the difference between safe and out.
Coming off the bag gives up the force play. Now you need to tag the runner, which is much harder on a bang-bang play. Keep your foot on the bag whenever possible.
Switching feet reduces your reach toward the throw. The glove-side foot on the bag keeps your body oriented toward the throw and gives you the longest stretch.
Staying flat-footed gives you no extra reach. The whole point of the stretch is to catch the ball as early as possible — every split second matters on a close play at first.
scenarios.concept: Stretch with your glove foot on the bag to maximize reach — keeping the force play alive is almost always better than coming off the bag to tag

scenarios.title: Holding the Runner With a Left-Handed Pitcher
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. Fast runner on first with a left-handed pitcher on the mound. The runner has been getting big secondary leads. How do you and the lefty work together to keep him close?
options: Hold your position at first — the lefty can see the runner and pick him off without your help
Give the pitcher a target with your glove, stay tight to the bag, and time the pickoff together with a pre-arranged signal
Cheat toward the bag early so the runner sees you're ready
Play behind the runner to take away the big lead
explanations: A left-handed pitcher has a natural advantage seeing the runner at first, but the first baseman is still critical. Without a target and a pre-arranged timing signal, pickoff throws are less accurate and less effective. Teamwork makes pickoffs work.

The first baseman and lefty pitcher work as a unit. Hold your glove up as a target near the bag, stay tight so you can receive the throw and apply the tag quickly, and use a pre-arranged count or signal so you both time the move together. Left-handed pickoffs are devastating when the first baseman and pitcher are in sync.
Cheating toward the bag too early tips off the runner. A good base stealer will see you moving early and shorten his lead. Stay relaxed until the signal, then move to the bag.

Playing behind the runner means you can't receive the pickoff throw at first base. If the pitcher fires over, there's nobody to catch it. Stay close to the bag where you can receive throws and apply tags.
scenarios.concept: First baseman and lefty pitcher work as a unit on pickoffs — pre-arranged signals and a visible target make the timing devastating

scenarios.title: Shallow Fly Tag-Up
scenarios.description: You're in right. Runner on 3rd, 1 out. Shallow fly — you catch it. Runner tags up.
options: Throw home immediately
Throw to cutoff
Hold — too shallow
Throw to 2nd
explanations: Shallow = you're CLOSE to home. Direct throw has your best shot.
Cutoff relay adds time on a short throw.
Being shallow means you're CLOSER. Best chance ever.
The runner scores the priority — second base doesn't matter on a tag-up play.
scenarios.concept: On shallow fly balls, throw directly home — the short distance is your advantage

scenarios.title: Backing Up Bases
scenarios.description: You're in right field. Grounder to shortstop, runner on 2nd going to 3rd. Where should you go?
options: Stay in position
Back up 1st base
Back up 3rd base
Back up home
explanations: Staying in position means you can't help if there's an overthrow.
Correct! Right fielder backs up throws to 1st. If the ball gets away, you're there.
Left fielder backs up 3rd, not right fielder. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
Catcher covers home. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
scenarios.concept: Always back up the base where the throw is going — anticipate overthrows

scenarios.title: Who Backs Up the Throw?
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, runner on 1st, nobody out. Ground ball to the shortstop — he's throwing to first. You're the right fielder. What should you be doing?
options: Stand and watch — it's an infield play
Back up first base
Run toward the infield to help
Back up second base
explanations: Standing and watching is lazy outfield play. Every outfielder has a backup responsibility on every play.
The right fielder backs up throws to first base. If the throw gets past the first baseman, you're there to keep the runner from advancing. This prevents one error from becoming two bases.
Running toward the infield gets you too close. You need to be behind first base at a distance.
The throw is going to first, not second. Back up the base where the throw is going.
scenarios.concept: Right fielders back up first base on infield grounders — prevent one error from becoming two bases

scenarios.title: Runner Rounding Second Hard
scenarios.description: Bot 4th, runner on 1st, nobody out, down 2-1. Clean single to right field. The runner from first is rounding second hard — the first base coach is waving him to third.
options: Throw to third — cut him down
Hit the cutoff man and let him redirect
Throw home in case he tries to score
Hold the ball — he's already at third
explanations: Throwing directly to third from right field is a long throw that's likely to sail. If it gets past the third baseman, the runner scores.

Hit the cutoff man! On a single to right with a runner going first to third, your job is to throw a strong, low throw to the cutoff. He can redirect to third if there's a play, or hold it to prevent the batter from advancing. The cutoff creates options.
Throwing home when the runner is only going to third wastes a throw and lets the batter advance.
Holding the ball lets runners take extra bases for free. Always make a throw.
scenarios.concept: On singles to right with runners advancing, hit the cutoff — he can redirect to the right base

scenarios.title: Fly Ball in the Sun
scenarios.description: Bot 4th, nobody on, 1 out. Fly ball hit to right field — you look up and the sun is directly in your eyes. You lost the ball!
options: Use your glove as a sun shield — put it up to block the sun
Close your eyes and guess where it'll land
Call off and let someone else catch it
Drop your head and pick up the ball below the sun
explanations: Use your glove to shield the sun! Raise your glove above your eyes to block the sun while keeping your other eye on the ball. This is the classic sun-fly technique that every outfielder learns. You can track the ball in the shadow your glove creates.
Never close your eyes in the outfield. You'll get hit in the face.
Calling off wastes time. Use the glove-shield technique to track the ball yourself.
Dropping your head means you lose the ball completely. Keep your head up and use the glove to block the sun.
scenarios.concept: On fly balls in the sun, use your glove as a sun shield — raise it to block the sun while tracking the ball

scenarios.title: Communication Between Outfielders
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, nobody on. Ball hit to the gap in right-center. Both you (RF) and the center fielder are sprinting for it. You're both close.
options: Call 'I got it!' loudly and clearly
Don't call it — whoever gets there first catches it
Wave your glove so the CF sees you
Call 'Mine!' and keep running — he'll get out of the way
explanations: CALL IT! Yell 'I got it!' or 'Mine!' loudly and clearly. If the center fielder also calls it, defer to him — he has priority. If he doesn't call it, it's yours. Communication prevents collisions and dropped balls. The call must be LOUD — a stadium is noisy.
Not calling it guarantees confusion and possibly a collision. Always communicate.
Waving your glove takes your hand off the catch. Use your voice, not your glove.
'Mine!' works, but you should also listen for the CF's call. If he calls it, peel off.
scenarios.concept: On fly balls between fielders, CALL IT loudly — communication prevents collisions and errors

scenarios.title: Ball at the Wall — Make the Play or Protect Yourself?
scenarios.description: Top of the 9th, runner on 1st, 2 outs, up 5-4. Deep fly ball to the warning track. You're sprinting back and feel the warning track under your feet. The ball is catchable but you'll hit the wall.
options: Make the catch — win the game, worry about the wall later
Pull up — your safety comes first
Reach up and try to time the catch before impact
Feel the wall with your hand, then make the catch
explanations: Crashing into the wall without awareness is how outfielders get seriously hurt. Be smart.
Pulling up lets the ball drop and the game might be lost. There's a better way.
Reaching up blindly might work, but you could hit the wall face-first.

Feel the warning track, then find the wall with your non-glove hand. Reach up with your glove hand for the catch while bracing yourself against the wall with your other hand. This is the technique all great outfielders use — it lets you make the catch while protecting yourself from the full impact of the wall.
scenarios.concept: At the wall, use your free hand to find and brace against it while catching with your glove hand

scenarios.title: Gun Down at Third
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A sharp single is hit to right field. The runner on first base is rounding second hard and trying to advance to third. You field the ball cleanly on one hop. You have a strong arm and third base is open.
options: Fire a strong throw to third base on a line to try to nail the runner
Throw to second base to keep the batter from advancing
Hold the ball and jog it back in to the cutoff man
Throw home in case the runner doesn't stop at third
explanations: You have a clean field and a strong arm. Throwing to third base to get the advancing runner is the high-value play. Getting an out here stops a runner from reaching scoring position.
Throwing to second lets the runner take third base for free. The batter already has a single — the dangerous runner is the one heading to third.
Holding the ball and jogging it in is playing too safe. You have a chance to get an out, and that's always worth taking when you field the ball cleanly.
The runner is going to third, not home. Throwing home wastes your arm and lets the runner take third easily while accomplishing nothing.
scenarios.concept: Right fielders with strong arms can keep runners from going first-to-third — that throw takes away a scoring-position runner.

scenarios.title: Stop First-to-Third
scenarios.description: You're in right field. A ground ball single comes through the infield and rolls toward you. There's a runner on first base who is a fast runner. Your coach always says the right fielder's most important job is preventing first-to-third advances.
options: Charge the ball aggressively to field it quickly and throw to third base
Hang back and wait for the ball to come to you, then throw to second
Let the ball roll to you while watching the runner
Throw to first base to make sure the batter doesn't take second
explanations: Charging the ball cuts down the time the runner has to advance. Getting to the ball fast and making a quick throw to third gives you the best chance to cut down the runner or at least hold him at second.
Hanging back lets the runner round second and cruise into third easily. Every extra second the ball is on the ground is an extra step for the runner.
Watching the runner instead of attacking the ball is passive play. By the time the ball reaches you, the runner is standing on third.
Throwing to first doesn't help since the batter already has a single. The critical play is stopping the lead runner from taking third.
scenarios.concept: Charge ground ball singles aggressively in right field — every second you save keeps runners from going first-to-third.

scenarios.title: Hit the Cutoff Man
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A deep fly ball drops in front of the warning track for a hit. A runner is scoring from second base. You pick up the ball 320 feet from home plate. The second baseman is set up as your cutoff man about 150 feet from you. Your arm can reach home on the fly from 250 feet but not from 320.
options: Throw a hard one-hop throw to the cutoff man so he can relay it home
Air-mail a throw toward home plate even though you can't reach it
Run the ball in closer before throwing so you can reach home
Throw to third base instead to keep the batter from advancing
explanations: Hitting the cutoff man with a strong throw gives your team the best chance. The relay can reach home faster than you can throw it there, and the cutoff man can redirect the throw if needed.
Throwing a rainbow toward home that bounces multiple times gives the runner an easy score and lets the batter take extra bases. A throw you can't reach is a wasted throw.
Running the ball in takes too long. The runner scores easily while you're jogging forward, and now the batter advances too.
Throwing to third ignores the runner scoring. The run matters more than the batter's position, and the cutoff man can redirect to third anyway if needed.
scenarios.concept: When you can't reach home on the fly, always hit your cutoff man — a good relay is faster than a bad long throw.

scenarios.title: Playing the Wall
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A hard line drive is hit down the right field line toward the corner. The ball is going to hit the wall. You need to play the carom. Your coach taught you that balls hitting the wall straight-on bounce straight back, but balls hitting the corner angle toward center field.
options: Angle your run toward center field side of the corner to play the carom off the wall
Run straight at where the ball will hit the wall and wait for it
Run to the corner and put your back against the wall to trap the ball
Stay where you are and let the ball bounce back to you
explanations: The ball hitting the corner will angle back toward center field. By running to where the carom will end up instead of where the ball hits, you save precious seconds and field the ball much quicker.
Running straight at the wall means the carom bounces past you toward center field. You'll have to spin around and chase it, giving the runners extra time to advance.
Putting your back against the wall is dangerous and the ball will bounce off the wall well before it reaches you, caroming away toward center field.
Staying where you are means the ball bounces around in the corner while runners circle the bases. You need to attack the ball, not wait for it.
scenarios.concept: Learn how balls carom off the wall — corner hits angle toward center field, so position yourself where the bounce will go, not where the ball hits.

scenarios.title: Shallow Fly Communication
scenarios.description: You're in right field. A soft fly ball is hit to shallow right field. The second baseman is drifting back and you're charging in. You can see the second baseman is going to have to make an over-the-shoulder catch, while you're coming straight in with the ball in front of you.
options: Call 'I got it!' loudly and keep charging — the ball is in front of you for an easier catch
Stay quiet and let the second baseman take it since he's closer
Call off the second baseman but then slow down and let it drop
Call for the infielder to take it since infielders have priority on pop flies
explanations: An outfielder coming in has the ball in front of them and more momentum for a throw. Outfielders have priority over infielders on balls they can reach. Call it loud and clear to avoid a collision!
Staying quiet with two fielders converging is how collisions happen. Someone must call for the ball, and the outfielder has the better angle coming forward.
Calling for the ball and then slowing down could cause the ball to drop for a hit with runners on base. If you call it, commit to making the catch.
Infielders have priority on infield pop-ups, but on balls in the shallow outfield, the outfielder has priority because they're running forward with the ball in front of them.
scenarios.concept: Outfielders have priority over infielders on fly balls because running in is easier than running out with the ball over your shoulder.

scenarios.title: Back Up First Base
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A ground ball is hit to the shortstop with nobody on base and one out. The shortstop fields it and throws to first base. You know your coach always says right fielders should back up first base on infield grounders.
options: Sprint toward the area behind first base in foul territory to back up the throw
Stay in right field and watch the play since it's a routine grounder
Run toward second base to cover in case of an error
Move toward center field to back up a possible overthrow there
explanations: Backing up first base is one of the right fielder's most important responsibilities. If the throw gets past the first baseman, you're there to keep the ball from rolling to the fence and the batter from taking extra bases.
Watching from right field means an overthrow rolls all the way to the fence. That turns a routine out into a runner on second or third. Never stand and watch!
Second base doesn't need coverage on this play. The throw is going to first, so that's where the backup needs to be.
An overthrow at first goes into foul territory on the right side, not toward center field. You need to be behind first base, not in center.
scenarios.concept: The right fielder backs up first base on every infield grounder — it's a routine responsibility that prevents disaster on overthrows.

scenarios.title: Charge or Play It Safe
scenarios.description: You're in right field. A hard ground ball single gets through the infield into right field. There's a runner on second base who's rounding third. The ball is bouncing toward you with good speed. If you charge hard, you might bobble it but will have a shorter throw. If you play it safe, you'll field it cleanly but the throw will be longer.
options: Charge hard and field the ball aggressively to make the shorter throw home
Hang back, field it cleanly, then make a long throw home
Charge hard but throw to second base instead of home
Let the ball come to you and throw to the cutoff man
explanations: With two outs in a one-run game, you need to be aggressive. Charging the ball saves time and shortens the throw home. A clean aggressive play gives you the best chance to throw out the tying run at the plate.
Playing it safe means a longer throw home, and the runner scores easily. In a close game with two outs, the safe play lets the tying run score without a contest.
Throwing to second base concedes the run at home. With two outs in a one-run game, the only throw that matters is to home plate.
Letting the ball come to you and throwing to the cutoff is fine when the run doesn't matter, but this is the tying run with two outs. You need to be aggressive and try to make the play at the plate.
scenarios.concept: In close games with two outs, charge the ball aggressively — saving a half-second on your throw can be the difference between an out and a run scoring.

scenarios.title: Throw Through or Hit Cutoff
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A line drive single comes right at you and you field it on one clean hop. The runner on first is rounding second. You're playing at medium depth, about 280 feet from home. Your arm is decent but not elite — you can reach third on the fly from here but home would require a bounce. The first base coach is waving the runner to third.
options: Hit the cutoff man with a strong throw so the relay can go to third or home
Try to throw all the way home on one bounce in case the runner goes
Throw directly to third base to try to get the runner
Hold the ball since the runner is going to make it to third anyway
explanations: Hitting the cutoff man gives your team options. The relay man can throw to third if the runner stops, or redirect home if the runner rounds too far. A good cutoff throw keeps all your options alive.
Throwing home on a bounce from 280 feet will be too slow and the ball could bounce away from the catcher. Meanwhile, if the runner stops at third, your throw to home accomplishes nothing.
You might get the runner at third, but a throw that misses goes into the dugout and the runner scores. Hitting the cutoff is safer and the relay can still get him at third.
Holding the ball gives the runner a free base and possibly lets him round third. Always make a strong throw to keep the pressure on the baserunner.
scenarios.concept: When in doubt, hit the cutoff man — it keeps every option alive and prevents the throw from becoming an error that gives up extra bases.

scenarios.title: Ball Down the Right Field Line
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. The batter slashes a hard ground ball down the right field line. It's hugging the foul line and rolling fast toward the corner. There's a runner on first who is already sprinting toward second. You need to get to the ball quickly and make a smart play.
options: Angle your run toward the foul line side so you can field the ball and already be facing the infield for your throw
Run straight at the ball and then spin around to throw after you pick it up
Let the ball roll and see if it goes foul — it's close to the line
Jog toward the ball to save energy for the throw
explanations: Angling toward the line puts your body in position to field the ball and make a throw in one fluid motion. You're already facing the infield when you pick it up, saving a full turn. That saved time can be the difference between a double and holding the runner at second.
Running straight at the ball means your momentum carries you away from the infield. You have to stop, spin, and then throw — that extra time lets the runner take an extra base.
Hoping a ball goes foul is never a plan. If it stays fair and you waited, the runner is rounding third. Always play the ball as if it's fair until it isn't.
Jogging to a live ball in play is unacceptable. The runner is sprinting — you need to match his urgency. Every step you jog is an extra base for the other team.
scenarios.concept: On balls down the right field line, angle your approach so you're already facing the infield when you field the ball — it saves a full turn on your throw.

scenarios.title: Backing Up First on a Throw From Third
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. The batter hits a slow roller to the third baseman, who bare-hands it and fires a long throw across the diamond to first base. There are no other runners on base. You're playing at normal depth and watching the play develop.
options: Sprint toward the foul territory behind first base to back up the throw from third
Stay in right field and watch — it's a routine play
Run toward second base in case the batter tries to stretch for a double
Move toward center field to cover more ground
explanations: Long throws from third base are the most likely to pull the first baseman off the bag or skip past him. Backing up first base on throws from the left side of the infield is one of your most important responsibilities as a right fielder.

A throw from deep behind third base is 130+ feet across the diamond. That's a long, hard throw that can sail or bounce. If nobody backs it up and it gets past the first baseman, the runner takes second or third for free.
The batter is trying to beat out a grounder to first — he's not going to second unless there's an error. Your job is to prevent the error from becoming a disaster by backing up first.
Moving toward center leaves first base completely unprotected. An overthrow rolls to the fence and turns a routine groundout into a runner on second or third.
scenarios.concept: The right fielder must back up first base on throws from the left side of the infield — long throws from third are the most likely to get past the first baseman.

scenarios.title: Cutoff Man or Throw Home Direct
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Bottom of the 8th, one-run game. Runner on second, one out. The batter singles sharply to right field and you charge in to field it cleanly on one hop. The runner from second is rounding third and heading home. You're about 250 feet from home plate. You have a strong arm — you can reach home on the fly from 250 feet, but it won't be your most accurate throw. The first baseman is lined up as the cutoff man at about 130 feet from you.
options: Throw home directly — your arm is strong enough and this is a do-or-die situation
Hit the cutoff man with a chest-high throw so he can relay it home or redirect
Throw to second base to keep the batter from advancing into scoring position
Hold the ball and concede the run since the runner has too big a head start
explanations: Your arm CAN reach home, but a 250-foot throw under pressure is likely to tail, bounce, or miss the catcher entirely. If it gets past the catcher, the batter takes second or third. One bad throw costs you more than the run.

Hitting the cutoff man with a strong, accurate throw gives your team the best chance. The relay can reach home faster and more accurately than your long throw, and the cutoff man can redirect if the play at home is too late. This keeps all options alive.
Throwing to second concedes the tying run without a fight. In a one-run game with one out, you have to at least try to get the runner at home.
Conceding the run without making a throw lets the other team tie the game for free. You fielded the ball cleanly — make a play. A throw to the cutoff man at least puts pressure on the runner.
scenarios.concept: In pressure situations, hitting the cutoff man is smarter than a hero throw — accurate relay throws beat long, risky heaves to the plate.

scenarios.title: Fly Ball With Wind Blowing In
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. A high fly ball is hit to medium-deep right field. Before the game, your coach told you the wind is blowing straight in from right field at about 15 mph. The ball looks like a routine fly at first, but you can see it starting to hang in the air and slow down. There's a runner on third with one out.
options: Play it where you think it was hit and wait for the ball to come down to you
Take a few steps IN from where the ball was hit because the wind will knock it down short
Drift BACK a few steps because wind can be tricky and you don't want it over your head
Charge in hard and try to make a diving catch
explanations: Wind blowing in at 15 mph can knock 20-30 feet off a fly ball. If you stay where the ball was originally hit, it will drop in front of you for a hit. You have to adjust your positioning to where the ball will actually land.

Exactly right! Wind blowing in will hold the ball up and push it back toward the infield. Take a few steps in so the ball comes down to you instead of dropping in front of you. Read the wind and adjust.
Drifting back when the wind is blowing IN is the worst adjustment. The wind pushes the ball TOWARD you, not away. Going back means the ball falls 30 feet in front of you.

Charging in hard and diving is reckless when you can simply take a few calm steps in and catch the ball at your chest. Diving creates a chance for the ball to get past you and the runner on third to score.
scenarios.concept: When the wind blows in, fly balls lose distance — adjust your positioning a few steps toward the infield to catch the ball where it actually lands.

scenarios.title: Cut Off the Extra Base to the Gap
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. The batter drives a hard line drive into the right-center field gap. The ball splits the gap and is rolling to the wall. A runner on first is sprinting hard. The center fielder is also chasing the ball but you're closer. If you get to the ball quickly, you might hold the runner to third instead of letting him score.
options: Sprint to the ball at full speed, pick it up, and fire to the cutoff man lined up between you and third base
Let the center fielder handle it since gap balls are usually the center fielder's responsibility
Jog to the ball since the extra base is already given up
Sprint to the ball, pick it up, and throw directly home to try to get the runner
explanations: Getting to the ball fast and hitting your cutoff man is the smart play. The cutoff man can relay to third to hold the runner or redirect home. Your urgency to the ball saves the extra base — every second counts.
The ball is closer to you than the center fielder. When you're closer, the ball is YOURS regardless of who normally covers the gap. Waiting for the center fielder wastes precious seconds.
Jogging to any ball in play is never acceptable. The runner is sprinting at full speed — if you jog, he scores standing up. Hustle to the ball and you might hold him at third.

Throwing directly home from deep right-center is an extremely long throw. Even with a strong arm, the ball bounces multiple times and arrives too late. Hitting the cutoff man gets the ball to the right base faster.
scenarios.concept: On gap hits, the closest outfielder sprints to the ball and hits the cutoff man — urgency prevents extra bases and keeps runners from scoring.

scenarios.title: Charge the Shallow Fly for the Throw
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Top of the 9th, runner on third, one out, tied game. The batter lofts a shallow fly ball to medium-right field — deep enough that the runner on third will tag up. You're charging in and you know this will be close. If you catch it, the runner will tag and try to score the winning run. You need to decide how to position your body WHILE catching the ball.
options: Catch the ball on your glove side while running in, crow-hop, and fire home in one motion
Drift under the ball and catch it standing still to make sure you don't drop it, then throw
Charge in and catch it on the run, but immediately throw to the cutoff man instead of home
Let the ball drop on purpose so the runner can't tag up — play it on the bounce instead
explanations: This is the elite outfield play! Catching on your glove side while moving forward gives you momentum toward home. The crow-hop transfers that momentum into your throw. You catch AND throw in one fluid motion, giving the runner the least possible time.

Stopping to catch the ball kills your momentum. You'll be throwing from a standstill, which gives the runner an extra two seconds to tag and score. In a tie game with the winning run, those seconds are everything.

The cutoff man adds a relay step that eats precious time. On a shallow fly ball, you're close enough to throw home directly. The relay is for deep flies when you can't reach home — not for shallow catches.

Letting a catchable fly ball drop intentionally is risky. The ball could take a bad hop, roll past you, or you might bobble it. You also give up a guaranteed out. A caught fly ball with a throw home gives you two chances for an out.
scenarios.concept: On shallow sacrifice fly attempts, catch the ball on the move with momentum toward home — the crow-hop throw gives the runner the least time to tag and score.

scenarios.title: Throwing on the Run vs. Setting Your Feet
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Bottom of the 6th, runner on first, one out, you're up by one run. The batter slaps a clean single into right field. The runner on first is rounding second hard and trying to take third. You field the ball cleanly on your glove side while moving forward. Do you throw on the run or plant and set your feet?
options: Field it and throw on the run to third — don't let the runner get there
Plant your back foot, crow-hop, and make a strong overhand throw to third
Throw to the cutoff man and let him decide whether to relay to third
Hold the ball — the runner will probably stop at second anyway
explanations: Throwing on the run from right field sacrifices accuracy. The throw to third is one of the longest in the outfield and requires precision. An offline throw lets the runner advance or even score.

Correct! From right field, the throw to third base is long and must be accurate. Planting your back foot and crow-hopping generates maximum arm strength and accuracy. The extra half-second to set up is worth the accurate throw.
The cutoff is for deep flies when you can’t reach the base. On a single fielded cleanly, you have a direct throw to third. Using the cutoff adds relay time the runner will exploit.
Never assume a runner will stop! Holding the ball with a runner in motion is how runs score. Always make the throw and force the runner to earn the base.
scenarios.concept: Right fielders must set their feet for the long throw to third — accuracy beats speed on this distance.

scenarios.title: Corner Carom — Ball Off the Right-Field Wall
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Top of the 4th, no one on, nobody out. The batter drives a line drive that hits the right-field wall at an angle, about 15 feet from the corner. The ball is going to carom off the wall. You need to read the angle to position yourself for the fastest play.
options: Run straight to where the ball hit the wall and wait for it to bounce back
Angle toward the foul line — the ball will kick toward the corner
Stay where you are and react after the ball bounces
Sprint to the wall and try to play it off the wall on the fly
explanations: Running straight to the impact point means you'll be behind the ball after it caroms. A line drive hitting the wall at an angle kicks toward the foul line, not straight back. You'll have to chase it.

Correct! When a ball hits the outfield wall at an angle, it caroms toward the foul line like a billiard ball. By angling toward the corner, you meet the ball where it's going, not where it was. This saves two or three steps and keeps the batter to a double instead of a triple.
Waiting to react gives the batter extra bases. Wall caroms are predictable — angle of incidence equals angle of reflection. Read it early, move early, keep the damage to a double.
Playing a line drive off the wall on the fly is extremely risky. If you misjudge the timing, the ball gets past you and rolls to the corner for a triple or inside-the-park home run.
scenarios.concept: Read wall caroms like a billiard ball — the angle of incidence predicts where the ball will kick. Move to where it's going, not where it hit.

scenarios.title: Do-or-Die — Runner Tagging from Second on a Fly
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Bottom of the 8th, runner on second, one out, tied game. The batter hits a medium-depth fly ball to right-center. You're going to catch it, but the runner on second is going to tag up and try to advance to third. If he gets to third, a sacrifice fly scores the winning run. Your arm is above average. How do you play this?
options: Catch it and throw to third to try to get the tagging runner
Catch it and throw to the cutoff man at shortstop
Catch it and immediately fire home to prevent the run
Catch it and hold the ball — don't risk an overthrow
explanations: Correct! With one out and a runner tagging from second, you MUST try to throw him out at third. If he reaches third, the next batter just needs a fly ball to win the game. This is a do-or-die moment — throw to third with everything you've got. Your above-average arm makes this play.
The cutoff relay adds time and the runner will beat the throw to third. On a medium-depth fly, you're close enough for a direct throw. Cutoffs are for deep flies.
The runner is going to third, not home. Throwing home has no purpose here — you'd be throwing to a base where nothing is happening while the runner takes third for free.
Holding the ball concedes third base. With one out in a tie game, a runner on third scores on a fly ball, groundout, wild pitch, passed ball, or balk. You must try to keep him at second.
scenarios.concept: When a runner tags from second to third with one out in a close game, the right fielder MUST challenge the throw — third base with one out is nearly automatic scoring position.

scenarios.title: Reading the Runner's Commitment — Bluff or Go?
scenarios.description: You're the right fielder. Top of the 7th, runner on first, nobody out, up by two. The batter lines a single to right field. You field it cleanly and look up. The runner who was on first has rounded second base aggressively — he's about a third of the way to third. He could be bluffing to draw a throw, or he could be committed to taking third. How do you read this?
options: Throw to third immediately — he's going!
Throw to second base to keep the batter at first
Pump-fake toward third, then throw to second if the runner retreats
Hold the ball and run it toward the infield, watching the runner
explanations: If the runner is bluffing, you've thrown behind him and the ball could get past third base, letting him score. A committed throw to a base where the runner isn't going is a free advance for everyone.
Throwing to second ignores the lead runner entirely. Yes, it keeps the batter at first, but you've given the lead runner a free look at third without any consequences.

Smart play! The pump-fake forces the runner to commit. If he keeps going to third, you throw there. If he stops or retreats, you throw to second to keep the batter at first. You've used deception to read his intention without risking an error.
Walking the ball in is too passive. The runner will read your hesitation and take third easily. Outfielders must be decisive — make the runner react to YOU, not the other way around.
scenarios.concept: Use the pump-fake to read a runner's commitment before throwing. Force THEM to react to you — a rushed throw to the wrong base is worse than no throw at all.

scenarios.title: Bunt Against the Shift
scenarios.description: You're 3B, shifted right vs a lefty pull hitter. He squares to bunt down the 3B line!
options: Sprint back to 3B position
Stay in the shift
Yell for the pitcher
Charge from where you are
explanations: React and get back! Don't let a bunt beat the shift.
Staying in the shift with a bunt coming gives a free base hit.
Pitcher coverage is limited. YOU make this play.
Charging from the wrong side — you won't get there.
scenarios.concept: Always adjust positioning when the batter shows bunt

scenarios.title: Wheel Play
scenarios.description: You're 3B. Runner on 1st, 0 outs. Batter bunts. Coach called a wheel play — SS covers 3rd.
options: Charge hard (no plan)
Hold position
Charge, throw to 1st
Charge, spin-throw to 3rd
explanations: Charging without a throw target wastes time. At third base, you need to be ready for anything and react fast.
Holding lets the bunt die. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
1st gets 1 out but lead runner advances to scoring position.
On a wheel play, charge and throw to 3rd. Getting the lead runner is worth more.
scenarios.concept: On bunt defense, getting the lead runner is more valuable than the sure out

scenarios.title: Runner on Second — Check Before Throwing
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th, runner on 2nd, 1 out, down 2-1. You're the third baseman. Ground ball hit to you. But the runner on 2nd is edging toward third.
options: Throw to first — get the sure out
Throw to third to tag the runner
Look the runner back to 2nd, then throw to first
Hold the ball and chase the runner
explanations: Throwing to first without checking the runner could let him advance to third — then he scores on anything.
There's no force at third, so you'd need to tag him. He's probably not committed yet.
Smart! Glance at the runner first. If he's going, throw to the shortstop covering third. If he's staying, throw to first. This quick look takes half a second but prevents a free base.
Chasing a runner starts a rundown that gives the batter time to reach base. Make a throw instead.
scenarios.concept: With a runner on 2nd, look him back before throwing to first — don't give a free base

scenarios.title: Slow Roller — Eat It or Throw?
scenarios.description: Top of the 8th, nobody on, 2 outs, up 2-1. You're the third baseman. Slow roller — you charge in and barehand it, but the batter is fast and almost to first.
options: Throw it anyway — you might get him
Eat the ball — don't risk an overthrow
Flip it underhand to first
Sprint toward first and throw on the run
explanations: A rushed throw from deep in the grass is the #1 cause of throwing errors. If the batter is almost at first, a wild throw puts him in scoring position.

Smart! If you don't have a clean throw, hold the ball. An error with 2 outs and a 1-run lead could let the tying run reach scoring position. A single is much less dangerous than a runner on 2nd or 3rd from an error.
An underhand flip from the third base side doesn't have the arm strength to reach first.
Throwing on the run from deep grass is one of the hardest throws in baseball. If the batter is almost there, the risk isn't worth it.
scenarios.concept: If you don't have a clean throw, eat the ball — an error is worse than a single

scenarios.title: Bunt Situation — Where to Move
scenarios.description: Bot 5th, runner on 1st, nobody out, tied 3-3. The #8 hitter is up — he's almost certainly going to bunt. You're the third baseman. Where should you play?
options: Charge in aggressively — be ready to field the bunt
Play normal depth — react after the ball is bunted
Halfway in — compromise between bunt and swing
Stay deep and let the pitcher field it
explanations: When you KNOW a bunt is coming, charge aggressively as the pitcher delivers. The third baseman crashing in on a bunt gives you the best angle to field it and throw to second for the force on the lead runner. Don't wait — attack the bunt.
Normal depth means the bunt dies before you get to it. The runner advances for free.
Halfway is better than deep but not as good as a full crash. When you're confident of the bunt, commit.
The pitcher can field some bunts, but the third baseman crashing has a better angle for the throw to second.
scenarios.concept: When you know a bunt is coming, crash in aggressively — field it and throw to second for the force

scenarios.title: No Outs, Known Bunter — Stay or Crash?
scenarios.description: Bot 4th, runner on 1st, nobody out, tied 2-2. A known bunter steps in. Your first baseman is holding the runner. You're the third baseman. How do you play?
options: Crash in on every pitch — be ready for the bunt
Play in but wait to see if he actually shows bunt
Play normal depth — he might slash instead
Crash in but only after the pitcher starts his motion
explanations: Crashing on every pitch means you're out of position if he swings away. Be smart about when you crash.
Waiting to see the bunt show means you're late charging in. You need to commit.
Playing normal depth against a known bunter means the bunt dies before you reach it.

Crash in as the pitcher starts his delivery. If the batter shows bunt, you're already charging and can field it quickly to get the lead runner at second. If he pulls the bat back and swings, you're caught in no-man's land — but that's a risk worth taking against a known bunter. Time your crash with the pitch.
scenarios.concept: Against known bunters, crash in as the pitcher delivers — time your charge with the pitch

scenarios.title: Power Hitter — Deep or Guard the Line?
scenarios.description: Top of the 9th, nobody on, 2 outs, up 5-4. Their power hitter steps in — he's a right-handed pull hitter. You're the third baseman.
options: Guard the line — a double down the line extends the inning
Play normal — he might go the other way
Shade toward the hole — most ground balls go to short
Play deep — give yourself more reaction time
explanations: In the 9th with a lead and 2 outs, guard the line. A double down the third base line puts the tying run in scoring position. A single through the normal hole keeps him at first — less dangerous. You'd rather give up a single than a double in this situation.
A power hitter who pulls the ball is not likely to go the other way. Use the data.
Shading toward the hole ignores the line — a double down the line is the most dangerous hit.
Playing deep against a power hitter is fine for range, but the LINE is the priority in the 9th.
scenarios.concept: In the 9th with a lead, guard the line — a double is more dangerous than a single

scenarios.title: Bunt Charge
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 6th, runner on first, one out. The batter squares to bunt and drops it about 15 feet in front of home plate. The ball is rolling slowly toward you on the grass.
options: Charge hard, field with bare hand, throw to first
Wait for it to come to you, field with glove, throw to first
Charge hard, field with glove, throw to second for the lead runner
Let the catcher handle it and cover third
explanations: Charging aggressively and bare-handing the slow roller lets you get rid of the ball quickly for the out at first. This is the textbook third baseman play on a bunt.
Waiting lets the batter reach safely. On slow rollers you must attack the ball, not let it come to you.

Trying for the lead runner at second is the ideal play if you can execute it — getting the lead runner is always better. But after charging forward on a bunt, you've lost momentum toward second, making it a very tough throw.
The catcher has to come too far out — this is clearly in your zone. Letting it go means the batter reaches easily.
scenarios.concept: On bunts, the third baseman charges aggressively and bare-hands when needed to get the out at first.

scenarios.title: Bare-Hand Roller
scenarios.description: You're at third base. Top of the 3rd, nobody on, two outs. The batter chops a slow roller that dies on the infield grass about 20 feet from you. It's barely moving and the batter is fast — he's already halfway to first.
options: Sprint in, bare-hand the ball, and make a strong throw across the diamond
Run in, field it with your glove, transfer to throwing hand, then throw
Charge in and try to backhand flip it to the shortstop covering
Hold your position and wait for the ball to reach you
explanations: With a fast runner and a dying roller, bare-handing lets you skip the glove transfer and fire to first in one motion. Every split second counts on the long throw across the diamond.
Fielding with the glove adds a transfer step. On a ball this slow with a fast runner, that extra half-second often means the runner beats the throw.
There's no reason for the shortstop to be involved — the play is to first base. A flip to short just adds a relay and wastes time.
Waiting means the runner reaches first easily. On slow rollers at third, you must come get it.
scenarios.concept: On dying rollers, bare-handing saves the glove transfer time needed to beat fast runners on the long throw to first.

scenarios.title: Long Throw Mechanics
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 4th, one out, no runners on. A hard grounder is hit right at you and you field it cleanly. You're about 120 feet from first base — the longest infield throw on the diamond. The batter is an average-speed runner.
options: Crow-hop toward first to build momentum, then throw overhand with your body behind it
Stand still and rifle it from where you fielded it to save time
Throw sidearm to get it there faster
Lob it high so the first baseman has time to adjust
explanations: The crow-hop builds momentum for the long throw across the diamond. Using your body and an overhand release gives you accuracy and carry. This is how third basemen are taught to make this throw.
Standing still means you're throwing with only arm strength from 120 feet. Without body momentum, the throw tails or bounces, and you risk a throwing error.
Sidearm throws from third base tend to tail away from the first baseman, especially on long throws. Save sidearm for short-range plays.
A lob takes too long in the air and gives the runner extra steps. Even average runners will beat a rainbow throw from third.
scenarios.concept: The crow-hop builds momentum for the long throw across the diamond — your body generates the power, not just your arm.

scenarios.title: Guard the Line
scenarios.description: You're at third base. Top of the 8th, your team leads 3-2. Runner on first, one out. The coach signals you to guard the line. The batter is a left-handed pull hitter who has hit two doubles down the line tonight.
options: Move two steps closer to the foul line and shade toward the bag
Stay in your normal position — you can react to anything
Cheat toward the shortstop hole to take away the ground ball up the middle
Move in closer to home plate for a potential bunt
explanations: In late innings with a lead, preventing extra-base hits is the priority. Shading the line takes away the double down the line from a known pull hitter. A single through the hole hurts less than a double.
Your normal position leaves the line exposed against a proven pull hitter. In a close late-inning game, a double could tie or win it for the other team.
Shading toward the middle leaves the line wide open for the left-handed pull hitter. You're giving him exactly what he wants.
There's no bunt situation here with one out and a runner on first in a one-run game. The hitter is looking to drive the ball, not bunt.
scenarios.concept: In late innings with a lead, guard the line to prevent extra-base hits — a single hurts less than a double.

scenarios.title: Tag Play at Third
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 5th, runner on second, no outs. The batter hits a single to right field. The runner rounds third hard but the right fielder comes up throwing. You receive the throw right as the runner is sliding back to the bag.
options: Catch the ball and sweep a quick tag down low in front of the bag
Catch the ball and tag the runner on the chest as he slides
Catch the ball, hold it up to show the umpire, then tag
Catch the ball and block the bag with your foot
explanations: A quick sweep tag low and in front of the bag is the fastest way to apply the tag on a sliding runner. You meet him before he reaches the base, giving the umpire a clear look.
Tagging the chest on a sliding runner is awkward — the runner is low to the ground and sliding feet-first. You're more likely to miss or apply a slow tag.
Holding the ball up wastes precious time. The runner is sliding NOW — tag first, celebrate later.
Blocking the bag with your foot without the ball in your glove isn't a tag. You could also get hurt in a collision. You need ball-in-glove touching the runner.
scenarios.concept: On tag plays, sweep the glove low in front of the bag to meet the sliding runner before he reaches the base.

scenarios.title: Hot Corner Rocket
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Top of the 7th, tie game, runners on first and second, one out. The cleanup hitter smashes a one-hop rocket right at you. The ball is hit so hard you barely have time to react. You knock it down but it rolls a few feet to your right.
options: Pick it up and step on third for the force out
Pick it up and throw to second to start a double play
Pick it up and throw to first for the sure out
Hold the ball — with runners moving you might throw it away
explanations: Stepping on third base for the force out is the smart play. It's the closest base, you don't need a throw, and you get the lead runner. With two outs, it changes the whole at-bat for the next hitter.
After knocking a ball down and scrambling, a throw to second for a double play is extremely difficult. You'll likely rush it and throw it into center field.
Throwing to first after a knockdown means a long throw while off-balance. With runners moving, an error puts the go-ahead run in scoring position or worse.
Holding the ball with bases loaded and one out lets everyone advance safely. The tying and go-ahead runs both move up. You need to get an out.
scenarios.concept: After knocking down a hard shot, look for the closest force out — stepping on the bag beats a risky throw every time.

scenarios.title: Foul Ball Near Dugout
scenarios.description: You're at third base. Bottom of the 2nd, nobody on, one out. The batter pops up a high foul ball drifting toward the third base dugout. You're tracking it well, but it's carrying closer to the dugout railing. The dugout steps are about three feet away.
options: Track the ball but pull up if you get within one step of the dugout — player safety comes first
Sprint full speed, reach over the railing, and try to make the spectacular catch
Call off your teammates and catch it, planting your feet well before the dugout edge
Ignore it and let the catcher handle all foul pops
explanations: Pulling up too early gives away a free out. You should know your surroundings and catch the ball as long as you can plant safely before the dugout.
Reaching over the railing is dangerous — you can tumble into the dugout and get seriously injured. No single out is worth an injury.
Calling the ball, tracking it, and planting your feet safely while still being far enough from the dugout is the smart play. You get the out without risking injury.
Foul pops near third base are the third baseman's ball. The catcher has to spin around and fight his mask. You have a much better angle.
scenarios.concept: On foul pops, know your surroundings — catch the ball confidently, but never blindly run into the dugout.

scenarios.title: Cutoff From Left
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Top of the 9th, your team leads 5-4. Runner on second, one out. The batter singles to left field. The runner rounds third heading home. The left fielder throws it in, and you're the cutoff man positioned between third and home.
options: Let the throw go through to the catcher — the play is at home
Cut it off and throw home because the throw is offline
Cut it off and look to get the batter who rounded first too far
Check the runner at home, and if the throw is on line, let it go through; if not, cut and redirect
explanations: Automatically letting it go through is risky — if the throw is offline, it gets past the catcher and the tying run scores easily. You need to read the throw first.
Automatically cutting every throw takes away plays at the plate when the outfielder's throw is right on target. You should read it first.

Looking to get the batter at first sounds smart, but with the tying run heading home in the 9th, the priority is always the lead runner. Letting the batter take second is fine if you get the out at home.

As the cutoff man, your job is to read the throw. If it's on line and has a chance, let it through. If it's off target, cut it and redirect to give the catcher a better throw or hold the ball. This is the most advanced cutoff decision.
scenarios.concept: The cutoff man reads the throw — let accurate throws go through, cut off bad ones and redirect to give your team the best chance.

scenarios.title: Bare-Hand the Slow Roller
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 4th, runner on second, one out. The batter chops a slow roller down the third base line. The ball is dying on the grass about 25 feet from you and barely spinning. The batter is fast — he's already three steps out of the box. You need to decide how to field this ball and where to throw.
options: Charge hard, bare-hand the ball, and fire to first base in one motion
Charge hard, field it with your glove, transfer to your throwing hand, then throw to first
Charge in but look the runner back to second before throwing to first
Hold your position and wait for the ball to roll closer to you
explanations: On a dying roller, bare-handing saves the split second of glove-to-hand transfer. You scoop it with your throwing hand and fire to first in one seamless motion. With a fast runner, that saved time is the difference between out and safe.
Fielding with your glove is safer for catching, but the transfer from glove to hand takes too long. On a slow roller with a fast runner, you need every millisecond. The bare hand is the play here.

Looking the runner back is a good instinct, but with one out and a fast batter, you don't have time to check the runner AND throw out the batter. Get the sure out at first — the runner on second isn't going home on a slow roller to third.
Waiting for a dying roller is a death sentence. The ball is barely moving — it might stop completely before it reaches you. Meanwhile the batter is sprinting to first. Attack the ball!
scenarios.concept: On slow rollers, bare-handing eliminates the glove transfer and lets you throw in one continuous motion — every split second counts.

scenarios.title: Tag the Runner at Third
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Runner on second, no outs. The batter singles to left field and the runner rounds third hard. The left fielder throws to you at third base and the runner decides to come back. He's sliding into the bag headfirst. You catch the throw and need to apply the tag.
options: Catch the ball and sweep your glove down low in front of the bag to tag the runner's hands before they reach the base
Catch the ball and try to tag the runner on the back as he slides
Catch the ball, hold it up for the umpire to see, then apply the tag
Stand on top of the bag and wait for the runner to slide into your feet
explanations: On a headfirst slide back to the bag, the runner's hands are reaching for the base. Sweeping your glove low in front of the bag meets his hands BEFORE they touch the base. This is the textbook tag on a headfirst slide.

Tagging the runner's back on a headfirst slide is difficult because his hands reach the base before his back arrives at the tag point. You need to tag the part of his body that arrives first — his hands.
Showing the ball to the umpire before tagging is a waste of time. The runner will be safe by the time you finish showing off. Tag first, appeal later if needed.
Standing on the bag blocks the runner's path and could cause a collision or interference call. You need to let the runner access the base and apply a clean tag.
scenarios.concept: On headfirst slides, sweep the tag low in front of the bag — meet the runner's hands before they reach the base.

scenarios.title: Cut Off the Throw From Left
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Runner on first, one out. The batter doubles into the left field corner. The runner from first is rounding second and heading to third. The left fielder picks up the ball and throws it toward the infield. You're positioned between third base and the left fielder. The throw is coming at you about head height, and you need to decide: cut it off or let it go through to the catcher?
options: Let the throw go through to the catcher — the runner from first might try to score
Cut the ball off and tag the runner coming into third
Cut the ball off and hold it — check all the runners before deciding where to throw
Listen for the catcher's call — he'll yell 'cut' or 'let it go' based on what he sees
explanations: Automatically letting the throw go through could be wrong. If the runner stops at third, the throw sails past the catcher for nothing and the batter advances.
Cutting it off and tagging third could work, but you don't know where all the runners are while facing the outfield. You might miss a better play.
Cutting and holding is safe but passive. If the runner IS going home, you just killed the play at the plate by holding the ball too long.

The catcher can see the entire field — he's watching every runner while you face the outfield. As the cutoff man on this LF throw, your job is to react to his call: 'CUT' means catch it and redirect, silence means let it through. Trust the catcher on all cutoff plays — he commands the relay because he has the only unobstructed view of the whole field. Cutoff man listens; catcher directs.
scenarios.concept: On cutoff plays, listen to your catcher — he sees the whole field and will tell you to cut or let it through based on where the runners are.

scenarios.title: Bunt Defense — Charge and Throw
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Bottom of the 3rd, runner on first, no outs. The batter squares to bunt and pushes it toward the left side — the ball rolls about 20 feet up the third base line. The ball has some pace on it and is rolling right toward you as you charge in. The second baseman is covering first.
options: Charge in, field it with your bare hand, and throw to first for the sure out
Charge in, field it with your bare hand, and throw to second to get the lead runner
Let the ball come to you at your normal depth, then throw to first
Charge in, pick it up, and run toward first base to make a shorter throw
explanations: Charge aggressively, bare-hand the rolling ball, and fire to first for the guaranteed out. The bunt has pace on it so you'll be close enough for a strong throw. Getting the sure out is the smart play on bunt defense.

Throwing to second for the lead runner sounds great, but after charging forward on a bunt, your momentum is going the wrong way for a throw to second. It's a much harder throw and you risk throwing it away.
Waiting for the ball at normal depth gives the batter too much time to reach first. On bunt defense, you must charge in aggressively to have any chance of making the play.
Running toward first base wastes time and makes your throw awkward. A strong throw from where you field the ball is faster than jogging closer and then throwing. Use your arm, not your legs.
scenarios.concept: On bunt plays, charge aggressively and throw to first for the sure out — don't try for the lead runner when your momentum is going the wrong way.

scenarios.title: Hot Corner Line Drive
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Top of the 8th, one-run game. A right-handed power hitter smashes a screaming line drive right at you, about chest high. The ball is on you in less than a second — there's almost no time to think. You're playing at normal depth, about 90 feet from home plate. Your reflexes take over.
options: React with your glove — keep your glove open, centered on your body, and try to snare it
Duck out of the way — self-preservation comes first on a ball hit that hard
Knock the ball down with your body to keep it in front of you, then pick it up and throw
Try to backhand it since the ball is slightly to your glove side
explanations: On a screaming liner, keeping your glove centered and open gives you the biggest target. You react with soft hands and let the ball hit the glove rather than stabbing at it. At 90 feet, you have just enough time to react if your glove is ready.

Ducking gives the batter a free hit and runners advance. Third basemen are taught to keep their glove in front of their body on every pitch. The 'hot corner' requires courage — you'll face hard-hit balls regularly.

Intentionally using your body to block a line drive is painful and unnecessary when you can use your glove. Your glove is designed to catch the ball — your chest is not. Plus a body block might deflect the ball away and you can't make a throw.
A backhand on a ball coming at 100+ mph gives you less glove surface and less control. Center your glove in front of your body with the pocket open — maximum catching area for maximum reaction time.
scenarios.concept: At the hot corner, keep your glove centered and ready on every pitch — react with soft hands and let the ball hit the glove rather than stabbing at it.

scenarios.title: Cover Third on a Steal From Second
scenarios.description: You're the third baseman. Runner on second, one out, and the runner has been taking aggressive leads all game. The pitcher is in the stretch. As the pitcher starts his delivery, you see the runner from second break toward third base — he's stealing! The shortstop yells 'He's going!' The catcher catches the pitch and fires a throw to third.
options: Sprint to third base, straddle the bag, and catch the throw with your glove ready to sweep a tag on the runner's feet
Stay in your fielding position because the shortstop should cover third on steals of third
Run toward the runner to cut down the throwing distance for the catcher
Move behind third base to back up the throw in case it's off target
explanations: When a runner steals third, the third baseman covers the bag. Sprint to third, straddle it so the runner has access to the base, and catch the catcher's throw with your glove sweeping down to tag the runner's feet as he slides in.

The shortstop does NOT cover third on steals — that's the third baseman's responsibility. The shortstop covers second base on steal situations. Third base is your bag, and you need to be there when the ball arrives.
Running toward the runner leaves third base uncovered. The catcher is throwing to the BASE, not to you in the middle of the baseline. If you're not on the bag, the throw has nowhere to go.
Moving behind third means the catcher's throw has to go past the bag and reach you deeper. That's a longer throw and gives the runner more time to reach the base safely. Be on the bag, not behind it.
scenarios.concept: On steals of third, the third baseman covers the bag — straddle it, catch the throw, and sweep the tag down on the runner's feet.

scenarios.title: The Slow Roller — Charge or Wait?
scenarios.description: Slow roller down the third base line. Spin on the ball — it might stay fair or go foul. What do you do?
options: Let it roll — if it goes foul, you avoid making a play on a foul ball
Charge hard and field it immediately while fair
Charge and knock it foul with your foot if it's rolling toward the line
Wait at the bag for the ball to decide its fate
explanations: Letting it roll hoping it goes foul is a passive play that backfires constantly. The ball spinning toward fair territory often hugs the line or curves back fair. By waiting, you lose the chance to field it cleanly.

Correct. Charging hard and fielding it quickly while it is clearly in fair territory is almost always the right play. A rolling ball with spin is unpredictable — waiting is gambling. Charge, field it quickly, and make your throw from an off-balance athletic position. MLB third basemen make this play at 78% success rate when they commit early versus 41% when they hesitate.
Deliberately kicking a fair ball foul is interference — it's an automatic dead ball and the batter is awarded first base. Never use your foot to redirect a batted ball.
Waiting at the bag while a slow roller dies in front of you is the worst option. You have no throw angle, no momentum, and the batter is already halfway to first.
scenarios.concept: Slow roller: charge hard and field while clearly fair — never wait

scenarios.title: The Bunt Crash — Who Gets It?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st and 2nd, nobody out. Batter squares to bunt. You're crashing hard from third. The pitcher and catcher also break. The bunt is right down the third base line. Who fields it?
options: You field it — you have the best angle and you called it
Let the pitcher have it — he has momentum toward the line
Catcher takes it — he sees the whole field
Whoever gets there first fields it
explanations: Correct. Third base owns the bunt down the third base line. You're crashing with momentum toward the ball and you call it loudly. The pitcher peeling toward that line creates collision risk — verbal communication wins. 'I got it!' called early gives pitcher and catcher time to redirect. On third-base-side bunts, third base has the clearest lane and the best throw angle to first.

The pitcher's momentum carries him toward first base, making him ideal for first-base-side bunts. On third-base-side bunts, the pitcher has to turn back across his momentum to field it — awkward angle, late throw. Third base takes these.

The catcher handles bunt plays in front of the plate and on the first-base side — not down the third-base line. Catcher direction on bunts: 'Third! Third!' is communication, not a fielding assignment.
'Whoever gets there first' creates collisions and dropped balls. Bunt defense coverage zones are pre-assigned. Verbal communication backs up the zone assignments.
scenarios.concept: Third-base-line bunts: 3B owns it — call it loudly and early

scenarios.title: Guarding the Line — When?
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 7th, you're leading by 1, runner on 1st, 1 out. Do you guard the third base line?
options: Yes — guard the line in this late/close situation to prevent a double
No — play normal depth, doubles are less common than grounders
Shift toward second base to improve DP range
Play at the edge of the grass for the bunt
explanations: Correct. Guarding the third base line in innings 7-9 while leading by 1-2 runs is textbook late-game positioning. A double down the line scores the tying run easily — preventing 0.4 extra base hits per game is worth the 8% increase in singles through the hole. Per MLB defensive data, line-guarding in this exact scenario (late, 1-run lead, runner on) is the correct call. The run that ties or wins the game is your priority, not the average play.

Playing normal depth treats a late/close 1-run lead the same as a random inning. The strategic context changes the optimal position. This is exactly the scenario where guarding the line is justified — a double ties the game, a single through the hole still gives the runner only first.

Shifting toward second base in a 1-run game abandons your line protection and gives the offense an extra base hit lane. DP range matters more with a runner on in neutral innings — here, preventing the go-ahead run is the priority.
Playing at the grass edge for a bunt makes sense with runners on 1st and 2nd with nobody out. With a runner on 1st and 1 out, a bunt is uncommon — guard the line.
scenarios.concept: Guard the third base line in innings 7-9 with a 1-2 run lead

scenarios.title: The Tag Play at Third
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st and 2nd. Ground ball to short. SS throws to second for the force. Runner from 2nd rounds third and the second baseman fires to you. Is this a force or a tag?
options: Tag play — the force at second removed the force at third
Force play — runners were on 1st and 2nd so all bases are forced
Either way — I'll just touch the bag to be safe
Tag play only if the runner stops — otherwise touch the bag
explanations: Correct. When the force out is recorded at second base, the force condition at third base is removed. The runner from second is no longer forced to advance — he could have stopped at third. Your throw from second base is now a TAG situation. You must apply the tag. Touching the bag without tagging a runner who isn't forced is not an out. This is one of the most common base-running rule errors in youth baseball.

The force at third existed when both 1st and 2nd were occupied — but the moment the 2nd base force out was recorded, the trailing runner no longer has a runner behind him pushing him forward. Force removed = tag required.
'Touch the bag to be safe' will cost you this out. The umpire sees a runner who is not forced — they will call him safe if you touch the bag but don't tag him. Always know your force status.
The force/tag distinction has nothing to do with whether the runner stops. It depends entirely on whether the runner behind him was retired. Force out at 2nd = force removed at 3rd, period.
scenarios.concept: Force at 2nd removed = tag play at third, not force

scenarios.title: Third Base Leads — Coaching the Runner
scenarios.description: You're playing third base. Runner on 2nd, 1 out. Deep fly ball to center. Runner is tagging up. The throw is coming to you from the cutoff. What do you do?
options: Catch the cutoff throw and apply the tag if the runner is coming home
Let the throw go through to home — don't cut it
Redirect the throw back to second base
Block the base path and let the runner crash into you
explanations: Correct. As the cutoff position in the tag-up situation, you receive the throw and read the play. If the runner has already committed home, let it go and the catcher handles it — or cut and re-throw home if the throw won't make it. If the runner hesitates or stops at third, you apply the tag. Reading the runner and making a decision is the job. Cutting at the wrong moment lets a run score; not cutting on a dying throw also lets a run score.

Letting every throw go through removes your decision-making entirely and eliminates any chance to cut a ball that won't reach home. Cutoff men exist precisely because throws from the outfield aren't always perfect.

Redirecting to second makes sense only if the original runner (from 2nd) stops at third and there's a chance to double up another runner. With just one runner, redirect to second accomplishes nothing.
Blocking the base path is obstruction. You are a fielder, not a physical obstacle. Present the tag — don't block.
scenarios.concept: As cutoff at third: read the runner and decide to cut or let it through

scenarios.title: Stealing Second
scenarios.description: You're on 1st, 2-1 count. Pitcher is slow to the plate, catcher has a weak arm. You've got speed.
options: Go! Steal on the next pitch
Wait for a better count
Stay — let the batter work
Take a bigger lead but don't go
explanations: Perfect conditions: slow pitcher, weak catcher, hitter's count. MLB runners succeed 85%+ here.
You might not get a better spot. All signs point to GO.
The pitcher is slow, the catcher has a weak arm, and you have speed — this is your best opportunity to steal.
Indecisive! If you take a bigger lead, the pitcher throws over.
scenarios.concept: Steal when conditions align: slow pitcher, weak catcher, hitter's count

scenarios.title: Tag Up Deep Fly
scenarios.description: You're on 3rd, 1 out. Deep fly to center. Routine catch. Tie game, 8th inning.
options: Tag up and score after the catch
Go halfway
Stay on 3rd
Break for home immediately
explanations: Deep fly, 1 out — textbook tag up. The throw from deep center is long.
Halfway on a routine catch = can't score from there.
A deep fly with 1 out? That's your chance for the go-ahead run.
Leaving early = out on appeal. Wait for the catch!
scenarios.concept: Always tag up on deep fly balls from 3rd with less than 2 outs

scenarios.title: Ball in the Dirt
scenarios.description: You're on 1st, 2 outs. Batter swings and misses, pitch bounces away from catcher.
options: Sprint to 2nd!
Wait for the catcher
Stay at 1st
Only go if it hits backstop
explanations: Run! With 2 outs, go on anything in the dirt. Catcher has to find, pick up, and throw.
Hesitation kills. By the time you decide, the catcher has recovered.
Having 2 outs makes advancing MORE important.
Any ball away from the catcher is your chance.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs, always advance on balls in the dirt

scenarios.title: First-to-Third
scenarios.description: You're on 1st, 1 out. Clean single to right. Right fielder has a strong arm.
options: Round 2nd and read the coach
Sprint to 3rd no matter what
Stop at 2nd
Watch the throw first
explanations: Round 2nd aggressively and trust your 3rd base coach. He sees everything.
Running blind into a strong arm gets you thrown out.
Too conservative. At 3rd you score on a sac fly or wild pitch.
By the time you watch the throw, it's too late.
scenarios.concept: Round bases aggressively and trust your 3rd base coach

scenarios.title: Delayed Steal
scenarios.description: You're on 2nd. Middle infielders aren't covering the bag. Catcher lazily throwing back to pitcher.
options: Go! Break when catcher throws back
Don't — stealing 3rd is too risky
Wait for a passed ball
Only go on the coach's signal
explanations: Nobody covers, catcher isn't paying attention. Baseball IQ beats speed here.
Normally risky, but nobody is covering. Risk drops dramatically.
Waiting for a passed ball is passive — this moment of sloppy defense is YOUR chance to steal third.
Smart runners see opportunities the coach might miss.
scenarios.concept: The delayed steal exploits defensive inattention — awareness over pure speed

scenarios.title: Two Outs, Run on Contact
scenarios.description: You're on 2nd, 2 outs. Sharp grounder to SS — tough play, he has to charge it.
options: Sprint home!
Wait to see if SS fields it
Go halfway
Stay at 2nd
explanations: Run! With 2 outs, always run on contact. SS has a tough play AND has to throw to 1st.
With 2 outs, hesitating wastes the advantage — the pitcher can't force you at any base, so run!
Halfway with 2 outs makes no sense.
NEVER assume a routine play with 2 outs.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs, ALWAYS run on contact — nothing to lose

scenarios.title: Reading the Pitcher
scenarios.description: You notice the pitcher's shoulder dips before a pickoff but stays level when going home. You're on 1st.
options: Use the tell — go when shoulder stays level
Ignore it, might be a decoy
Share with dugout but don't go
Wait for the steal sign
explanations: Reading a pitcher's tell gives you a massive jump. The best base stealers do this.
A consistent tell is very reliable. Trust your eyes.
Good to share, but acting NOW helps your team right now.
You have better info than the coach here.
scenarios.concept: Reading the pitcher's tells gives a huge advantage on the basepaths

scenarios.title: Running Out a Double
scenarios.description: You hit one in the gap. Definite single. Ball is still rolling as you round 1st.
options: Peek at the ball while running
Full sprint to 2nd, then look up
Round 1st and decide
Sprint all the way to 3rd
explanations: Peeking breaks your stride and momentum — sprint full speed and trust your coach to guide you.
Run full speed to 2nd, then read your 3rd base coach.
Deciding at 1st is too early and slows you down.
Going to 3rd without reading is reckless.
scenarios.concept: On extra base hits, sprint full speed and let the coach guide you

scenarios.title: Freeze on a Line Drive
scenarios.description: You're on 2nd. Screaming line drive toward shortstop. It might be caught.
options: FREEZE!
Sprint to 3rd
Go halfway
Break back to 2nd
explanations: On line drives, FREEZE. If caught, you're safe at 2nd. If it drops, advance.
Sprinting on a line drive = doubled off. The #1 baserunning mistake.
Even halfway is dangerous on a liner.
Breaking back means you can't advance if it's a hit.
