options: Infield in — must cut off the tying run
Normal depth — the force play at first is all you need
Infield halfway — still protect against the run but keep range
Corners in, middle back — cover all the options
explanations: Infield in with 2 outs is almost never correct. Here's why: with 2 outs, ANY out ends the inning. You don't need to get the runner at home — you just need one out at first base. Normal depth gives you the maximum range to field the groundball and throw to first. The runner at third is irrelevant because the force play at first ends the inning regardless.

Correct! With 2 outs, the defense only needs ONE out to end the inning. That out can come at first base on any groundball. Normal depth maximizes your range and throwing accuracy. The runner on third doesn't matter because if you get the batter out at first, the inning is over and the run doesn't score. Infield in reduces your range and makes it HARDER to get the out you need. The only time infield in with 2 outs makes sense is if you're playing a bunt defense — but nobody bunts with 2 outs.
Halfway still sacrifices range. With 2 outs, commit to normal depth for maximum range. Simple and effective.
Split alignments add confusion. With 2 outs, every fielder should be at normal depth for maximum range.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs, the run on third doesn't matter — normal depth and get the out at first to end the inning

scenarios.title: Win Probability — Tie Game in the 9th, Sacrifice or Swing Away?
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 9th, tie game. Leadoff man reaches on a single. Your #2 hitter is up — a good contact hitter who rarely strikes out. Do you sacrifice bunt him to second?
options: Sacrifice bunt — move the winning run to second with one out
Let him swing away — he's a good hitter, don't give away outs
Hit and run — move the runner and put pressure on the defense
Steal second — don't risk an out at the plate, get the runner in scoring position for free
explanations: Bunting drops your win probability from about 64% to roughly 59%. You're trading an out to move a runner 90 feet — but that out is more valuable than the base. With a runner on 1st and 0 outs, you have multiple chances to drive him in. With a runner on 2nd and 1 out, you need a hit. The math consistently shows bunting in tie games costs you wins.

Correct! Win Probability Added (WPA) research shows that runner on 1st with 0 outs in a tie game gives you about a 64% chance of winning. After a successful sacrifice (runner on 2nd, 1 out), it drops to about 59%. Your good contact hitter is more likely to get a hit, walk, or advance the runner than a bunt gains. Outs are precious in the 9th — you only get three, and giving one away for 90 feet of baserunning rarely helps.
Hit and run is aggressive and can backfire if the batter misses or lines out, turning a double play. It's not bad, but the highest win probability comes from simply letting a good hitter hit.
Stealing is risky — a caught stealing drops your win probability to about 40%. Even with a fast runner, the risk-reward ratio doesn't justify it when you have 0 outs and a good hitter up.
scenarios.concept: In a tie game in the 9th with 0 outs, don't sacrifice bunt — outs are too precious, and the math shows your win probability drops after a successful bunt

scenarios.title: Win Probability — Down 1 in the 8th, Use Your Closer?
scenarios.description: You're down 3-4 in the bottom of the 8th. Your offense is about to bat. Your closer is available and fresh. The other team's 3-4-5 hitters are due up in the top of the 9th. Do you save your closer for the 9th if you tie it up, or use him now in a non-save situation?
options: Save the closer for the 9th — he only pitches in save situations
Use the closer in the 9th regardless — their best hitters are up, keep the game within reach
Use a middle reliever in the 9th — the closer needs to be fresh for extra innings
Match up: use your best lefty for their lefty #3 hitter, then bring the closer for #4 and #5
explanations: Saving the closer for a save situation that might never come is results-oriented thinking. If you don't keep the game close in the 9th, you might never get the chance to tie it up. Your best pitcher should face their best hitters at the most critical moment.

Correct! Win probability analysis shows that keeping the deficit to 1 run in the 9th gives you about a 28% chance of winning. Letting it grow to 2+ runs drops that to around 12%. Using your closer against their heart-of-the-order in the 9th — even though it's not a 'save situation' — maximizes your chance of keeping the game within reach. The traditional 'save the closer for the save' approach loses more games than it wins. High-leverage moments matter more than labels.
A middle reliever against their 3-4-5 hitters is a mismatch. Your win probability depends on keeping the game close. Use your best arm when the game is on the line.

Matchup pitching in the 9th when you're down is overthinking it. Your closer is your best pitcher — let him face all three and minimize damage. Managing by matchups works when you're ahead, but when you're behind, you need maximum outs with minimum runs.
scenarios.concept: Use your best pitcher in the highest-leverage moment, not just in 'save situations' — win probability doesn't care about traditional closer roles

scenarios.title: Win Probability — Intentional Walk to Load the Bases?
scenarios.description: Top of the 7th, you're up 5-4. Runners on 2nd and 3rd, 1 out. Their cleanup hitter is at the plate — he's 2-for-3 today with a double. The #5 hitter behind him is 0-for-3 with 2 strikeouts. Do you intentionally walk the cleanup hitter to load the bases?
options: Walk him — load the bases to set up the force at every base and face the weaker hitter
Pitch to him carefully — don't put the go-ahead run on base
Walk him and bring in a new pitcher to face #5
Pitch around him — nothing to hit, but don't intentionally walk him
explanations: Correct! Loading the bases sets up a force play at every base, including home. This means a groundball to any infielder can get an out at home or start a double play. The #5 hitter has 2 strikeouts today, and with the bases loaded, any out ends the inning with your lead intact. Win probability actually goes UP slightly when you walk a hot hitter to face a cold one — the force at home plate is worth more than the extra baserunner.
Pitching 'carefully' to a hitter who's 2-for-3 with a double is how you give up a 3-run triple. He's locked in today. The risk of him driving in 2 runs is much higher than the risk of walking him.
Walking him AND changing pitchers is too disruptive. Your current pitcher has been fine against everyone except the cleanup hitter. Let him face the struggling #5 hitter.
Pitching around him risks a wild pitch that scores a run, or an accidental strike that he crushes. If you've decided not to let him beat you, commit to the walk — half-measures are the worst option.
scenarios.concept: Loading the bases to set up force plays and face a weaker hitter can increase your win probability — the force at home is worth the extra baserunner

scenarios.title: Pitch Calling — Full Count
scenarios.description: Runner on 2nd, 2 outs, full count 3-2. The batter has struck out twice on breaking balls today. What do you call?
options: Fastball — don't walk him
Slider low and away — he's been chasing
Changeup — keep him guessing
Curveball in the dirt — try to get him to chase
explanations: A fastball on 3-2 is predictable. Good hitters sit fastball in this count.
He's struck out twice on breaking balls — go back to the well. Make him prove he can lay off.
A changeup that misses is ball four. Too risky with a runner in scoring position.
Curveball in the dirt on 3-2 risks a wild pitch advancing the runner to 3rd on ball four.
scenarios.concept: Use a hitter's weakness even in full counts — pattern recognition wins

scenarios.title: Blocking a Pitch in the Dirt
scenarios.description: Runner on 3rd, 1 out, tie game. Your pitcher just threw a curveball in the dirt. How do you block it?
options: Drop to both knees and center your body on the ball
Reach out and try to catch it with your mitt
Stay standing and swipe at it
Turn sideways to create a wider wall
explanations: Textbook! Drop to knees, tuck chin, center body on ball, keep it in front. Runner stays at 3rd.
Trying to backhand a ball in the dirt often leads to it squirting away. Block first, catch second.
Staying standing leaves a huge gap underneath. The ball will roll to the backstop.
Turning sideways can deflect the ball unpredictably. Square up and smother it.
scenarios.concept: Block first, catch second — keep your body centered behind the ball in the dirt

scenarios.title: Steal Attempt at 2nd
scenarios.description: Fast runner on 1st takes off for 2nd on the pitch. Fastball, good location. What's your play?
options: Fire to 2nd immediately
Pump fake, then throw
Hold the ball — don't risk a wild throw
Throw to the pitcher for a rundown
explanations: Quick transfer, strong throw to 2nd! A 1.9-second pop time gives you the best chance to nail him.
Pump fakes work in football. In baseball they just waste time — the runner is already sliding.
Holding lets him take 2nd for free. Trust your arm.
The runner is already at 2nd by the time the pitcher gets involved. Throw through to the bag.
scenarios.concept: Quick transfer and pop time under 2.0 seconds is the key to throwing out runners

scenarios.title: Framing a Borderline Pitch
scenarios.description: Your pitcher just threw a fastball that caught the outside corner — barely. It could go either way. How do you frame it?
options: Hold your glove completely still right where you caught it
Pull the mitt toward the center of the zone subtly
Stab at the ball dramatically to show it was a strike
Stand up immediately — show confidence it was a strike
explanations: Holding still is solid framing, especially in high-leverage situations. But on a routine borderline pitch like this, a subtle pull can help sell it without being obvious.

On a borderline pitch in a normal count, the best framers 'stick and pull' — catch it cleanly and gently bring it toward the zone. Studies show this adds 20+ called strikes per season. The subtle pull works best when the umpire isn't hyper-focused on your glove.
Stabbing is obvious and umpires hate it. It screams 'I know that was a ball.'
Standing up tells the umpire you're already moving on — but it can also look like you're lobbying.
scenarios.concept: Elite framing is subtle — catch, stick, and gently guide toward the zone

scenarios.title: Pitch Calling — Leadoff Hitter
scenarios.description: Top of the 1st inning, nobody on. Leadoff hitter is a contact guy who slaps singles. What's your game plan?
options: Start with a first-pitch fastball for a strike
Curveball to surprise him
Pitch around him — he's not a power threat
Changeup low to induce a groundball
explanations: Get ahead! First-pitch strikes put the at-bat in the pitcher's control. 0-1 counts favor the pitcher significantly.
Risky to start with off-speed — if he's patient, you're behind 1-0 immediately.
Never pitch around a leadoff hitter. Don't put a free runner on base.
Changeups are great — but on the first pitch, the hitter doesn't have a timing reference yet.
scenarios.concept: Getting strike one against leadoff hitters sets the tone for the entire inning

scenarios.title: Pop-Up Coverage
scenarios.description: High pop-up near home plate! It's drifting toward the 1st base dugout. Whose ball is it?
options: It's the catcher's ball — rip off your mask and get under it
Let the first baseman take it — he has a better angle
Call for the pitcher to catch it
Wait and see who gets there first
explanations: That's your ball! Toss your mask AWAY from the play, turn your back to the field, and track the spin. Catchers own all pop-ups near home.
The first baseman would be running away from the ball. You're already facing the right direction.
Pitchers rarely catch pop-ups. This is the catcher's domain.
Waiting causes collisions and dropped balls. Someone has to take charge — and that's you.
scenarios.concept: Catchers own pop-ups near home — toss mask away, turn to the infield, and take charge

scenarios.title: Squeeze Play Defense
scenarios.description: Runner on 3rd, 1 out, close game. You notice the batter's hands shift on the bat and the runner on third is leaning hard. You suspect a squeeze play is coming on the next pitch.
options: Call for a pitchout to catch the runner
Charge the bunt and fire home
Set up normally and react to whatever happens
Signal the pitcher to throw high and tight — bust the squeeze
explanations: A pitchout can catch the runner, but if the batter doesn't bunt, you've just thrown a ball for no reason. It also doesn't make the bunt harder if they do squeeze.
If you charge before the bunt, you leave home plate uncovered. You need to stay back.
Reacting is too passive when you've spotted the squeeze coming. You have the advantage of reading it early — use it.
High and tight makes the bunt almost impossible. The runner is committed and dead to rights. This is how you kill a squeeze — a pitch up and in is nearly unbuntable.
scenarios.concept: The best squeeze defense is pitching high and inside — it's nearly impossible to bunt

scenarios.title: Pitch Calling — Power Hitter
scenarios.description: Cleanup hitter, known for pulling inside pitches. Count is 1-1. How do you attack him?
options: Fastball inside — challenge him
Slider away — make him reach
Changeup inside — he'll pull it foul
Curveball right down the middle — surprise him
explanations: He thrives on inside pitches. Don't feed his strength.
Work the outer half! If he pulls everything, make him beat you the other way. Most power hitters are weakest on pitches away.
Inside changeup still gives him something to turn on.
Hanging a curve over the plate to a power hitter? That's a souvenir in the stands.
scenarios.concept: Pitch away from a pull hitter's strength — make them beat you the opposite way

scenarios.title: Wild Pitch — Ball to the Backstop
scenarios.description: Runner on 2nd, 1 out, tie game in the 7th. Your pitcher's slider bounces in the dirt and skips past you to the backstop. What do you do first?
options: Look at the runner to see if he's going to 3rd
Sprint to the ball immediately — retrieve it first
Yell for the pitcher to cover home
Point at the ball so the pitcher can get it
explanations: Never look at the runner before getting the ball! Every split-second you waste watching the runner is a step closer he gets to scoring.
Go to the ball FIRST, then look at the runner. The fastest recovery wins — you can't make any play without the ball in your hand.
The pitcher should cover home, but that's his read — your job is to retrieve the ball as fast as possible.
Pointing wastes time. The pitcher already heard the ball hit the backstop. Sprint to it yourself.
scenarios.concept: On a wild pitch, go to the ball FIRST — you can't make any play without the ball in your hand

scenarios.title: Pitch Calling — Two Strikes
scenarios.description: Count is 0-2. The batter has been late on fastballs all night. Runner on 1st. What do you call?
options: Fastball up and in — he can't catch up to it
Waste a pitch in the dirt, then come back with heat
Slider on the outside corner — finish him
Changeup — change his eye level
explanations: He's late on fastballs, but don't groove one. 0-2 gives you room to expand.
Perfect! Waste one to expand the zone, THEN finish him. You have two strikes to play with — use that advantage.
Going right for the corner on 0-2 is a mistake if you miss back over the plate.
Changeup is fine but you want to set up the put-away pitch with a ball first.
scenarios.concept: On 0-2, waste a pitch to expand the zone — then go for the strikeout

scenarios.title: Communication — Positioning
scenarios.description: Left-handed pull hitter at the plate. Your infielders haven't shifted. What do you do?
options: Nothing — fielders should know their positioning
Signal the infield to shift toward the pull side
Call time and physically walk out to reposition them
Yell positioning adjustments from behind the plate
explanations: As catcher, YOU are the field general. You can see the whole field — take charge.
Subtle signals might not be seen or understood in-game.
Calling time slows the game and tips off the batter about the upcoming shift.
Catchers are the field generals! Quick verbal calls to shift the defense are your job. You see the whole field.
scenarios.concept: The catcher is the field general — direct defensive positioning from behind the plate

scenarios.title: Passed Ball Prevention
scenarios.description: Runner on 2nd, 2 outs, pitcher's count 1-2. Your pitcher wants to throw a hard curveball. How do you set up?
options: Set up normally and react to the bounce
Pre-shift your weight forward, ready to drop and block
Set the target low to keep the pitch down
Move your target up — don't risk a pitch in the dirt
explanations: Reacting after the ball bounces is too late. You need to anticipate.
Get your weight forward and be READY to block before the pitch. Great catchers expect to block curveballs in the dirt. Prevention starts before the pitch.
A low target is fine, but you need body preparation, not just glove positioning.
Moving the target up might cause the pitcher to leave the curve flat — a mistake to hit.
scenarios.concept: Anticipate the block before the pitch — pre-shift weight and be ready for dirt

scenarios.title: Runner on First — Call a Pickoff?
scenarios.description: Top of the 4th, runner on 1st, 1 out, tied 2-2. Your pitcher is ignoring the runner and the runner keeps taking bigger leads. You're the catcher.
options: Call for a pickoff throw to first
Call a pitchout
Ignore the runner — focus on the batter
Throw down to first yourself after the pitch
explanations: When the runner gets comfortable with big leads and your pitcher isn't holding him close, call for a pickoff! Signal your pitcher to throw to first base. Even if you don't get the runner out, it shortens his lead and makes him think twice about stealing.
A pitchout is a ball — it puts your pitcher behind in the count. A pickoff attempt doesn't cost a ball.
Ignoring a runner who's taking huge leads is asking for a stolen base. Keep him honest.
Throwing behind the runner after a pitch rarely works — the runner is usually moving back already.
scenarios.concept: When a runner takes big leads, call for a pickoff — keep him honest even if you don't get him

scenarios.title: Ball in the Dirt — Block It
scenarios.description: Bot 5th, runner on 3rd, 1 out, down 3-2. Your pitcher throws a curveball that bounces in the dirt right in front of you. The runner on 3rd is watching.
options: Try to catch it cleanly
Drop to your knees and block it — keep it in front of you
Reach down and backhand it
Move to the side to avoid it
explanations: Trying to catch a ball in the dirt often leads to it getting past you. If it gets past, the runner on 3rd scores.

Drop to your knees, tuck your chin, and make yourself as wide as possible. The goal isn't to catch it — it's to keep it in front of you. With a runner on 3rd, a passed ball scores a run. Blocking is the most important skill a catcher has.
Backhanding a ball in the dirt is unreliable. If you miss, the runner scores. Block it with your body.
Moving away from the ball lets it go to the backstop. The runner scores easily.
scenarios.concept: With runners on base, block balls in the dirt — drop to your knees and keep it in front of you

scenarios.title: Pop-Up Near the Backstop
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, nobody on, 2 outs. The batter pops up a foul ball drifting toward the backstop. You're the catcher. Whose ball is it?
options: Your ball — catchers take all pop-ups near home
Let the pitcher get it — he's closer
Call off the first baseman — it's your territory
Wait and see who gets there first
explanations: Foul pop-ups near home plate are the catcher's ball. Rip off your mask, find the ball, and call everyone off. You have the best angle on pop-ups behind and around home plate because you're already facing the right direction.
The pitcher should be backing up, not fielding pop-ups behind home. That's your job.
You should call off the first baseman, but more importantly — it's YOUR ball to catch, not just to direct traffic.
Waiting creates confusion and lets easy outs drop. Someone has to take charge, and near home plate, that's always the catcher.
scenarios.concept: Pop-ups near home plate are the catcher's ball — rip off the mask, call everyone off, and catch it

scenarios.title: Batter Keeps Fouling Off Pitches
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, nobody on, 2 outs, up 4-3. Count is 1-2 but the batter has fouled off your last 4 pitches. He keeps fighting off your fastball. What do you call?
options: Keep throwing fastballs — eventually he'll miss
Change speeds — call for a changeup or curveball
Move the pitch location — go inside if you've been outside
Waste a pitch way out of the zone
explanations: If he's fouling off your fastball, he's timed it. Throwing more of the same pitch at the same speed just gives him more chances to connect.

When a hitter keeps fouling off your fastball, he's ON your fastball. Change speeds! A changeup or curveball arrives 8-15 mph slower and completely disrupts his timing. That's how you get the swing-and-miss after a long battle.
Moving location helps, but if he's timed the speed, he'll foul it off anywhere in the zone. Change SPEED, not just location.
Wasting a pitch after a 7-pitch at-bat just adds to the count. Go for the strikeout with a speed change.
scenarios.concept: When a batter is fouling off your fastball, change speeds — a changeup disrupts his timing

scenarios.title: Mound Visit — Pitcher Losing Control
scenarios.description: Top of the 4th, your pitcher just walked back-to-back batters. Runners on 1st and 2nd, nobody out. He looks frustrated. You're the catcher — time for a mound visit.
options: Tell him to calm down and throw strikes
Remind him of the game plan — get a ground ball for a DP
Joke around to lighten the mood, then talk strategy
Yell at him to focus
explanations: Telling someone to 'calm down' rarely works. It often makes them more tense.
Going straight to strategy is fine, but a frustrated pitcher might not hear you if he's still in his head.

A quick joke or light moment breaks the tension and resets his mental state. Then talk strategy: 'Hey, let's get a ground ball — sinker down, double play, we're out of this.' The mood shift followed by a clear plan is the best mound visit.
Yelling at your pitcher destroys his confidence. Build him up, don't tear him down.
scenarios.concept: On mound visits, lighten the mood first, then give a clear plan — frustrated pitchers need a reset

scenarios.title: Wild Pitch Recovery
scenarios.description: Bot 7th, runner on 3rd, 2 outs, tied 5-5. Your pitcher's curveball gets away — wild pitch to the backstop! The runner from 3rd is sprinting home. You're the catcher.
options: Sprint to the ball, pick it up, and throw to the pitcher covering home
Sprint to the ball, pick it up, and dive back to tag the runner
Sprint to the ball and rush a throw home
Sprint to the ball and hold it — the runner already scored
explanations: On a wild pitch with a runner on 3rd, the pitcher MUST cover home plate. Sprint to the backstop, pick up the ball, and throw to your pitcher who should be standing on home plate ready for the tag. This is a practiced play.
Trying to dive back and tag the runner yourself takes too long. By the time you get the ball and turn around, the runner is already home.
Rushing a throw from the backstop to an empty home plate (no one covering) just throws the ball into the field.
Never give up on a play! Even if it looks hopeless, the pitcher covering home gives you a chance to save the run.
scenarios.concept: On wild pitches, the catcher gets the ball and the pitcher covers home — it's a team play

scenarios.title: Batter Peeks at Your Signs
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th, nobody on, 1 out, up 3-1. You notice the batter keeps glancing down at your hand as you set up. He's trying to see what pitch you're calling.
options: Ignore it — he can't really see your signs
Change your signs to a more complex system
Call the opposite of what you show — fake him out
Tell the umpire to make him stop
explanations: Ignoring it gives the batter free information. If he can read even one sign, he knows what's coming.
Changing to complex signs is smart long-term, but right now you can use his peeking against him immediately.

If the batter is peeking, show him one sign and call another. Flash a fastball sign but set up for a changeup. He'll be expecting heat and get fooled by the speed change. Turn his cheating into your advantage.
The umpire may warn him, but the smarter play is to exploit his peeking to your benefit.
scenarios.concept: If a batter peeks at your signs, show one pitch and call another — use his cheating against him

scenarios.title: Runner on Second Sees Your Signs
scenarios.description: Bot 3rd, runner on 2nd, nobody out, tied 2-2. You realize the runner on second might be relaying your pitch signs to the batter. What do you do?
options: Keep your regular signs — they can't really see them
Switch to a series of signs with an indicator
Use your glove to hide your hand better
Shake off every pitch to confuse them
explanations: Regular single signs are easy to steal with a runner on second looking right at your hand. This is a real problem.

With a runner on second, switch to multiple signs with an indicator. For example, show three signs and the second one is the real pitch. Or the sign after a fist is the real one. This makes it nearly impossible for the runner to relay the pitch to the batter.
Hiding your hand better helps, but a runner directly behind the pitcher has a great angle. Multiple signs are the standard solution.
Shaking off every pitch just slows down the game and confuses your own pitcher.
scenarios.concept: With a runner on 2nd, use multiple signs with an indicator — single signs can be stolen

scenarios.title: Pitcher Wants Fastball, You See Danger
scenarios.description: Top of the 8th, runner on 1st, 2 outs, up 4-3. The batter has been sitting on off-speed all night. Your pitcher shakes you off — he wants his fastball. But you've seen the batter tee off on fastballs twice.
options: Go with the pitcher's fastball — he has to be comfortable
Put down the off-speed sign again — trust your read
Call timeout and talk to him on the mound
Signal for a different location — fastball but away from his hot zone
explanations: Just giving in without communicating can cost you a big hit. The pitcher might not know what you know.
Putting the same sign down again when he's already shaken you off creates a disagreement on the mound. Talk about it.

A quick mound visit solves this. Tell him what you've seen: 'He's been sitting fastball — he crushed your heater twice. Let's go changeup and fool him.' Now you're both on the same page. Communication is what great batteries do.
Different location helps, but the batter has been teeing off on the fastball speed. Changing location still leaves him timed on the velocity.
scenarios.concept: When you disagree with your pitcher, call a mound visit — great catchers communicate, not just receive

scenarios.title: Passed Ball vs Wild Pitch
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, runner on 2nd, 1 out, down 4-3. Your pitcher throws a fastball that tails in on the right-handed batter. It's catchable but tough — it nips the inside corner and dips.
options: Stab at it with your glove — try to catch it
Shift your whole body to the ball — receive it with two hands
Just block it — drop to your knees
Let it go — it's too far inside
explanations: Stabbing with just your glove on a tough pitch often leads to it deflecting off your mitt. That's a passed ball and the runner advances.

Move your whole body behind the ball. Shift your feet first, then receive it with soft hands. Catching the ball cleanly prevents the runner from advancing and might earn your pitcher a called strike on a borderline pitch. Body movement first, then glove.
Blocking is for balls in the dirt. This pitch is catchable — block only if you can't get to it cleanly.
Letting a borderline pitch go means the runner could advance on a passed ball, and you lose a potential strike call.
scenarios.concept: On tough pitches, move your whole body first, then receive — don't just stab with the glove

scenarios.title: Pitch Calling With Runner on Second
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. There's a runner on second base who can see your signs. The batter is a right-handed pull hitter with a 1-1 count. Your pitcher has a nasty slider today.
options: Switch to a secondary sign system so the runner can't steal your signs
Keep using regular signs but call for all fastballs
Just use one finger for every pitch and let the pitcher decide
Yell out the pitch name so your pitcher can hear you clearly
explanations: Smart move! With a runner on second, they can read your normal signs and relay them to the batter. Switching to a secondary system keeps your pitch calling secret.
Limiting yourself to only fastballs makes your pitcher predictable. The runner on second will figure this out quickly and the batter will sit on the fastball.
Letting the pitcher decide defeats the purpose of pitch calling. The catcher sees the whole field and the batter's stance — you need to stay in control of the game plan.
Never yell out pitch names! The other team will hear you and the batter will know exactly what's coming. Signs exist for a reason.
scenarios.concept: When a runner reaches second base, switch to a secondary sign system to protect your pitch calls.

scenarios.title: Blocking a Curveball in the Dirt
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on third, one out, and the count is 0-2. Your pitcher throws a sharp curveball that bounces in the dirt about two feet in front of home plate. The runner on third is watching closely.
options: Drop to your knees, tuck your chin, and smother the ball with your body to keep it in front of you
Try to backhand the ball with your glove for a quick transfer in case the runner goes
Stand up and try to catch the ball on a short hop cleanly
Dive to the side to try to snag the ball before it gets past you
explanations: Perfect technique! With a runner on third, your number one job is to keep the ball in front of you. Drop, block, and smother — even if you don't catch it cleanly, the runner can't score.
Trying a fancy backhand with a ball in the dirt is risky. If it gets past your glove, the runner on third scores easily. Block first, worry about throwing later.
Standing up exposes gaps between your legs and body where the ball can scoot through. The blocking position on your knees creates a wall that keeps everything in front.
Diving to the side opens up the entire plate area. If you miss, there's nothing between the ball and the backstop, and that runner is scoring for sure.
scenarios.concept: With a runner on third, blocking is more important than catching — drop to your knees and keep the ball in front of you.

scenarios.title: Pop Fly Behind the Plate
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. The batter pops a ball straight up behind home plate. It's drifting back toward the screen. Your third baseman is running over, and the umpire is right behind you. The ball is spinning and curving toward the backstop.
options: Rip off your mask, toss it away from where you're running, turn your back to the field, and track the ball
Keep your mask on for protection and try to find the ball through the cage
Let the third baseman take it since infielders have an easier angle
Wait for the umpire to tell you who should catch it
explanations: This is textbook! Toss the mask away from your path so you don't trip, then turn your back to the infield because pop flies behind the plate curve back toward the field. You've got the best angle on this ball.
Your mask blocks your vision and makes it harder to track pop flies. Always remove it first, but throw it in the opposite direction of where you're headed so you don't trip over it.
Pop flies behind the plate are the catcher's ball. The third baseman has a harder time tracking a ball moving away from them, but you're already underneath it and can follow it naturally.
Waiting wastes precious time. On pop flies, the catcher takes charge and calls for the ball. You're the captain behind the plate — be aggressive and take control.
scenarios.concept: On pop flies behind the plate, toss your mask away from your path, turn your back to the field, and take charge of the catch.

scenarios.title: Pickoff Play at First Base
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Fast runner on first is taking an aggressive secondary lead after every pitch. He's been leaning toward second and you've noticed he's slow getting back to first. The count is 2-1 on a weak hitter.
options: Call a pitchout on the next pitch to give yourself a clean throw to first for the pickoff
After receiving the pitch, snap throw to first immediately — you've timed his slow return
Signal the pitcher for a pickoff throw to first from the mound since the pitcher has a quicker throw
Ignore the runner and focus on the batter since it's a tied game
explanations: A pitchout helps for stealing situations, but here you want to pick off at first base. A pitchout gives the runner a clue that something is up because the pitch is wide, and he may dive back early.

Great awareness! You've noticed the runner's pattern — slow to get back after his secondary lead. A quick snap throw from the catcher right after receiving the pitch can catch a runner who's lazy getting back.

The pitcher can try a pickoff, but you just noticed the runner is slow getting back after the PITCH is thrown, not during the pitcher's stretch. Your snap throw catches him at the moment he's most vulnerable.
With a fast runner in a tie game, you can't just ignore him. Your job as a catcher is to control the running game. You've spotted a weakness — take advantage of it.
scenarios.concept: Watch runners' habits after every pitch — a quick snap throw from the catcher can catch a runner with a lazy secondary lead.

scenarios.title: Framing a Borderline Pitch
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Full count, two outs, bases loaded. Your pitcher throws a fastball that catches the outside corner just below the letters. It's borderline — could go either way. The umpire hasn't made the call yet.
options: Hold your glove perfectly still right where you caught it and keep your body quiet — let the umpire see the location
Quickly jerk your glove toward the center of the strike zone to make it look like a strike
Stand up immediately and start walking back to the dugout like you know it's strike three
Pull the ball down into the zone since it was a little high
explanations: This is elite framing for a high-leverage moment! Full count, bases loaded, two outs — the umpire is watching your glove like a hawk. Any movement looks like you're trying to fool him. A quiet glove and still body tell the umpire you received the ball exactly where you expected it. In big moments, stillness is more convincing than pulling.
Jerking your glove is called 'stabbing' and umpires hate it. In a bases-loaded, full-count moment, the umpire is extra alert — any sudden movement screams 'I know that was a ball.'
Walking away is disrespectful to the umpire and might cost you calls later in the game. It also makes you look like you're trying too hard to sell it, which backfires.
Pulling the ball down makes it obvious the pitch was high. In a big moment like this, the umpire is watching closely — hold the ball where it was caught.
scenarios.concept: In high-leverage moments, stillness beats pulling — the umpire is watching your glove closely, and quiet presentation is more convincing.

scenarios.title: Mound Visit Decision
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Your pitcher has walked two batters in a row and just threw three straight balls to the next hitter. He looks frustrated and is rushing his delivery. The pitching coach glances at you from the dugout.
options: Call time and go visit the mound to slow things down and settle your pitcher
Signal for a fastball right down the middle — just let him throw a strike
Wait for the pitching coach to make the mound visit since he's looking over
Keep calling pitches and hope your pitcher works through it on his own
explanations: Perfect call! Your pitcher is spiraling and needs a reset. A mound visit slows the game down, lets him take a breath, and gives you a chance to remind him to trust his stuff and focus on your glove. This is a catcher being a leader.

Grooving a fastball down the middle with a 3-0 count might get a strike, but a frustrated pitcher throwing flat fastballs over the plate can get crushed. You need to fix the problem, not put a bandage on it.
The pitching coach might come out, but the catcher is the pitcher's first line of defense. You're right there — don't wait for someone else to help YOUR pitcher. Take charge.
Hoping things get better isn't a strategy. Your pitcher has walked two and thrown three straight balls. He needs help now, and the catcher is the one who gives it. Be a leader.
scenarios.concept: A catcher is a pitcher's best friend — when your pitcher is struggling, visit the mound to slow things down and settle him.

scenarios.title: Defending a Delayed Steal
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on second base, one out. After you receive a routine fastball for a called strike, you casually lob the ball back to the pitcher. Suddenly the runner on second breaks for third as your throw floats toward the mound.
options: Yell to your pitcher to cut the throw and fire to third base immediately
Jump out and sprint toward the mound to get the ball back faster
Do nothing — the ball is already out of your hands so it's the pitcher's play now
Call timeout to stop the play
explanations: Yes! On a delayed steal, the key is recognizing it fast and communicating. Yelling to your pitcher tells him to catch your lob and immediately throw to third. The pitcher is in the best position to make that throw if you alert him.
Running toward the mound wastes time and takes you out of position. Your throw is already in the air — you can't outrun a baseball. Use your voice, not your legs.
It IS partly the pitcher's play now, but the pitcher might not see the runner going. As the catcher, you see everything — it's your job to alert your teammates. Communication wins baseball games.
You can't call timeout while a play is in progress. The runner is already moving, so the umpire won't grant time. You need to react to the play, not try to stop it.
scenarios.concept: Delayed steals catch catchers off guard — stay alert after every pitch and use your voice to direct teammates.

scenarios.title: Bunt Play With Runner on First
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on first, no outs, and the batter squares around to bunt. The pitch is a fastball low and inside. The batter drops a bunt about eight feet up the third base line. Your third baseman is charging hard.
options: Rip off your mask, field the ball if the third baseman can't get there, and be ready to tell everyone where to throw
Sprint past the ball to cover third base since the third baseman left it open
Stay at home plate since the pitcher or third baseman will handle all bunts
Run straight at the ball even though the third baseman is closer
explanations: Great instinct! On bunt plays the catcher is the quarterback. Remove your mask, read the play, and if your third baseman has the ball, yell where to throw it. If he can't reach it, you grab it. Either way, you're directing traffic.

Covering third is important but not your first job here. The shortstop or someone else can rotate to cover third. Your job is to be the closest backup fielder and the one who tells everyone where the throw goes.

Never just stand and watch! On bunt plays, the catcher must get out of the crouch immediately. Even if someone else fields it, you need to direct the play — you're the only one who can see the whole field.
Running at a ball when a teammate is closer creates confusion and collisions. Let the third baseman field it since he's already charging. Your job is to read the play and communicate.
scenarios.concept: On bunt plays, the catcher is the quarterback — get your mask off, read the play, and tell your teammates where to throw.

scenarios.title: Ball in the Dirt, Strike Three!
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Curveball hits the dirt for strike three! With two outs and first base open, you have a split second to react. The batter is already starting toward the dugout.
options: Scramble for the ball and throw to first — the batter can run!
Hold the ball — that's strike three, the batter is automatically out
Throw to third to hold the runner
Appeal to the umpire — you need a ruling before throwing
explanations: Great reaction! With 2 outs and first base empty, a dropped third strike lets the batter run to first. Recover that ball and throw to first immediately — it's a live play!

With 2 outs and first base empty, the batter is NOT automatically out on a dropped third strike. The catcher must either tag the batter or throw to first. This is one of the trickiest rules in baseball!
The runner on second isn't going anywhere — the batter running to first is the urgent play. Get the ball to first base before the batter gets there.
There's no appeal needed — the umpire already called strike three. The play is live and it's YOUR job to complete the out by tagging the batter or throwing to first.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs and first base empty, a dropped third strike lets the batter run — always recover the ball and throw to first.

scenarios.title: Runner on First Complicates It
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Strike three in the dirt! The runner on first breaks for second on contact. But there's already a runner on first base and only one out. Do you chase the ball?
options: Recover and throw to first immediately
The batter is out — the dropped third strike rule doesn't apply with a runner on first and fewer than 2 outs
Tag the batter with the ball before he leaves the box
Throw to second to catch the stealing runner
explanations: You don't need to throw to first! With a runner on first and fewer than 2 outs, the batter is automatically out on strike three regardless of whether you catch it. Throwing to first wastes time.

Exactly right! The dropped third strike rule does NOT apply when first base is occupied with fewer than 2 outs. This prevents the defense from intentionally dropping a third strike to start a double play. The batter is out — now focus on the runner stealing second.
You don't need to tag the batter — he's already out! With a runner on first and one out, the dropped third strike exception applies. Save your energy for the runner going to second.

Good instinct to throw out the stealing runner, but recognize the batter is already out first. The dropped third strike rule doesn't apply here, so the batter is out on strikes. Now you can focus entirely on throwing out the runner at second.
scenarios.concept: With a runner on first and fewer than 2 outs, the dropped third strike rule does NOT apply — the batter is out to prevent easy double plays.

scenarios.title: Tag Play at Home Plate
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on 3rd, 1 out. Ground ball to the shortstop, and the runner on third breaks for home. The throw is coming to you. How do you set up for the tag?
options: Stand in front of the plate, give the runner a clear lane, and swipe tag as he slides
Block the plate with your shin guard and wait for the ball
Stand behind the plate and tag him after he crosses
Catch the ball and chase the runner down for the tag
explanations: Correct! Under the collision rule, you must give the runner a lane to the plate. Position yourself in front of home, catch the throw, and swipe the tag as the runner slides in. You can't block the plate without the ball.
Blocking the plate without the ball is illegal under the collision rule (Rule 6.01(i)). You'll get called for obstruction and the runner is safe automatically.
Standing behind the plate means the runner scores before you can tag him. You need to be in position to make the tag as he arrives, not after.
Chasing a runner wastes time and lets other runners advance. You have the ball and the runner is coming to you — let the play come to you.
scenarios.concept: Give the runner a lane to the plate, catch the throw, and apply a swipe tag — blocking without the ball is obstruction

scenarios.title: Runner Stealing Third Base
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on 2nd, 1 out, count is 1-0. The runner takes off for third on the pitch. Fastball, middle of the zone. What's your play?
options: Fire to third immediately — you've got a chance
Hold the ball — throwing to third is too risky
Fake the throw to third and hold
Throw to the pitcher and forget about the runner
explanations: Throw it! Stealing third is harder to defend than stealing second because the throw crosses through the batter's box, but a quick release and accurate throw to the shortstop side of the bag can nail a runner. Don't give third base away for free.
Holding the ball lets the runner take third without a fight. If you have a clean throw lane, take the shot — catching a runner at third is a huge momentum swing.
Faking doesn't get outs. The runner is already sliding into third by the time you pump fake. Either commit to the throw or don't.
Throwing to the pitcher accomplishes nothing — the runner takes third and is now 90 feet from scoring with only 1 out.
scenarios.concept: Don't concede third base — a strong throw to the shortstop side of the bag can catch a runner stealing third

scenarios.title: Snap Throw to Third Base
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on 3rd, 1 out, tie game. After receiving a called strike, you notice the runner on third has wandered too far off the bag and isn't paying attention. What do you do?
options: Pop up and snap throw to third — pick him off!
Hold the ball and wait — too risky to throw to third
Throw back to the pitcher as normal
Call time and point out the runner to your teammates
explanations: Great awareness! A snap throw to third from the catcher is one of the most exciting plays in baseball. Pop up quickly, throw to the inside of the bag, and your third baseman applies the tag. If the runner is napping, he's out.
Being cautious has its place, but if you see a runner off the bag and not paying attention, take the shot. A pickoff at third is a massive play in a tie game.
Throwing to the pitcher is routine and lets the runner get back to the bag safely. You've spotted an opportunity — don't waste it.
Calling time stops the play and alerts the runner to get back. You'd be giving up your advantage. Strike while the iron is hot.
scenarios.concept: A catcher snap throw to third catches runners who wander off the bag — pop up quickly and throw to the inside of the base

scenarios.title: Intentional Walk Setup
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on 2nd, first base open, 1 out. A dangerous power hitter is at the plate, and the on-deck hitter is much weaker. Your coach signals for an intentional walk. What do you do?
options: Stand up and signal to the umpire for the automatic intentional walk
Stay in your crouch and catch four wide pitches
Stand up immediately and reach way outside — give the pitcher a huge target
Stand up on the first pitch of the at-bat and tell the umpire you're walking him
explanations: In modern baseball, the automatic intentional walk rule means you don't have to throw four pitches. Your coach or manager signals to the umpire, and the batter goes to first. It's faster and eliminates the risk of a wild pitch or hit batter.
Before the rule change, catchers did catch four wide pitches. But since 2017, you can simply signal for the automatic walk without throwing a pitch. It's safer and quicker.
Standing and reaching outside was the old-school way. The new rule makes this unnecessary and removes the risk of a wild pitch that lets the runner on second advance.
You can't just tell the umpire on your own — the intentional walk signal comes from the manager or coach, not the catcher. But you're right that it happens before the at-bat starts.
scenarios.concept: The automatic intentional walk rule lets you skip the four pitches — the manager signals the umpire and the batter takes first base

scenarios.title: Setting Up the Double Play
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Runner on 1st, nobody out, and the batter is a right-handed ground ball hitter. You need a double play. What pitch do you call and where?
options: Fastball down the middle — let him put it in play
Sinker or two-seam low and inside to a righty — induce a grounder to the left side
Slider low and away — get a swing and miss
Curveball in the dirt — try to get the runner going on a pitch in the dirt
explanations: A fastball down the middle might get hit in play, but it could also get hit hard in the air. You want a specific ground ball, not any contact.

A sinker or two-seam fastball low and inside to a right-handed hitter produces ground balls to the shortstop or third base side — perfect for starting a double play. Pitch location and movement down in the zone are how catchers set up twin killings.
A slider away might get a swing and miss, but you want contact — specifically a ground ball. Strikeouts only get one out. A double play gets two.
A curveball in the dirt is risky with a runner on first. If it gets past you, the runner advances to second for free. Focus on getting the ground ball.
scenarios.concept: To set up a double play, call pitches low in the zone that produce ground balls — sinkers and two-seamers to the pull side are ideal

scenarios.title: Catching a Knuckleball Pitcher
scenarios.description: You're the catcher for a knuckleball pitcher. The pitch is dancing and wobbling toward you unpredictably. Runner on 1st, 1 out. How do you handle this?
options: Use your regular mitt and try to squeeze it quickly
Switch to an oversized catcher's mitt and let the ball come to you with soft hands
Stab at the ball aggressively so it doesn't get past you
Set up extra wide to give yourself more room
explanations: A regular mitt makes catching knuckleballs much harder. The smaller pocket means more balls squirt out. Knuckleball catchers use specialized oversized mitts for a reason.

An oversized mitt and soft, relaxed hands are the keys to catching a knuckleball. Don't squeeze too early — let the ball settle into the big pocket. React late rather than early because the pitch is still moving unpredictably until the last moment.
Stabbing at a knuckleball is the worst thing you can do. The pitch is moving randomly, so an aggressive lunge will miss. Soft hands and patience are essential.
Setting up wide doesn't help because the knuckleball moves in every direction. You need to track it all the way in and react with soft hands, not guess where it's going.
scenarios.concept: Catching knuckleballs requires an oversized mitt and soft hands — let the ball come to you and react late, not early

scenarios.title: Back-Pick on a Runner Who Rounds the Bag
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. Single to left field, runner from first rounds second hard and overslides the bag by several feet. The throw comes in to you at home, but no play there. You see the runner scrambling back to second.
options: Hold the ball — the play is over
Fire to second base immediately — catch the runner off the bag
Throw to the pitcher and let him handle it
Fake a throw to second and hold — see if the runner takes off for third
explanations: The play is never over until all runners are safe on a base! Great catchers keep their eyes on every runner after the ball comes in. If someone overruns a bag, that's an opportunity for an out.

Excellent awareness! When a runner overslides second base, a quick throw from the catcher to the shortstop or second baseman covering can catch him before he gets back. This is a heads-up play that great catchers make by staying alert after every play.
The pitcher isn't in position to make this play. You have the ball and a clear throw — go directly to the base where the runner is off the bag.
Faking wastes time. The runner is already scrambling to get back — every second you hesitate is a second he gets closer to the bag. Throw it now.
scenarios.concept: Great catchers stay alert after every play — when a runner overslides a base, a quick throw can catch him off the bag

scenarios.title: Pitcher Tipping His Pitches
scenarios.description: You're the catcher. The opposing hitters seem to know what's coming — they're sitting on off-speed and crushing it, but laying off fastballs. You suspect your pitcher might be tipping his pitches. What do you do?
options: Keep calling the same game plan — eventually they'll miss
Call time, visit the mound, and check if he's tipping (glove position, hand movement, head tilt)
Switch to calling only fastballs until you figure it out
Tell the umpire the other team is cheating
explanations: Hoping hitters will start missing is not a strategy. If they know what's coming, they'll keep crushing it. You need to diagnose the problem immediately.

As the catcher, you have the best view of your pitcher's mechanics. Visit the mound and watch his delivery closely. Common tells include: the glove rising higher before a curveball, the wrist twisting visibly before a slider, or the delivery slowing down before a changeup. If you spot something, tell him to keep his delivery identical on every pitch.
Switching to all fastballs makes your pitcher one-dimensional. Even if the hitters are reading off-speed tells, a pitcher with only one pitch is easier to hit than a pitcher who fixes his tipping.

The other team isn't cheating — reading a pitcher's tells from the batter's box or the base paths is a legal and fundamental part of baseball. It's your job to help your pitcher stop tipping, not blame the other team.
scenarios.concept: Catchers must watch for pitch-tipping — visit the mound and check glove position, hand movement, and delivery tempo for tells

scenarios.title: Jeter's Flip Play
scenarios.description: 2001 ALDS, Game 3. Runner rounding 3rd heading home. The throw from right field sails up the first base line — way off target. You're the shortstop. What do you do?
options: Let the first baseman handle it — it's his side
Cut the throw off and relay home
Sprint to the first base line and shovel-flip to the catcher
Call for the ball and throw home yourself
explanations: The first baseman can't reach it in time. The runner scores easily.
A standard cutoff relay takes too long — the runner beats it home.
This is THE play! Jeter sprinted from shortstop to the first base line, grabbed the overthrow, and backhanded a shovel-flip to the catcher — nailing Jeremy Giambi. Baseball's greatest hustle play.
You're 150 feet away. By the time you field and throw, the run scores.
scenarios.concept: Great plays happen when you anticipate — Jeter's hustle and instinct created the impossible

scenarios.title: Merkle's Boner
scenarios.description: 1908, Giants vs Cubs. Bottom 9th, tie game. Your teammate singles — the winning run appears to score from 3rd! But you were the runner on 1st. What must you do?
options: Run toward 2nd base and touch the bag
Celebrate with your teammates — the game is over!
Run to the dugout — the fans are storming the field
Stay at 1st base — you didn't need to advance
explanations: Correct! You MUST touch 2nd base for the run to count. In 1908, Fred Merkle didn't — the Cubs got the ball, touched 2nd, and Merkle was a force out. The run was nullified.
Celebrating cost Fred Merkle his legacy. The run didn't count because he never touched 2nd.
Leaving the field is abandoning the play. The defense can appeal and you're out.
The force at second still existed because Merkle was forced to advance when the batter hit the ball. You MUST touch second base — without it, you can be forced out and the run doesn't count.
scenarios.concept: Always touch the next base — Merkle's mistake in 1908 changed a pennant race

scenarios.title: Baez's Rundown Magic
scenarios.description: 2021 Cubs vs Pirates. You're Javy Baez on 1st. The batter grounds out but you get caught in a rundown between 1st and 2nd. What's your strategy?
options: Give up and get tagged — save energy
Run full speed toward 2nd to force a bad throw
Stay in the rundown as long as possible to let the other runner score
Make the fielders throw as many times as possible — force an error
explanations: Never give up! Rundowns create chaos. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
Running full speed at one fielder doesn't create enough confusion.
Good thinking — but the key is actively FORCING errors, not just surviving.
Baez made the Pirates throw 6 times, running back and forth, drawing TWO throwing errors. He ended up safe at 2nd AND a run scored. Chaos wins.
scenarios.concept: In a rundown, make them throw — every throw is a chance for an error

scenarios.title: The Hidden Ball Trick
scenarios.description: You're the first baseman. The runner on 1st just took a lead. Your pitcher is on the rubber. How do you execute the hidden ball trick?
options: Keep the ball in your glove and tag the runner when he leads off
Ask the pitcher to pretend to have the ball while you keep it
Wait for the pitcher to step OFF the rubber, then tag the runner
Call time and tag the runner during the timeout
explanations: If the pitcher is on the rubber, this is a balk. The pitcher MUST have the ball when on the rubber.
This is a balk! The pitcher cannot be on the rubber without the ball.
The pitcher MUST step off the rubber for this to be legal. Once off, you can tag the unsuspecting runner. This is the only legal way to pull it off.
You can't tag a runner during a timeout — the ball is dead.
scenarios.concept: The hidden ball trick only works if the pitcher is OFF the rubber — otherwise it's a balk

scenarios.title: Lonnie Smith Gets Deked
scenarios.description: 1991 World Series, Game 7. You're Lonnie Smith on 1st. The batter drives a ball to deep left-center gap. You're running on contact. But as you round 2nd, the middle infielders fake a double play. What should you do?
options: Stop and check if the ball was fielded
Keep running full speed — the ball was hit to the outfield
Hesitate briefly then continue to 3rd
Dive back to 2nd — it might be a line drive out
explanations: Stopping is exactly what happened — Lonnie Smith hesitated, and it cost the Braves the winning run in Game 7 of the World Series.
Never stop running on a gap hit! The ball is clearly in the outfield. Trust your eyes and ears, not the infielders. Smith should have scored easily.
Even a brief hesitation costs critical seconds. That hesitation kept Smith at 3rd instead of scoring.
You saw the ball hit — it was a gap shot. Don't fall for the fake.
scenarios.concept: Watch the ball, not the fielders — dekes only work if you take your eyes off the play

scenarios.title: The Suicide Squeeze
scenarios.description: Runner on 3rd, 1 out, tie game in the 8th. You're the batter. The coach gives the squeeze sign. The runner will break for home as the pitcher starts his motion. What's your job?
options: Bunt the ball ANYWHERE in fair territory — just get it down
Swing away — surprise the defense
Fake the bunt and let the runner steal home
Bunt only if the pitch is in the strike zone
explanations: On a suicide squeeze, the runner is COMMITTED. Your ONLY job is to get the bunt down — anywhere, any pitch, even in the dirt. The runner is dead if you don't bunt.
Swinging means the catcher has a clear throw to nail the runner at home.
A fake bunt leaves your runner hung out to dry — he's already sprinting.
On a suicide squeeze, you bunt EVERYTHING. The runner is already committed. If you take a pitch, he's out by 20 feet.
scenarios.concept: On a suicide squeeze, bunt EVERYTHING — the runner is committed to scoring

scenarios.title: Walk-Off Balk
scenarios.description: Bottom 9th, tie game, bases loaded, 1 out. You're a runner on 3rd. The pitcher starts his windup, then stops mid-motion. What happens?
options: Nothing — play continues
It's a balk — all runners advance one base, you score!
The pitch doesn't count — redo it
It depends on whether the batter swung
explanations: A pitcher cannot stop mid-motion — that's always a balk.
Correct! A balk awards every runner one free base. With the bases loaded and a runner on 3rd, the winning run scores on a walk-off balk! It's rare but it happens.
Balks are not do-overs. The runners advance immediately.
A balk is called regardless of what the batter does. It's about the pitcher's illegal motion.
scenarios.concept: A balk advances all runners one base — with bases loaded, that's a run

scenarios.title: Bonds — Intentional Walk with Bases Loaded
scenarios.description: 1998. Barry Bonds at the plate, bases loaded, 2 outs. You're the opposing manager. The Diamondbacks chose to intentionally walk Bonds, forcing in a run. When might this make sense?
options: It never makes sense — you just gave them a free run
Only if you're up by more than 1 run AND the next batter is much weaker
Only in extra innings
Only if Bonds has already homered in the game
explanations: It's counterintuitive but sometimes the math works. Buck Showalter did it when up by 2.
Exactly! If you're up 8-6 with bases loaded, walking Bonds makes it 8-7 but you face a weaker hitter. A Bonds grand slam makes it 10-8 — game over. Sometimes the intentional walk is the right risk.
Extra innings have nothing to do with this decision — it's about run prevention math.
His earlier at-bats don't change the math. It's about expected runs.
scenarios.concept: Sometimes giving up 1 run to avoid giving up 4 is the smart play — think expected value

scenarios.title: Three Men on Third
scenarios.description: 1926 — Babe Ruth, Lou Gehrig, and a runner are all somehow near 3rd base at the same time. You're the third baseman with the ball. Who do you tag?
options: Tag Babe Ruth first — he's the biggest star
Tag the runner closest to home plate
Tag the runner who was originally on 3rd — they have the right to the base
Tag all of them just to be safe
explanations: Star power doesn't matter in the rules. Tag the right runner.
The runner closest to home isn't necessarily the one you need. Think about who has the right to the base.
Correct! The lead runner (originally on 3rd) has the RIGHT to the base. The other two runners are trespassers. Tag the runners who don't belong — they're the outs.
Tagging all of them works, but you need to know WHY. Only the runners without the right to the base are out.
scenarios.concept: The lead runner has the right to the base — tag the others, they're trespassing

scenarios.title: Maddux Pitch Sequencing
scenarios.description: You're Greg Maddux — the greatest pitch sequencer ever. Bottom of the 7th, facing a power hitter. He's seen mostly fastballs from you. Count is 2-1. What do you throw?
options: Fastball on the inside corner — your best pitch
Changeup — make him way out front
Cutter that looks like a fastball but moves
Two-seam sinker for a ground ball
explanations: He's timing your fastball now. Throwing it again is predictable.
Changeup on 2-1 when behind is risky — if you miss, it's 3-1.
Maddux mastered the cutter for exactly this reason. It looks like a fastball out of the hand, but cuts 3-4 inches at the last moment. The batter swings where the ball WAS. Pure genius.
A sinker could work, but the cutter's deception is what made Maddux legendary.
scenarios.concept: Maddux's genius: throw pitches that LOOK like one thing but DO another — deception over velocity

scenarios.title: The Catch
scenarios.description: 1954 World Series, Game 1. Vic Wertz crushes a deep drive to center field at the Polo Grounds. Willie Mays turns his back to home plate and sprints toward the wall, 460 feet away. The ball is sailing over his head.
options: Sprint full speed with your back turned and make an over-the-shoulder catch
Stop, turn around, and try to play the ball off the wall
Let it go — no one can catch a ball hit that far
Call for the left fielder to take it instead
explanations: This is exactly what Mays did — one of the greatest catches in baseball history. He ran full speed, caught it over his shoulder at the warning track, then spun and fired a throw back to the infield to hold the runners. Pure instinct and elite athleticism.
Playing it off the wall would likely score both runners and give Wertz extra bases. In a tie World Series game, you can't give up those runs if there's any chance of making the catch.
Giving up on the play would be a disaster. Mays had the speed and tracking ability to reach it, and great outfielders never quit on a ball. This drive was catchable for someone with Mays' talent.
The center fielder has priority on this ball, and Mays was already in full sprint. Calling off for the left fielder would cause confusion and almost certainly let the ball drop.
scenarios.concept: Elite outfielders trust their speed and instincts to make plays that seem impossible — never give up on a fly ball.

scenarios.title: Buckner's Grounder
scenarios.description: 1986 World Series, Game 6, bottom of the 10th. The Mets trail 5-3 but have rallied to tie it. Mookie Wilson hits a slow roller down the first base line. You're Bill Buckner at first base, hobbling on bad ankles. The ball is bouncing toward you.
options: Stay down, keep your glove on the ground, and field it cleanly
Try to backhand it while moving toward the bag
Stand up tall to get a better view of the hop
Charge the ball aggressively to cut down the runner
explanations: Fundamentals save games. Stay low, keep your glove down, and smother the grounder. In the actual game, the ball went through Buckner's legs because he lifted his glove, and the winning run scored. This play haunted him for decades, though he was a great player who deserved better.
A backhand on a slow roller adds unnecessary risk. The ball is coming right at you — use two hands and stay square to it. Fancy plays in the World Series with the game on the line are a bad idea.

Standing up tall is exactly how balls go under your glove. The number one rule for grounders is stay low. Buckner's legs were hurting, which may have caused him to rise up, but the fundamental remains: glove on the dirt.
The ball is a slow roller already close to you. Charging aggressively could cause you to overrun it or field it off-balance. Set your feet and make the routine play.
scenarios.concept: In pressure moments, trust the fundamentals — stay low, glove on the ground, field it clean. Routine plays win championships.

scenarios.title: Gibson's Moment
scenarios.description: 1988 World Series, Game 1. The Dodgers trail 4-3 in the bottom of the 9th against Oakland's dominant closer Dennis Eckersley. Kirk Gibson can barely walk due to injuries in both legs. Manager Tommy Lasorda sends him up to pinch hit with a runner on base.
options: Look for a backdoor slider and try to drive it the other way
Sit dead red fastball and swing as hard as possible
Take the pitch — try to draw a walk to extend the inning
Bunt for a base hit since you can barely run
explanations: Gibson studied Eckersley's tendencies and knew the backdoor slider was coming on a full count. He sat on it, got it, and launched one of the most iconic home runs in World Series history — limping around the bases with his famous fist pump. Preparation meets opportunity.

Eckersley's fastball was elite, but he loved his slider in full counts. Sitting fastball would have left Gibson vulnerable to the pitch Eckersley actually threw. Great hitters study the pitcher and anticipate.
With two outs and down a run in the World Series, taking a pitch to try for a walk is too passive. Gibson was sent up there to hit. You don't pinch hit an injured star to watch pitches.
Bunting when you can barely run makes no sense. Gibson could hardly move — a bunt would be a guaranteed out. Lasorda put him in to swing the bat, not bunt.
scenarios.concept: Great hitters study the pitcher's tendencies. Kirk Gibson knew what pitch was coming and was ready for it — preparation is a superpower.

scenarios.title: Koufax's Decision
scenarios.description: October 6, 1965. Sandy Koufax, the best pitcher in baseball, is scheduled to start Game 1 of the World Series against the Minnesota Twins. But today is Yom Kippur, the holiest day in the Jewish faith. Koufax must make a deeply personal decision.
options: Sit out Game 1 to honor your faith, then dominate when you pitch later
Pitch Game 1 — the team needs you and you can observe next year
Ask the manager to move the game to a different day
Pitch but donate your game check to charity as a compromise
explanations: Koufax chose to honor Yom Kippur and sat out Game 1. Don Drysdale started instead and lost. But Koufax came back to pitch a shutout in Game 5 and a 3-hit shutout in Game 7 to win the Series. His decision showed that character and values matter more than any single game, and he became an even greater hero for it.

While the team wanted Koufax, he believed his faith came first. He showed that standing up for your beliefs — even under enormous pressure — is the right thing to do. The Dodgers still won the Series.

Players don't get to move World Series games. The schedule is set. But this option shows good instinct to look for a solution. In the real world, Koufax simply made his choice and trusted his teammates.
While generous, this misses the point. Yom Kippur is a day of complete rest and reflection, not partial observance. Koufax understood that honoring his values meant fully committing to them.
scenarios.concept: Standing up for your beliefs takes courage. Sandy Koufax showed the world that character matters more than any game.

scenarios.title: Jackie Steals Home
scenarios.description: 1955 World Series, Game 1. Jackie Robinson is on third base against the Yankees. The great Whitey Ford is pitching, and Yogi Berra is catching. Robinson takes an aggressive lead, dancing off third, daring the Yankees to stop him.
options: Break for home plate — steal it with pure speed and daring
Bluff a steal to distract the pitcher, then stay at third
Wait for the batter to drive you in with a hit
Take a big lead but retreat when the pitcher checks you
explanations: Jackie Robinson actually stole home in this game — one of the most daring plays in World Series history. Berra argued he was out (the call is still debated today), but Robinson was called safe. His fearless baserunning symbolized his entire career: bold, aggressive, and refusing to be intimidated.
Bluffing is a decent distraction, but Robinson was a man of action. He had stolen home 19 times in his career and wasn't afraid of the biggest stage. Sometimes you have to be bold to make history.

Waiting is the safe play, but Robinson didn't become a legend by playing it safe. He broke baseball's color barrier and played every game with fearless intensity. Sitting and waiting wasn't in his DNA.
Taking a big lead just to retreat is a half-measure. Robinson committed fully to everything he did. His aggressive style forced opponents into mistakes and changed how the game was played.
scenarios.concept: Jackie Robinson played baseball with fearless courage — the same courage that made him a pioneer who changed the game forever.

scenarios.title: The Pine Tar Game
scenarios.description: July 24, 1983. George Brett of the Kansas City Royals hits a go-ahead two-run homer off Goose Gossage in the 9th inning at Yankee Stadium. But Yankees manager Billy Martin comes out arguing that Brett's bat has too much pine tar on the handle — more than 18 inches. What should the umpires rule?
options: The homer counts — pine tar doesn't help the ball travel farther
Call Brett out — the rules say pine tar can't go past 18 inches on the bat
Let Brett re-do the at bat with a legal bat
Eject Brett from the game and award the Yankees the win
explanations: This is what the American League president ultimately decided! The umpires originally called Brett out, and Brett charged out of the dugout in one of baseball's most famous arguments. But the league overturned it, ruling that pine tar is just for grip and doesn't make the ball go farther. The homer counted, and the Royals won when the game was resumed weeks later.

This is what the umpires originally called — and George Brett's furious reaction became one of baseball's most iconic moments. But the rule was about bat specifications, not cheating. Pine tar doesn't help the ball travel farther, so the AL president overturned the call.
You can't re-do an at bat in baseball. Once the pitch is thrown and hit, the result stands (unless there's interference). This isn't how baseball rules work.
Ejection and forfeiting a game over pine tar on a bat handle is wildly excessive. The rules violation was technical, not about cheating or sportsmanship.
scenarios.concept: The Pine Tar Incident taught baseball that rules should focus on fair play, not technicalities. Pine tar helps grip — it doesn't help you hit farther.

scenarios.title: Carter's Walk-Off
scenarios.description: 1993 World Series, Game 6. The Toronto Blue Jays lead the series 3-2 but trail the Phillies 6-5 in the bottom of the 9th. Joe Carter steps up with two runners on base against closer Mitch Williams, who has been wild all series. One swing can end it all.
options: Look for a fastball down and in — Williams has been overthrowing all night
Sit on the slider and try to go the other way
Take the next pitch to work the count full
Try to bunt the runners over for the next hitter
explanations: Carter knew Williams was overthrowing and struggling with command. He sat on a fastball down and in, got exactly that, and launched it over the left field wall for only the second walk-off homer to win a World Series in history (after Bill Mazeroski in 1960). Carter's leap around the bases is one of baseball's most joyous moments.

Williams was primarily a fastball pitcher who was struggling with his slider's command. Sitting slider against a struggling power pitcher in this count was the wrong read. Study the pitcher and play the percentages.

With two runners on, down one run, and the pitcher struggling with his control, being aggressive is the right approach. Taking a pitch hoping for a walk is too passive — Carter was looking to drive the ball.

Bunting with the chance to win the World Series? Absolutely not. Carter was a power hitter in a situation that called for a big swing. You don't sacrifice in the 9th inning of a World Series elimination game.
scenarios.concept: In the biggest moments, study the pitcher's weaknesses and be ready to attack. Joe Carter's World Series walk-off was all about knowing what was coming.

scenarios.title: Ripken's Record
scenarios.description: September 6, 1995. Cal Ripken Jr. of the Baltimore Orioles is about to play in his 2,131st consecutive game, breaking Lou Gehrig's record that stood for 56 years. Ripken has played through injuries, slumps, and exhaustion. The game becomes official after the 5th inning. What makes this achievement so special?
options: Showing up every single day — consistency and dedication are the ultimate skills
Being the most talented player on the field
Hitting a home run in the record-breaking game
Playing despite having serious injuries that should have kept him out
explanations: Cal Ripken Jr. showed that the most important ability is availability. For over 13 straight years, he showed up to work every single day and gave his best. He did hit a homer in the record game, but the streak was about dedication, reliability, and loving the game enough to never take a day off. When the game became official, he took a victory lap around Camden Yards that is one of baseball's most emotional moments.

Ripken was very talented — a Hall of Famer — but the record wasn't about talent. Plenty of more talented players couldn't play every day. It was about his work ethic, preparation, and mental toughness to show up 2,131 times in a row.

Ripken actually did hit a homer that night, which was magical. But the record is about the streak itself — the daily commitment over more than a decade. The home run was the cherry on top, not the main achievement.

Playing hurt isn't something to celebrate — it can make injuries worse. Ripken's streak was about being prepared and healthy enough to play, not about ignoring injuries. He took care of his body to be available every day.
scenarios.concept: Cal Ripken Jr. proved that showing up every day and giving your best is one of the greatest achievements in all of sports.

scenarios.title: Clemente's 3000th
scenarios.description: September 30, 1972. Roberto Clemente of the Pittsburgh Pirates doubles off the wall for his 3,000th career hit — only the 11th player in history to reach that milestone. Tragically, it would be his last regular season hit. Three months later, Clemente died in a plane crash while delivering earthquake relief supplies to Nicaragua.
options: Drive the ball hard into the gap — play your best until the very end
Try to bunt for a milestone hit to make sure you reach base
Wait for a perfect pitch — this moment is too important to waste
Swing for a home run to make the milestone extra special
explanations: Clemente ripped a line drive double into the left-center gap — playing hard and true, exactly how he played every game. He was one of baseball's greatest right fielders, a 15-time All-Star, and an even greater human being. His legacy of using his fame to help others makes him one of the most admired figures in baseball history.

Roberto Clemente would never bunt for a milestone hit. He was a proud, aggressive hitter who played the game the right way. His 3,000th hit came the same way as his first — swinging hard and making solid contact.

Clemente was always aggressive at the plate — he famously swung at pitches outside the zone and still hit them hard. Waiting for a perfect pitch wasn't his style. He attacked and let his incredible bat speed do the rest.

While a homer would have been exciting, Clemente wasn't about showing off. His double was pure Clemente — a hard line drive that showed his incredible bat control. The milestone was special enough on its own.
scenarios.concept: Roberto Clemente played baseball with pride and purpose, and used his fame to help others. He showed that being a great person matters more than being a great player.

scenarios.title: Shot Heard Round the World
scenarios.description: October 3, 1951. The Giants trail the Dodgers 4-2 in the bottom of the 9th of a winner-take-all playoff game at the Polo Grounds. Bobby Thomson steps up against reliever Ralph Branca with runners on second and third. The Dodgers could walk Thomson to load the bases and set up a double play, or pitch to him. Branca delivers a fastball up and in.
options: Turn on the inside fastball and drive it — this is your moment
Take the pitch and look for something on the outer half
Bunt to squeeze the tying run home from third
Try to hit it on the ground to the right side to score a run
explanations: Bobby Thomson turned on Branca's fastball and launched it into the left field stands for a three-run homer that won the pennant.
