Radio announcer Russ Hodges screamed 'The Giants win the pennant!' four times in one of the most famous calls in sports history. It capped an incredible comeback — the Giants had trailed the Dodgers by 13.5 games in August.

With the game on the line, you can't afford to be passive looking for a specific location. Thomson knew he needed a big hit, and when he got a fastball he could handle, he jumped on it. Aggressive approach in big moments.

A squeeze bunt ties one run but doesn't win the game, and it's an extremely risky play with one out. If the bunt is popped up or missed, the rally dies. Thomson needed to swing the bat to make history.

A ground ball to the right side might score one run, but the Giants needed three. Playing for one run when you're down two is playing not to lose. Thomson's aggressive swing gave the Giants everything they needed.
scenarios.concept: In baseball's biggest moments, the greatest players look to drive the ball. Bobby Thomson's homer is proof that aggressive confidence can produce miracles.

scenarios.title: Merkle's Boner — 1908
scenarios.description: September 23, 1908. Giants vs Cubs, bottom of the 9th, tie game 1-1. Al Bridwell singles to center, apparently scoring the winning run from 3rd. The crowd pours onto the field in celebration. But Cubs second baseman Johnny Evers notices that 19-year-old Fred Merkle — the runner on 1st — never touched 2nd base. He turned and ran to the clubhouse instead. Evers is screaming for the ball. You're the Cubs manager. What do you do?
options: Appeal at 2nd base immediately — Merkle never touched it
Let it go — the winning run already scored
Appeal at 1st base — make Merkle go back
Argue that the hit should be ruled foul
explanations: This is exactly what happened — and it changed baseball history. Johnny Evers got a baseball (the actual game ball was lost in the crowd chaos), touched 2nd base, and appealed. Umpire Hank O'Day ruled Merkle out as the 3rd out via force play. Because the 3rd out was a force, the run that crossed home plate did NOT count. The game was declared a tie and replayed at season's end — the Cubs won that replay and the pennant. 'Merkle's Boner' became the most famous baserunning mistake in history and established forever that EVERY base must be touched.

The run does NOT automatically count. Under the rules, Merkle was forced to advance to 2nd (the batter's single forced him). If the defense touches 2nd with the ball before Merkle does, he's out on a force. And a 3rd out that's a force play erases ANY run scored on that play — even if the runner crossed home first.
The appeal must happen at the base the runner failed to touch — 2nd base, not 1st. Merkle left 1st legally; he just never arrived at 2nd.
The hit was clearly fair. There's no basis to argue otherwise. The appeal at 2nd is the only play.
scenarios.concept: Touch every base — Merkle's failure to touch 2nd in 1908 cost the Giants the pennant and established that force-play appeals erase runs scored on the same play

scenarios.title: Infield Fly Rule
scenarios.description: Runners on 1st and 2nd, 1 out. The batter pops up to shallow left field. The umpire raises his hand and yells 'Infield fly!' What does this mean?
options: The batter is automatically out, runners can advance at their own risk
The play is dead — nobody moves
The fielder MUST catch it or it's an error
The runners must tag up like a regular fly ball
explanations: Correct! The infield fly rule protects runners from a fielder intentionally dropping the pop-up to turn a double play. Batter is out whether caught or not — runners can advance at their risk.
The ball is still live! Runners can advance at their own risk.
The rule doesn't require a catch — the batter is out either way.
They don't have to tag up because the batter is already called out. But they CAN stay on base safely.
scenarios.concept: Infield fly rule: batter is out, ball is live, runners advance at own risk — prevents trick double plays

scenarios.title: What's a Balk?
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st base. The pitcher comes set, then flinches his shoulder toward home plate but doesn't throw. What's the call?
options: No call — he didn't actually pitch
Balk! The runner gets 2nd base
Strike — any motion toward home counts
Ball — failed pitch attempt
explanations: Any motion toward home that doesn't result in a pitch is a balk when runners are on base.
Once a pitcher starts his motion toward home, he MUST deliver the pitch. Any flinch, stop, or fake is a balk. The runner advances one base.
It's not a strike because the ball was never thrown.
It's not counted as a ball either — it's a balk, which is its own category.
scenarios.concept: Any start-and-stop motion by the pitcher with runners on base is a balk — one free base

scenarios.title: Dropped Third Strike
scenarios.description: 2 outs, nobody on base. The pitcher throws strike three, but the ball bounces in the dirt and gets past the catcher. What can the batter do?
options: Nothing — strikeout, he's automatically out
Run to first! If the catcher doesn't throw him out, he's safe
Only run if the umpire signals
Ask the umpire for another pitch
explanations: With less than 2 outs AND a runner on 1st, you'd be right. But with 2 outs or 1st base empty, the dropped 3rd strike rule is in effect.
On a dropped third strike with 2 outs (or no runner on 1st), the batter becomes a runner! Sprint to first. If the catcher's throw doesn't beat you, you're safe.
The umpire doesn't signal — you need to KNOW the rule and react immediately.
There's no do-over for a dropped third strike. Know the rule and RUN.
scenarios.concept: Dropped third strike: with 2 outs or 1st base empty, the batter can run to first

scenarios.title: Appeal Play — Missing a Base
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st takes off on contact. Ball is hit deep to center — off the wall! The runner rounds 2nd but barely misses touching the bag. He reaches 3rd. Can the defense do anything?
options: Nothing — the play is over
Appeal! Throw to 2nd base and the runner is out
They can only appeal between innings
The runner has to go back and touch 2nd
explanations: The defense absolutely can appeal a missed base — it's one of the most missed rules in baseball.
The defensive team can 'appeal' by throwing to 2nd base while it's still a live ball. If the umpire saw the runner miss the bag, he's OUT. This has decided real MLB games.
Appeals can happen at any time while the ball is live — not just between innings.
Once the runner is at 3rd, he can't go back. The defense must appeal.
scenarios.concept: The defense can appeal a missed base — always touch every bag, even in the heat of the play

scenarios.title: Obstruction vs Interference
scenarios.description: Runner heading to 3rd base. The third baseman is standing in the baseline waiting for the throw, but doesn't have the ball yet. The runner has to go around him. What's the call?
options: Nothing — the fielder has the right to the baseline
Obstruction on the fielder — runner gets the base
Interference on the runner — runner is out
The runner should have slid
explanations: A fielder only has the right to the baseline when fielding the ball or in possession of it.
Obstruction! A fielder without the ball cannot block the baseline. The runner is awarded at least 3rd base. In the 2013 World Series, this call ended Game 3.
Interference is called on the RUNNER, not the fielder. This is obstruction.
Sliding doesn't matter. The fielder illegally blocked the path.
scenarios.concept: Obstruction: fielders can't block the baseline without the ball — runners get the base

scenarios.title: Designated Hitter Rule
scenarios.description: You're the manager setting your lineup. Your pitcher is due up 4th in the batting order. What's your option?
options: The pitcher must bat — that's the rule
Use a designated hitter (DH) to bat in the pitcher's spot
Pinch hit for the pitcher every time he's due up
Let a fielder bat twice
explanations: In the AL (and now universally since 2022), the DH replaces the pitcher in the batting order.
The designated hitter (DH) bats in place of the pitcher. Since 2022, both AL and NL use the DH. The pitcher never has to bat.
Pinch hitting removes the pitcher from the game. The DH stays in the lineup all game.
A player can only occupy one spot in the batting order.
scenarios.concept: The designated hitter (DH) bats for the pitcher — universal in MLB since 2022

scenarios.title: Ground Rule Double
scenarios.description: Runner on 1st, 1 out. The batter hits a screaming line drive that bounces once in fair territory, then hops over the outfield fence. Where do the runners end up?
options: Home run! Both runners score
Runner goes to 3rd, batter gets 2nd — ground rule double
Runner scores, batter gets 2nd
Batter returns to hit again — do-over
explanations: A ball that bounces over the fence is NOT a home run — it's a ground rule double.
A ground rule double awards all runners exactly two bases from where they were. Runner on 1st goes to 3rd. Batter stops at 2nd. Even if the runner could have scored, he only gets two bases.
The runner on 1st only advances to 3rd — two bases from 1st base, not home.
There's no do-over. The ground rule double stands. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
scenarios.concept: Ground rule double: all runners advance exactly two bases — even if they could have scored

scenarios.title: Tag Up on a Fly Ball
scenarios.description: Runner on 3rd, 1 out. The batter hits a deep fly ball to center field. The runner wants to score after the catch. What MUST he do?
options: Start running as soon as the ball is hit
Wait on the base until the fielder CATCHES the ball, then run
Wait until the umpire signals
He can't advance on a fly ball
explanations: Leaving early means the defense can throw to 3rd for an appeal out.
The runner must 'tag up' — keep his foot on 3rd base until the ball is CAUGHT, then sprint home. If he leaves early and the defense appeals, he's out.
There's no special umpire signal — you have to watch the catch yourself.
Runners absolutely can advance after a catch — that's the whole point of a sacrifice fly.
scenarios.concept: Tag up: on a fly ball, touch your base until the catch is made, THEN you can advance

scenarios.title: Infield Fly Rule
scenarios.description: Runners on first and second with one out. The batter pops up a high fly ball to the shortstop area. The umpire immediately points up and yells 'Infield fly!' before the ball even comes down. The shortstop lets the ball drop on purpose. What happens?
options: The batter is out automatically — the infield fly rule protects the runners
The ball is dead and all runners return to their bases
It's a live ball and the shortstop can throw to second for a double play
The batter gets to run to first because the fielder dropped it on purpose
explanations: Correct! The infield fly rule says that when there are runners on first and second (or bases loaded) with less than two outs, a pop fly that an infielder can catch with ordinary effort makes the batter automatically out — even if the fielder drops it. This prevents fielders from intentionally dropping easy pop-ups to turn double plays on confused runners.

The ball is NOT dead on an infield fly. It's still a live ball, and runners can advance at their own risk. They just aren't forced to run because the batter is already out. This is a common misconception about the rule.

Since the batter is already out due to the infield fly call, there's no force play anymore. The runners can stay on their bases safely. The whole point of the rule is to prevent this kind of trickery.
The batter is out regardless of whether the fielder catches it. That's the entire purpose of the infield fly rule — to protect the offense from fielders who might deliberately drop an easy fly ball.
scenarios.concept: The infield fly rule protects runners from being tricked. When called, the batter is automatically out even if the fielder drops the ball on purpose.

scenarios.title: Tagging on Fouls
scenarios.description: Runner on third base with one out. The batter hits a deep fly ball down the right field line, but it curves foul. The right fielder makes a nice running catch in foul territory. The runner on third wants to tag up and score. Is this allowed?
options: Yes — runners CAN tag up and advance on a caught foul ball
No — runners must stay on their base after a foul ball is caught
Only if the foul ball was caught in fair territory
Only if the umpire gives special permission
explanations: Correct! A caught foul ball is just like a caught fair ball for tagging up. Once the fielder catches it, runners can tag their base and advance at their own risk. This is a smart play — if the foul ball is caught deep enough, the runner can score! Sacrifice flies can happen on foul balls too.

This is a common myth! Runners absolutely can tag up on caught foul balls. Any time a fly ball is caught — fair or foul — runners may tag up and advance. This is especially important for runners on third.
It doesn't matter where the fielder is standing when catching a foul ball. Fair or foul territory, a catch is a catch. Runners can tag up on any caught fly ball regardless of where the fielder is.
The umpire doesn't need to give permission for a tag-up. It's the runner's right to tag and advance on any caught fly ball. The runner just needs to touch the base after the catch before running.
scenarios.concept: Runners can tag up and advance on ANY caught fly ball — fair or foul. A deep foul ball catch can turn into a sacrifice fly!

scenarios.title: Ball Hits a Runner
scenarios.description: Runner on second base, no outs. The batter hits a sharp ground ball up the middle. The ball passes the pitcher and hits the runner standing on the basepath between second and third. No fielder had a chance to make a play on the ball. What's the call?
options: The runner is out, the batter gets first base, and the ball is dead
The ball is still live — play continues as normal
Both the runner and batter are out — double play
The batter is out because the hit ball was interfered with
explanations: Correct! When a batted ball hits a runner in fair territory before passing a fielder (other than the pitcher), the runner is out, the ball is dead, and the batter is awarded first base. It doesn't matter if the runner tried to avoid the ball. This prevents runners from intentionally getting in the way of ground balls.
The ball is NOT live when it hits a runner. It becomes dead immediately. If the ball were still live, runners could deliberately get hit by ground balls to break up plays or cause confusion.
Only the runner who was hit is called out. The batter is awarded first base, not called out. You can't penalize the batter for something the runner did (or couldn't avoid).
The batter did nothing wrong — they hit a fair ball. The runner happened to be in the path. The batter is rewarded with first base, not punished with an out.
scenarios.concept: If a batted ball hits a runner before passing a fielder, the runner is out and the ball is dead. The batter gets first base.

scenarios.title: Dropped Third Strike
scenarios.description: Two outs, nobody on base. The pitcher throws a nasty curveball for strike three, but the ball bounces in the dirt and gets away from the catcher. The batter starts to walk back to the dugout. The catcher hasn't picked up the ball yet. What should the batter do?
options: Run to first base! On a dropped third strike, the batter can try to reach first safely
Walk to the dugout — strike three means you're out no matter what
Wait for the umpire to tell you whether to run or not
Stay in the box and ask for another pitch since the catcher dropped it
explanations: Correct! On a dropped third strike, the batter can run to first base if first base is unoccupied or there are two outs. The catcher must either tag the batter or throw to first to complete the out. Many runners have reached base this way — never assume you're out! Always run on a ball in the dirt with two strikes.

Never walk to the dugout on a dropped third strike! This is one of the most important rules to know. If the catcher doesn't catch strike three cleanly, you have a chance to reach base. Always be alert and run!
Don't wait for the umpire — just run! The umpire will signal strike three, but it's YOUR job to know the dropped third strike rule and take off for first base. Time is everything here.
You don't get an extra pitch. Strike three is still strike three — but if the catcher drops it, the play isn't over. You need to beat the throw to first base to be safe, just like on a ground ball.
scenarios.concept: On a dropped third strike, the batter can run to first if it's unoccupied or there are two outs. Never walk away — always run!

scenarios.title: What's a Balk?
scenarios.description: Runner on first base with no outs. The pitcher starts his windup but suddenly stops and doesn't throw the ball. The umpire immediately calls 'Balk!' The runner on first looks confused. What happens when a balk is called?
options: All runners advance one base — the balk gives them a free base
The pitch is just called a ball and the count changes
The runner on first is sent back and the pitcher gets a warning
The play is dead and everything resets — do-over for the pitcher
explanations: Correct! A balk means all runners advance one base for free. A balk is an illegal motion by the pitcher designed to trick the runners. The most common balks are: starting your windup and stopping, faking a throw to first without stepping off the rubber, or making a pitching motion without throwing. The rule prevents pitchers from deceiving runners.

A balk is not just a ball added to the count. It's a much bigger penalty — all runners move up one base. If a runner is on third, a balk scores a run! Pitchers have to be very careful with their motions.
There are no warnings for balks in baseball. Once the umpire sees an illegal motion, the balk is called immediately and runners advance. Pitchers learn proper mechanics to avoid balks.

The play doesn't reset. The balk has a real consequence — runners advance. You can't just call a do-over when a pitcher makes an illegal move. The penalty is there to keep the game fair for baserunners.
scenarios.concept: A balk is an illegal motion by the pitcher. When called, all runners advance one base for free. Pitchers must follow the rules of their windup!

scenarios.title: Tag Up on Foul Fly
scenarios.description: Runners on first and third with one out. The batter hits a towering pop-up that drifts into foul territory near the third base dugout. The third baseman makes the catch. The runner on third touches the base and sprints home. The runner on first stays put. Is this play legal?
options: Yes — you can tag up on any caught fly ball, including foul balls
No — you cannot tag up on a ball caught in foul territory
Only the runner on third can tag up, not the runner on first
It's the umpire's judgment call whether the catch was close enough to fair
explanations: Correct! Any caught fly ball — fair or foul — allows runners to tag up and advance. The runner on third tagging and scoring on a caught foul pop-up is a perfectly legal and smart play. If the catch is deep enough, it works just like a sacrifice fly. Both runners could tag up if they wanted to.
This is a common misunderstanding! Foul balls that are caught are treated exactly like fair ball catches for tag-up purposes. Runners can absolutely tag and advance once the catch is made.
Both runners can tag up on any caught fly ball. The runner on first could try to advance to second if they wanted, though it would be risky on a short pop-up. Any runner can tag up on any catch.
The fair/foul distinction doesn't matter for tagging up. A catch in foul territory works the same as a catch in fair territory. There's no judgment call needed — the rule is clear.
scenarios.concept: You can tag up on ANY caught fly ball — fair or foul! Smart baserunners are always ready to advance after a catch.

scenarios.title: The Appeal Play
scenarios.description: Runner on second, one out. A deep fly ball is hit to center field and caught. The runner on second tags up and advances to third. But the center fielder notices the runner may have left second base a split second before the catch. The defense wants to challenge. How do they appeal?
options: Throw the ball to second base and the fielder steps on the bag while the defense asks the umpire to make the call
Yell at the umpire that the runner left early
Push a button to trigger instant replay
The pitcher just has to point at the runner and the umpire checks
explanations: Correct! An appeal play requires the defense to physically throw the ball to the base in question and have a fielder touch the bag while appealing to the umpire. The umpire then rules whether the runner left early. If the runner did leave early, they're out. It's the defense's responsibility to make the appeal — umpires won't call it on their own.

Just yelling at the umpire doesn't count as a proper appeal. The defense must make a physical play — throw the ball to the base and have a fielder touch it while asking for the call. Umpires require a formal appeal.

Instant replay isn't used for tag-up appeals in this way. The defense must make a traditional appeal play on the field. In some leagues, replay can be used afterward, but the appeal must happen first.
There's no pointing rule in baseball. The defense has to actually throw the ball to the base and ask the umpire to make the call. It requires a real baseball play, not just a gesture.
scenarios.concept: An appeal play means the defense throws to the base and asks the umpire to rule. Umpires don't call tag-up violations unless the defense appeals!

scenarios.title: Running the Baseline
scenarios.description: Runner on first tries to steal second. The catcher throws to the shortstop covering the bag. The runner sees the throw coming and veers way off to the right — almost into the outfield grass — to try to dodge the tag. The shortstop chases and tags him. The runner says he was safe because he wasn't tagged on the base. Is the runner out?
options: Yes — the runner is out for running more than 3 feet outside the baseline to avoid a tag
No — runners can run anywhere they want on the field
Only if the umpire thinks the runner went too far — it's a judgment call with no set distance
The runner is safe because the shortstop left the base to chase him
explanations: Correct! A runner is out if they run more than three feet away from the baseline to avoid being tagged. The baseline is defined as a direct line between the runner and the base they're heading toward. Runners can't just run anywhere on the field to dodge tags — that would make the game chaos!

Runners definitely cannot run anywhere they want. There's a three-foot rule: if you deviate more than three feet from the baseline to avoid a tag, you're out. This keeps the game fair for both offense and defense.
There IS a set distance — three feet. The umpire judges whether the runner went beyond three feet from the baseline, but the rule is specific about the distance. It's not purely subjective.
The shortstop is allowed to chase a runner. That's part of playing defense. The issue is that the runner went too far off the baseline to avoid the tag, which is against the rules.
scenarios.concept: Runners must stay within three feet of the baseline when a fielder is trying to tag them. Going outside that line means you're out!

scenarios.title: Ground Rule Double
scenarios.description: The batter hits a hard line drive that bounces in fair territory in right field, then hops over the outfield wall. The batter is rounding first and heading to second. The runner who was on first is already past third base heading home. What's the ruling?
options: It's a ground rule double — batter gets second base, all runners advance two bases from where they started
The batter gets a home run since the ball went over the wall
It's a dead ball — everyone goes back to where they were
The batter gets only a single because the ball bounced first
explanations: Correct! When a fair ball bounces over the outfield wall, it's an automatic double (commonly called a ground rule double). The batter is awarded second base, and all runners advance two bases from where they were when the ball was hit. So the runner on first goes to third — NOT home, even though he was almost there. This can actually hurt the offense sometimes!
A home run requires the ball to fly over the wall without bouncing. A ball that bounces over is an automatic double, not a homer. The bounce is the key difference.

The ball is dead, but everyone doesn't go back. The rule awards the batter second base and advances all runners two bases. The play has a result — it's just determined by the rule rather than continued play.

A ground rule double is worth two bases, not one. Even though the ball bounced, the rule awards extra bases because the ball left the field of play. The batter gets second, and runners move up two bases.
scenarios.concept: A ground rule double happens when a fair ball bounces over the outfield wall. The batter gets second base and all runners advance two bases.

scenarios.title: Two Runners, One Base
scenarios.description: Runners on first and second, no outs. The batter hits a fly ball to right field. The runner on second tags up and advances to third. But the runner on first panics, thinks it's going to drop, and also runs to third base. Now both runners are standing on third base at the same time. Who gets the base?
options: The lead runner (who was on second) owns the base — the trailing runner can be tagged out
The first runner to touch the base gets it — first come, first served
Both runners are safe as long as they're touching the base
Both runners are out for causing confusion on the basepaths
explanations: Correct! When two runners end up on the same base, the lead runner (the one who was originally ahead on the basepaths) is entitled to the base. The trailing runner is considered 'off base' and can be tagged out. In this case, the runner who was on second base owns third, and the runner from first can be tagged out.

It's not about who arrives first. Baseball gives priority to the lead runner — the one who was originally farther ahead. Even if the trailing runner got there first, the lead runner has the right to the base.
Two runners cannot both be safe on the same base. Only one runner can legally occupy a base at a time. The lead runner gets priority, and the other runner is at risk of being tagged out.
Only the trailing runner is out, not both. The lead runner didn't do anything wrong — they legally advanced to third. You can't penalize a runner for their teammate's mistake.
scenarios.concept: When two runners are on the same base, the lead runner (the one originally ahead) gets the base. The other runner can be tagged out!

scenarios.title: Is He Out? The Third Strike Rule
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 8th, tie game. The pitcher throws a nasty splitter that hits the dirt — strike three! But the catcher bobbles it and the ball rolls to the backstop. Runner on third, 2 outs. The home crowd cheers thinking the inning is over. Is the batter out?
options: The batter is out — strike three ended the inning regardless
The batter can run to first — first base is empty and there are 2 outs
The runner on third automatically scores — the ball was a wild pitch
The umpire must signal before any runners can move
explanations: Not so fast! When the catcher doesn't cleanly catch strike three AND first base is unoccupied with 2 outs, the batter becomes a runner. The inning is NOT over until someone tags the batter or throws him out at first.

Correct! This is the dropped third strike rule in action. With 2 outs and first base empty, the batter can run to first. The catcher must recover the ball and throw to first (or tag the batter) before he's safe. Meanwhile, the runner on third might score on the chaos!

The runner on third doesn't automatically score — he can only advance at his own risk. If the catcher recovers the ball quickly, the runner might stay at third. The wild pitch just means the play is live.

No umpire signal is needed — the play is live immediately! Once the catcher fails to catch strike three, runners can advance at their own risk. The batter-runner needs to recognize the situation and run.
scenarios.concept: With 2 outs and first base empty, a dropped/uncaught third strike lets the batter run to first — the play is live and the catcher must throw him out.

scenarios.title: Batting Out of Order — Who's Really Out?
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th. Your team's lineup card shows: #3 hitter Smith, then #4 hitter Jones. But Jones accidentally bats in Smith's spot and lines a double! The opposing manager notices and appeals to the umpire. What happens?
options: Jones is called out and the double is erased
Smith is called out — HE was the proper batter who didn't bat
Nothing happens — once the pitch was thrown, it's too late
Jones keeps the double but gets a warning for next time
explanations: Jones isn't the one penalized — he's the improper batter but the PROPER batter is the one declared out. This is the most confusing part of the rule.

Correct! MLB Rule 6.03(b): when batting out of order is appealed, the PROPER batter (Smith) is declared out, and any advancement by the improper batter (Jones) is nullified. The double is erased. The next batter is whoever follows Smith in the order. The key: the defense must appeal BEFORE the next pitch.
It's only too late if the next pitch has already been thrown. Until then, the defense can appeal. Timing is everything with this rule.
There are no warnings in baseball for batting out of order. It's an immediate appeal play with real consequences — an out is recorded.
scenarios.concept: Batting out of order: the PROPER batter (who should have batted) is declared out, not the one who actually batted. Appeal must come before the next pitch.

scenarios.title: Runner's Lane Interference — The Last 45 Feet
scenarios.description: Runner on first, nobody out. The batter hits a ground ball to the pitcher. The pitcher throws to first, but the batter-runner is running in fair territory (on the grass) instead of in the runner's lane (the dirt path to the right of the foul line). The throw hits the runner in the back. Is this interference?
options: Yes — the batter-runner must run in the runner's lane for the last 45 feet to first
No — the runner can run anywhere between the foul lines
It's only interference if the runner intentionally got in the way
It depends on whether the runner was inside or outside the foul line
explanations: Correct! MLB Rule 5.09(a)(11): In the last 45 feet to first base, the batter-runner must run within the 3-foot runner's lane (the dirt path to the right of the foul line). If running outside the lane and interfering with the throw, the batter-runner is OUT. This is one of the most commonly misunderstood interference calls in baseball.
The runner cannot run just anywhere. The runner's lane exists specifically to prevent the batter-runner from interfering with throws to first base.
Intent doesn't matter here — it's a position-based rule. If the runner is outside the lane AND the throw is affected, that's interference regardless of intent.
The key is the runner's lane itself (the 3-foot dirt path), not the foul line. The lane is entirely in foul territory for the last 45 feet to first.
scenarios.concept: The runner's lane rule: batter-runners must use the 3-foot dirt lane for the last 45 feet to first base or risk interference if a throw is affected.

scenarios.title: Catcher's Interference — Free Base or Keep the Play?
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 6th, runner on second, one out. The batter swings and his bat makes contact with the catcher's glove on the follow-through. He still manages to hit the ball into right field for a single, and the runner scores from second. The umpire calls catcher's interference. What happens next?
options: The batter gets first base and the runner goes back to second — that's the interference penalty
The offensive manager can CHOOSE: accept the interference (batter to 1st, runners advance one base) or decline it and take the result of the play
The play is dead immediately when interference is called — nothing counts
The batter is automatically out for swinging into the catcher's glove
explanations: This would be the default interference penalty, but there's an important exception when the batter reaches base AND all runners advance at least one base on the play.

Correct! MLB Rule 6.01(c): If the batter reaches first AND all runners advance at least one base on the play, the offensive manager can DECLINE the interference and keep the result of the play. Here, the single scored a run — much better than just a walk! The manager would decline interference and take the RBI single.
Catcher's interference is a delayed dead ball, not an immediate dead ball. The play is allowed to continue, THEN the manager chooses.
The batter is never out for catcher's interference — it's a penalty on the CATCHER, not the batter. The catcher's glove was in the wrong position.
scenarios.concept: Catcher's interference is a delayed dead ball — the offense can decline the penalty and keep the play result if all runners advanced at least one base.

scenarios.title: Time Play vs. Force Play — Does the Run Count?
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 9th, tie game. Runners on first and third, one out. The batter hits a ground ball to the shortstop who throws to second for the force out (2nd out), then the relay goes to first but the batter-runner beats the throw. Meanwhile, the runner from third crossed home plate BEFORE the force out at second was recorded. Does the run count?
options: Yes — the runner scored before the out was made, so the run counts
No — the third out was a force out, so no run can score regardless of timing
Yes — since the batter-runner was safe at first, all runners are safe
It depends on whether the umpire saw the runner touch home plate
explanations: This is the classic trap! Timing doesn't matter on force outs. When the third out is a force out, NO run can score on that play, even if the runner crossed the plate first. This is the force play exception.

Correct! MLB Rule 5.08(a) exception: A run does NOT score if the third out is a force out. The out at second was a force (runner from first was forced to advance), so even though the runner from third touched home before the tag, the run is wiped out. This is the MOST important timing rule in baseball.
The batter-runner being safe at first doesn't affect the force at second. The second out was still a force out, and that's what matters for the run-scoring rule.
The umpire's timing observation doesn't matter because this is a force play situation. Force outs automatically negate any runs scored on that play, regardless of when the runner touched home.
scenarios.concept: The force play exception: when the third out of an inning is a force out, NO run can score on that play — even if the runner crossed home plate before the out was recorded.

scenarios.title: Pitch Clock Violation — Who Gets Penalized?
scenarios.description: Top of the 3rd, runner on first, 1-1 count. The pitch clock is at 20 seconds (runners on base). The pitcher is set but hasn't started his delivery. The clock hits zero. What happens?
options: An automatic ball is added to the count — now it's 2-1
An automatic strike is added — pitchers are penalized more
The umpire gives a warning first, then a ball on the next violation
The pitch clock resets and the pitcher gets another 20 seconds
explanations: Correct! When the pitcher fails to start his delivery before the pitch clock expires, it's an automatic BALL added to the count. The count goes from 1-1 to 2-1. The 2023 pitch clock rule (MLB Rule 8.04) penalizes pitchers with a ball for being too slow. This keeps the game moving.
Strikes are the penalty for BATTERS who aren't ready (not alert in the box with 8 seconds left). Pitchers get balls, batters get strikes — each side is penalized in a way that hurts them.
There are no warnings — pitch clock violations are automatic from the first infraction. The rule was designed to be immediate and consistent.
The clock does not reset for violations. An automatic ball is assessed immediately. The pitcher violated the time limit and is penalized.
scenarios.concept: Pitch clock violations: pitcher too slow = automatic ball, batter not ready = automatic strike. No warnings — the penalty is immediate.

scenarios.title: Fan Interference — Home Run or Dead Ball?
scenarios.description: Bottom of the 8th, tie game, runner on second. The batter hits a deep fly ball to right field that's heading for the wall. A fan in the front row reaches OVER the wall into the field of play and catches the ball before the right fielder can make the play. What's the call?
options: Home run — the ball was going out anyway
Fan interference — dead ball, umpire places runners where they would have been
The batter is out because the ball was caught (even by a fan)
Replay review — only the replay officials can make this call
explanations: Even if the ball was going out, the umpire can't assume that. When a fan reaches into the field of play, the result is fan interference regardless of the ball's trajectory.

Correct! MLB Rule 6.01(e): When a spectator reaches INTO the field of play and interferes with a fielder, it's spectator interference. The ball is dead immediately, and the umpire places all runners where they believe the runners would have ended up without the interference. The fan reaching over the wall is the key — the field of play boundary is the TOP of the wall.
Fans can't record outs! The ball is dead the moment a fan interferes with play. The batter is not out.

While this IS reviewable, the on-field umpire makes the initial call. The key ruling is fan interference (dead ball), which the crew chief can make in real time. Review can confirm or overturn whether the fan actually reached over the wall.
scenarios.concept: Fan interference: when a spectator reaches INTO the field of play, it's a dead ball. The umpire places runners where they would have been. The boundary is the top of the wall.

scenarios.title: Pitch Clock — Batter Not Ready
scenarios.description: Top of the 5th, nobody on, 2-1 count. The batter steps out to adjust his gloves. The pitch clock shows 8 seconds left. He's still adjusting when it hits zero. What happens?
options: Automatic strike — now it's 2-2
Automatic ball — now it's 3-1
The umpire pauses the clock until the batter is ready
Nothing — batters can't get clock violations
explanations: Correct! The batter must be alert in the box with at least 8 seconds on the pitch clock. If the batter isn't ready when the clock expires, it's an automatic STRIKE. The count goes from 2-1 to 2-2. Pitchers get balls, batters get strikes — each side is penalized in a way that hurts them.
That's the penalty for pitchers, not batters. Pitchers get balls when they're too slow. Batters get strikes when they're not ready.
The clock does not pause for batter delays. The rule is designed to keep the game moving, with immediate consequences.
Both pitchers AND batters can get pitch clock violations. The batter must be ready with 8 seconds left on the clock.
scenarios.concept: Pitch clock violations: batters must be alert with 8 seconds left or it's an automatic strike

scenarios.title: Pitch Clock — Strategic Step-Off
scenarios.description: Bot 7th, runners on 1st and 2nd, 0 outs, tie game. The speedy runner on 2nd is dancing off the bag. Your pitcher has already used both of his disengagements this plate appearance. The clock shows 12 seconds. Can the pitcher step off the rubber again?
options: No — a third step-off is an automatic balk, runners advance
Yes — but only if the pitcher picks off the runner successfully
Yes — the pitcher can step off as many times as he wants
No — but the pitcher can request time from the umpire instead
explanations: Close, but not quite! A third disengagement IS a balk — UNLESS the pitcher successfully picks off the runner. It's a high-risk, high-reward play.

Correct! Under the 2023 pitch clock rules, pitchers get two disengagements (step-offs or pickoff throws) per plate appearance. A third disengagement is an automatic balk — UNLESS the pitcher picks off the runner. This creates a fascinating cat-and-mouse game: the runner knows the pitcher used both disengagements, so he can take a bigger lead. But if the pitcher reads him leaning too far, a third throw that gets him out resets the count.
Pitchers are limited to two disengagements per plate appearance under the 2023 rules. Unlimited step-offs slowed the game down and frustrated base stealers — that's exactly what the rule changed.
Requesting time doesn't pause the disengagement limit. The pitcher either throws a pitch, attempts a third pickoff (risky), or deals with the runner's bigger lead.
scenarios.concept: Pitchers get 2 disengagements per plate appearance. A third is an automatic balk unless the runner is picked off.

scenarios.title: Pitch Clock — Mound Visit Resets the Clock
scenarios.description: Top of the 6th, runners on 1st and 3rd, 1 out. The pitcher looks rattled — just walked two straight batters. The catcher trots to the mound for a quick chat. When the catcher returns, what happens to the pitch clock?
options: The clock resets to the full time (20 seconds with runners on)
The clock continues from where it was — the visit doesn't reset it
The clock resets, AND the pitcher's disengagement count resets too
The clock starts at 30 seconds after a mound visit as a bonus
explanations: Correct! Any mound visit — whether from the catcher, pitching coach, or manager — resets the pitch clock to its full amount. With runners on base, that's 20 seconds. This is why smart catchers time their mound visits: it gives the pitcher a breather AND resets the clock. But remember, teams have a limited number of mound visits per game (5 in MLB).
The clock always resets after a mound visit. This is one of the strategic benefits of visiting the mound — it's a built-in reset button.
The clock resets, but the pitcher's disengagement count does NOT reset. The two-disengagement limit is per plate appearance, and a mound visit doesn't change the plate appearance.
The clock resets to the standard amount (15 seconds with bases empty, 20 with runners on), not a bonus 30 seconds.
scenarios.concept: Mound visits reset the pitch clock to its full amount — a strategic benefit of visiting the mound

scenarios.title: Pitch Clock — What Counts as 'Ready'?
scenarios.description: You're stepping into the batter's box for the first time in a pitch clock game. The announcer says you must be 'alert to the pitcher' with 8 seconds on the clock. What does that actually mean you need to do?
options: Be in the batter's box with both feet, looking at the pitcher
Just stand near the plate — doesn't matter if you're looking
Have your bat on your shoulder in your stance
Be in the box but you can still be adjusting your gloves
explanations: Correct! 'Alert to the pitcher' means you must be in the batter's box AND looking at the pitcher, ready for the pitch to come. Both feet in the box, eyes on the mound. If you're still adjusting your helmet, looking at the scoreboard, or not in your stance, the umpire can call an automatic strike.
You need to be IN the batter's box specifically, not just near the plate. And you need to be looking at the pitcher — being distracted counts as not ready.
Having your bat on your shoulder isn't required — some hitters hold their bat up, some down. What matters is that you're in the box and looking at the pitcher.
If you're adjusting your gloves with 8 seconds left, you're NOT alert to the pitcher. Get your adjustments done early! Step out between pitches if needed, but be ready at 8 seconds.
scenarios.concept: Being 'alert to the pitcher' means both feet in the box, eyes on the mound, ready for the pitch

scenarios.title: Infield Fly — Does It Apply with Two Outs?
scenarios.description: Bot 6th, runners on 1st and 2nd, TWO outs. The batter pops the ball straight up near the pitcher's mound. An easy catch. Should the umpire call 'Infield Fly'?
options: No — infield fly does NOT apply with 2 outs
Yes — infield fly applies whenever there are runners on 1st and 2nd
Yes — any easy popup with runners on base is infield fly
No — but only because the ball is near the mound, not in the infield
explanations: Correct! The infield fly rule ONLY applies with less than 2 outs (0 or 1 out) AND runners on 1st and 2nd, or bases loaded. With 2 outs, the rule doesn't apply because there's no reason for the defense to intentionally drop the ball — they can just catch it for the third out. The rule exists to prevent the defense from getting a cheap double play by dropping the ball on purpose.
The infield fly rule requires less than 2 outs. With 2 outs, the defense would just catch the ball for the easy third out — there's no incentive to drop it.
The infield fly rule has specific conditions: less than 2 outs, runners on 1st and 2nd (or bases loaded), and a fair fly ball that an infielder can catch with ordinary effort.
The location of the ball doesn't determine whether the infield fly rule applies — the conditions do (less than 2 outs + runners on 1st and 2nd or bases loaded).
scenarios.concept: Infield fly rule only applies with less than 2 outs. With 2 outs, the defense has no reason to drop the ball.

scenarios.title: Infield Fly — Can Runners Still Advance?
scenarios.description: Bot 4th, runners on 1st and 2nd, 1 out. The batter pops up to shallow left field. The umpire calls 'Infield Fly, batter is out!' But the shortstop loses the ball in the sun and it drops! Can the runners advance?
options: Yes — runners can advance at their own risk, but they don't have to
No — since infield fly was called, the ball is dead and runners must stay
Yes — runners MUST advance because the ball wasn't caught
No — runners can only advance if they were tagging up when the ball dropped
explanations: Correct! When the infield fly rule is called, the batter is out regardless of whether the ball is caught. But the ball is still LIVE! Runners can advance at their own risk. They are NOT forced to run (since the batter is already out, the force is removed). If the ball drops, runners can try to advance, but they can be tagged out. If the ball is caught, runners must tag up before advancing — just like any fly ball. The key: the batter is out, but the runners still have options.
The ball is NOT dead on an infield fly! This is one of the most common misconceptions. The ball stays live, and runners can advance at their own risk.
Runners do NOT have to advance. The infield fly call removes the force play (because the batter is already out). Runners can choose to stay at their base if it's safer.
Runners don't need to be tagging up. If the ball drops, they can simply run — there's no force and no tag-up requirement on a dropped ball. But they CAN be tagged out.
scenarios.concept: On an infield fly, the batter is out but the ball is LIVE. Runners can advance at their own risk — the force is removed.

scenarios.title: Infield Fly — Foul Territory Twist
scenarios.description: Runners on 1st and 2nd, 0 outs. The batter pops the ball high near the 3rd base line. It could land fair or foul. The umpire calls 'Infield Fly If Fair!' The third baseman drifts over and catches it in FOUL territory. Is the batter out?
options: Yes — the fielder caught it, so the batter is out regardless of fair/foul
No — since the ball was caught in foul territory, the infield fly is cancelled and it's just a regular foul ball
Yes — the umpire already called it, so the batter is out either way
No — the batter is out because of the catch, not the infield fly rule
explanations: Correct! This is a tricky one. The umpire called 'Infield Fly If Fair' — meaning the infield fly would apply IF the ball lands fair. But here the fielder CAUGHT the ball. When any fly ball is caught — fair or foul territory — the batter is out. The infield fly rule doesn't even matter here because the catch itself retires the batter. The ump's conditional call ('If Fair') was correct procedure for a ball near the line.
If the ball DROPPED in foul territory, the infield fly would be cancelled and it would be a foul ball. But the ball was CAUGHT, so the batter is out on the catch — just like any caught foul fly ball.

The umpire's call was conditional ('If Fair'). Since the ball was caught in foul territory, the infield fly technically didn't apply. But the batter IS out — because of the catch, not the infield fly.

Actually, the batter is out because of the catch, which is correct. But the answer should acknowledge that the catch in foul territory makes the infield fly rule moot. The catch itself retires the batter.
scenarios.concept: 'Infield Fly If Fair' is a conditional call. If caught in foul territory, the infield fly doesn't apply — but the catch still retires the batter.

scenarios.title: Checked Swing — Who Makes the Call?
scenarios.description: The batter starts to swing and tries to hold back. The home plate umpire calls it a ball. The catcher thinks it was a swing. What happens next?
options: The catcher asks the home plate umpire to appeal to the base umpire for a second opinion
The play is final — the home plate umpire's call cannot be changed
The batter gets to decide if he swung or not
The pitching coach can challenge the call
explanations: On a checked swing, the catcher (or the pitcher or manager) can ask the home plate umpire to appeal to the first base umpire (for a right-handed batter) or third base umpire (for a lefty). The base umpire then makes the final ruling on whether the batter swung.
The home plate umpire's initial call CAN be overturned on a checked swing. The appeal to the base umpire is a built-in second opinion, and it happens all the time in baseball.
The batter never decides whether he swung — that's always the umpire's call. Baseball rules don't care what the batter thinks about his own swing.
Managers can't challenge checked swing calls with replay review. The appeal to the base umpire is the only recourse, and it must happen immediately.
scenarios.concept: On a checked swing, the defense can appeal to the base umpire for a second opinion — the first or third base umpire makes the final call

scenarios.title: Hit by Pitch — But It Was a Strike?
scenarios.description: The pitcher throws a curveball that breaks into the batter's hands. The batter doesn't move and the ball hits him. But the pitch was clearly in the strike zone. Does the batter get first base?
options: No — if the pitch is in the strike zone, it's a strike even if it hits the batter
Yes — any pitch that hits the batter awards first base, no matter what
The umpire decides based on whether the batter tried to avoid the pitch
It depends on what the count was before the pitch
explanations: Correct! Under MLB Rule 5.05(b)(2), if the pitch is in the strike zone when it hits the batter, it's called a strike — not a hit by pitch. The batter does NOT get first base. This surprises a lot of people, but the strike zone takes priority.
Not all hit-by-pitches award first base. If the pitch was in the strike zone, it's a strike. The location of the pitch matters more than the fact that it hit the batter.
The 'attempt to avoid' rule is separate from the strike zone rule. Even if the batter tries to avoid it, a pitch in the strike zone when it hits the batter is a strike, not a HBP.
The count doesn't change the rule. A pitch in the strike zone is a strike regardless of the count, even if it hits the batter.
scenarios.concept: A pitch in the strike zone is a strike even if it hits the batter — the strike zone takes priority over hit-by-pitch rules

scenarios.title: Force Play Removal — Tricky Situation
scenarios.description: Bases loaded, 1 out. Ground ball to the shortstop. He steps on second base to force out the runner from first. Now the shortstop throws to the catcher at home for what he thinks is the second force out. Is the play at home a force or a tag?
options: It's still a force at home — all runners were forced to advance on the ground ball
It's now a tag play — the force at home was removed when the runner behind was put out at second
The catcher can choose to either tag the runner or touch the plate
It's a force play because the bases were loaded
explanations: This is one of the trickiest plays in baseball. When the shortstop forced out the runner at second base, the runner going from second to third is no longer forced to advance — because the runner behind him (the one who was on first) is now out. Since the runner going to third isn't forced, the runner going home isn't forced either. The force was removed at every base ahead of the out.

Exactly right! Force removal is one of the most misunderstood rules in baseball. The out at second removes the force at third, which removes the force at home. The catcher must now TAG the runner sliding into home — just touching the plate is not enough.
The catcher cannot choose. Once the force is removed, it's a tag play only. The catcher must tag the runner with the ball before the runner touches home plate.
Bases loaded creates a force initially, but any out behind a runner removes the force ahead. The force at home was removed the moment the shortstop recorded the force out at second.
scenarios.concept: When a trailing runner is forced out, the force is removed for all runners ahead — subsequent plays become tag plays, not force plays

scenarios.title: Dead Ball — When Does Play Stop?
scenarios.description: Runner on first, 1 out. The pitcher throws and the ball hits the batter. The runner on first starts running to second during the confusion. Does the runner get to advance?
options: No — hit by pitch is a dead ball, so the runner must go back to first
The runner on first advances to second because he was forced by the batter going to first
Yes — smart baserunning, the runner gets second for free
It depends on whether the runner was already running when the pitch was thrown
explanations: The ball IS dead on a hit-by-pitch, but runners who are forced to advance still get to move up one base. Since the batter gets first base, the runner on first is forced to second.

Hit by pitch is a dead ball, meaning runners can only advance if they're forced. The batter goes to first, which forces the runner on first to move to second. But the runner on first can only go to second — he can't take third just because he was already running.
Smart baserunning doesn't apply here. On a dead ball, you can't take extra bases just because you were moving. Only forced advances are allowed.
Whether the runner was already in motion doesn't matter on a dead ball. The rule is simple: runners advance one base only if they're forced by the batter being awarded first base.
scenarios.concept: On a dead ball like hit-by-pitch, runners can only advance if forced — no extra bases on hustle during a dead ball

scenarios.title: Runner Leaves Early on Tag-Up
scenarios.description: Runner on third, 1 out. Deep fly ball to center field. The runner on third leaves the base before the catch is made and jogs home. The center fielder catches the ball. What happens?
options: The run counts — the runner was already past home plate when the catch was made
The defense can appeal by throwing to third base — if the runner left early, he's out
The runner is automatically out because the umpire saw him leave early
The runner can go back and re-tag third base to make it legal
explanations: The run does NOT automatically count. If the runner left third base before the catch was made, the defense can appeal by throwing the ball to third base (with a fielder touching the bag). If the umpire confirms the runner left early, the runner is out on the appeal.

Leaving early on a tag-up is NOT an automatic out — the defense must appeal the play by throwing to the base the runner left from. If they don't appeal, the run counts. This is why coaches yell 'Tag up!' to remind runners to wait for the catch.
Umpires don't call leaving early on their own. The defense must appeal by throwing to the base. If nobody on the defense notices, the run counts even if the runner did leave early.
Once the runner has scored, he cannot go back and re-tag. The play is over for the runner once he crosses home plate. The defense's only option is to appeal at third base.
scenarios.concept: On a tag-up, the defense must appeal if a runner leaves early — it's not an automatic out, and the run counts if nobody appeals

scenarios.title: Runner Interference on a Double Play
scenarios.description: Runner on first, nobody out. Ground ball to short. The shortstop throws to second for the force out. As the second baseman tries to complete the double play throw to first, the runner from first slides wide into the second baseman's legs, clearly out of the base path. What's the call?
options: Just the out at second — the runner is out and the throw to first was disrupted
Both runners are out — the sliding runner AND the batter-runner at first
Only the runner at second is out — the batter-runner is safe at first
The play stands as-is — hard slides are part of the game
explanations: Under Rule 6.01(j), if a runner intentionally interferes with a fielder's attempt to complete a double play by sliding out of the base path, both the runner AND the batter-runner are called out. This is the slide rule — designed to protect middle infielders from dangerous takeout slides.

Correct! The slide rule (sometimes called the Utley Rule after Chase Utley's 2015 NLDS slide) requires runners to make a bona fide slide. Going out of the base path to take out the fielder results in a double play being called: the sliding runner is out for interference, and the batter-runner is also out.
The interference penalty is specifically designed to give the defense the double play they would have gotten without the illegal slide. Both outs are called.

Hard slides within the base path are legal. But sliding OUTSIDE the base path to interfere with the throw is illegal interference under the slide rule. The rule distinguishes between a competitive play and a dangerous one.
scenarios.concept: The slide rule: runners must make a bona fide slide at second base — going out of the base path to disrupt a double play results in two outs

scenarios.title: Neighborhood Play at Second Base
scenarios.description: You're watching a close play. Runner on first, 1 out. Ground ball to the shortstop. The second baseman receives the throw near second base, transfers quickly, and throws to first for the double play. On replay, his foot was about 6 inches off the bag when he caught the ball. Is the force out at second valid?
options: The out stands — the neighborhood play allows the fielder to be close to the bag on double play turns
The runner is safe — the fielder must touch the base for a force out, period
It depends on whether the runner slid into the fielder
The umpire on the field makes the final call — replay can't overturn it
explanations: Under the current rules, replay review has a specific standard for double play turns at second base. If the fielder is 'in the vicinity' of the bag and any part of the tag play is to protect himself from a sliding runner, the out can stand. This is the neighborhood play exception — MLB specifically protects middle infielders on double play turns.

While technically the fielder should touch the base, MLB's replay rules give special latitude on double play turns at second base. The 'neighborhood play' has been part of baseball for decades, and even with replay, umpires allow fielders to be slightly off the bag to protect themselves.
The runner's slide matters for the slide rule, but the neighborhood play is about protecting the fielder regardless. If the fielder is in the vicinity and transitioning quickly, the out can stand.
Replay CAN review force plays at second base, but the standard is different from other force plays. The neighborhood play exception gives fielders more leeway during double play turns.
scenarios.concept: The neighborhood play allows second basemen to be slightly off the bag during double play turns — protecting the fielder from sliding runners

scenarios.title: Ghost Runner — Extra Innings Rule
scenarios.description: It's the 10th inning in a regular season MLB game. Under the automatic runner rule, who starts on second base, and what are the strategic implications?
options: The player who made the last out of the previous inning starts on 2nd — bunt him to third and try to score on a sac fly or groundout
Any player the manager chooses starts on 2nd — pick your fastest runner
The leadoff hitter of the inning starts on 2nd — the lineup determines everything
Nobody starts on 2nd — the ghost runner rule was eliminated after 2021
explanations: Under the automatic runner rule (introduced in 2020 and made permanent), the player who made the last out of the previous inning is placed on second base at the start of each half-inning in extras. The standard strategy is to sacrifice bunt the runner to third, then try to score on a sacrifice fly, groundout, or base hit. With nobody out and a runner on second, the run expectancy is over 1.1 runs.
The manager does NOT choose the runner. It's automatically the player who made the last out of the previous inning. The only strategic element is the hitting lineup and whether to bunt.

The last out of the previous inning, not the leadoff hitter, determines who starts on second. This means managers sometimes think about the end of the previous inning's batting order when deciding strategy.
The automatic runner rule is still in effect. It was made permanent for MLB regular season games. It does NOT apply in the postseason, where traditional extra-inning rules still apply.
scenarios.concept: The automatic runner rule places the last out of the previous inning on second base in extras — bunt to third, then try to score with a sac fly

scenarios.title: 0-0: First Pitch Approach (Batter)
scenarios.description: You're the batter. Count is 0-0. The pitcher has been throwing first-pitch fastballs all game. What's your approach?
options: Sit on the fastball and swing aggressively
Take the first pitch no matter what
Bunt for a surprise hit
Choke up and just make contact
explanations: MLB hitters bat .340 on first pitches they swing at. If you KNOW it's a fastball, be aggressive! Jumping on first-pitch fastballs is how you take advantage of pitcher patterns.
Always taking first pitches makes you predictable and puts you behind.
Bunting on the first pitch wastes the advantage of knowing what's coming.
Just making contact wastes the advantage. If you know fastball, drive it.
scenarios.concept: Hitting 0-0: if you know what's coming, be aggressive — hitters bat .340 on first pitches

scenarios.title: 0-2: Pitcher's Count (Batter)
scenarios.description: You're behind 0-2. The pitcher has dominated this at-bat. What's your strategy?
options: Protect the plate — shorten your swing, fight off tough pitches
Swing for the fences — make him pay if he makes a mistake
Take the next pitch — he'll probably waste one
Bunt — surprise the defense
explanations: Down 0-2, your job is to survive. Shorten up, foul off borderline pitches, and wait for a mistake. MLB hitters bat only .167 on 0-2 counts — be defensive.
Swinging big on 0-2 leads to strikeouts. The pitcher has the advantage.
Good pitchers don't always waste on 0-2 — many go right after you. Be ready.
A 0-2 bunt is desperation. Just compete with a shortened approach.
scenarios.concept: Down 0-2, become a fighter — shorten your swing and protect the plate (.167 BA in 0-2)

scenarios.title: 3-1: Hitter's Count
scenarios.description: Count is 3-1, you're the batter. A ball four puts you on base. The pitcher needs a strike. What's your approach?
options: Sit on your pitch and crush it — hitters bat .340 on 3-1
Take the pitch — get to a full count
Swing at anything close — you don't want to strike out
Protect — shorten up like you're behind in the count
explanations: 3-1 is the best hitter's count in baseball! The pitcher MUST throw a strike. Look for your pitch in your zone and drive it. MLB hitters bat .340+ on 3-1.
Why give away your best advantage? 3-1 is YOUR count.
Swinging at anything close isn't selective enough. LOOK for your pitch.
You don't need to protect on 3-1 — you have the leverage. Be aggressive.
scenarios.concept: 3-1 is the hitter's best count (.340+ BA) — the pitcher must come to you, sit on your pitch

scenarios.title: 1-2: Pitcher's Strategy
scenarios.description: You're the pitcher. Count is 1-2 on a slugger. You're ahead — one more strike gets him. What do you throw?
options: Fastball right down the middle — challenge him
Slider just off the plate — expand the zone
Changeup in the dirt — make him chase
Same pitch you just threw — repeat it
explanations: Down the middle on 1-2 is a gift. You're ahead — use that leverage.
Expand the zone! On 1-2, throw to spots where the hitter THINKS it's a strike but it's actually a ball. Chase pitches get strikeouts.
In the dirt is too obvious — most hitters won't chase that far down on 1-2.
Repeating the same pitch is predictable. Mix it up.
scenarios.concept: 1-2 counts: expand the zone with chase pitches just off the plate — make the hitter swing at balls

scenarios.title: 2-0: Pitcher in Trouble
scenarios.description: You're the pitcher. Behind 2-0 to a good hitter. You need a strike. What's your play?
options: Fastball — your most command-able pitch for a strike
Breaking ball — surprise him
Nibble the corners — don't give him anything to hit
Changeup low — steal a strike
explanations: On 2-0, throw your best strike. Fastballs have the highest strike rate. You NEED to get back in the count. Don't try to be cute — be accurate.
Breaking balls are harder to control. On 2-0, command is everything.
Nibbling risks falling 3-0 which is even worse. Attack the zone.
Changeup on 2-0 is risky — if it misses, you're 3-0.
scenarios.concept: Behind 2-0, throw your best fastball for a strike — command beats trickery when you're behind

scenarios.title: Full Count: 3-2 with 2 Outs
scenarios.description: Full count 3-2, 2 outs, runner on 1st. You're the batter. The runner is going on the pitch. What's your approach?
options: Swing at anything close — can't take strike 3
Be selective — wait for YOUR pitch in YOUR zone
Take the pitch — if it's a ball, you walk
Swing for extra bases to drive in the runner
explanations: Swinging at everything leads to weak contact on bad pitches.
Even on 3-2 with 2 outs, be selective within the zone. You don't need to swing at balls. LOOK for a pitch you can drive. The runner is going, so contact puts him in scoring position.
You can't just take on 3-2 — if it catches the corner, you're out looking.
Trying to do too much leads to overswinging. Just find a good pitch and put a good swing on it.
scenarios.concept: Full count discipline: be aggressively selective — swing at strikes, lay off balls, even with 2 strikes

scenarios.title: First Pitch After a Walk (Pitcher)
scenarios.description: You just walked the previous batter. New batter up. What should your first pitch be?
options: A careful pitch on the corner — don't fall behind again
A first-pitch strike — get ahead immediately
A pitchout to check the runner
Whatever you're most comfortable with
explanations: Being too careful after a walk often leads to another walk. Attack the zone.
After a walk, the most important thing is getting ahead on the new batter. Throw a strike. Pitchers who throw first-pitch strikes after walks cut their walk rate dramatically.
A pitchout on 0-0 puts you behind immediately. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
Comfort is secondary — the game situation demands a strike. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
scenarios.concept: After a walk, throw strike one immediately — getting ahead prevents walk snowballs

scenarios.title: 2-2: Even Count Battle
scenarios.description: Count is 2-2. Both sides have fought hard this at-bat. You're the pitcher. What's your approach to get the strikeout?
options: Your best pitch, best location — trust your stuff
Something he hasn't seen this at-bat
The same sequence that got you to 2-2
A fastball up to set up a breaking ball next pitch
explanations: Your best pitch is predictable if he's seen it multiple times this at-bat.
Change the eye level and the look! On 2-2, show something different. If you've been throwing low, go up. If fastballs, throw off-speed. The element of surprise gets strikeouts.
Repeating sequences is predictable for good hitters. Think about what gives your team the best result here.
Setting up a next pitch works, but you might not GET a next pitch — this could be ball three.
scenarios.concept: On 2-2, change the hitter's eye level — show something he hasn't seen this at-bat

scenarios.title: 3-0 Hitter's Count
scenarios.description: You're batting with a 3-0 count. The pitcher has missed badly with three straight pitches and is struggling with control. The bases are empty and it's the 4th inning of a close game. What's the smartest approach for this at bat?
options: Take the pitch — a 3-0 count is the best time to wait for ball four and a free walk
Swing at a fastball right down the middle if you get one, but take anything else
Swing at the first pitch you can reach — this pitcher can't throw strikes
Bunt for a base hit while the defense isn't expecting it
explanations: At 3-0, the pitcher is in deep trouble. Taking the pitch is usually the smartest play because you're one ball away from a free walk. Even if the pitcher throws a strike, you're still ahead 3-1 with the advantage. Let the struggling pitcher dig their own hole — patience wins in a 3-0 count.

Swinging 3-0 is sometimes okay for elite hitters who get the green light from their coach, but for most players, the best strategy is to take the pitch. The odds of a walk are very high, and there's no reason to help a struggling pitcher by swinging.

Swinging at anything you can reach on 3-0 is reckless. The whole point of a 3-0 count is that the pitcher can't find the zone. Let them keep missing! Swinging at a marginal pitch lets the pitcher off the hook.
Bunting on 3-0 makes zero sense. You're about to get a free base via a walk. Why would you bunt and risk making an out when the pitcher can't throw strikes? Be patient and take your free base.
scenarios.concept: A 3-0 count is called a 'hitter's count' because the pitcher is in trouble. The smartest play is usually to take the pitch and get closer to a walk.

scenarios.title: Behind 0-1
scenarios.description: The pitcher threw a first-pitch strike and now you're behind 0-1. The pitcher has a good fastball and a sharp breaking ball. You're a contact hitter who doesn't strike out much. How do you adjust your approach now that you're behind in the count?
options: Shorten your swing and look to make contact — protect the zone
Keep swinging the same way — one strike doesn't change anything
Guess breaking ball and sit on it exclusively
Look for a pitch you can pull for extra bases to make up for falling behind
explanations: Smart adjustment! At 0-1, the pitcher has the slight advantage and might throw something tricky. Shortening your swing and focusing on making contact keeps you in the at bat. You don't need to do anything special — just put the bat on the ball and avoid falling further behind to 0-2.

Actually, one strike changes a lot. At 0-1, the pitcher can afford to throw a waste pitch or expand the zone. You need to adjust by being more selective on pitches outside the zone while still protecting against strikes. Approach should always adapt to the count.

Guessing one pitch exclusively at 0-1 is risky. If you guess wrong, you could fall to 0-2 and be in real trouble. At 0-1, stay balanced and ready for any pitch in the zone. Guessing games are for deeper counts.

Trying to pull everything for power when behind in the count leads to weak grounders and strikeouts. Contact hitters succeed by using the whole field. Stay within your game — don't try to be a power hitter because of one strike.
scenarios.concept: When you fall behind 0-1, shorten your swing and focus on contact. Protect the zone and stay alive in the at bat.

scenarios.title: 1-2 Two Outs Rally
scenarios.description: Two outs, runners on second and third. Your team is down by one run in the 7th inning. The count is 1-2 — the pitcher is one strike away from ending the inning. He's been mixing fastballs and sliders all game. You need to keep this rally alive.
options: Choke up on the bat, shorten your swing, and fight off anything close — foul off tough pitches until you get something to hit
Sit on fastball and take a big cut — go for the game-tying hit
Take the next pitch hoping it's a ball to even the count at 2-2
Swing at anything near the zone — you can't afford to take strike three looking
explanations: This is elite hitting mentality! At 1-2 with the game on the line, the priority is survival. Choke up, shorten your swing, and battle. Foul off tough pitches to stay alive. Great hitters turn 1-2 counts into 3-2 counts all the time by fighting off pitches. Eventually the pitcher might make a mistake you can drive.

Taking a big hack at 1-2 with two outs is how you end up walking back to the dugout. The pitcher has the advantage and might throw something nasty. A big swing has more holes in it. Shorten up and battle.

Taking a pitch at 1-2 is extremely risky. If it catches the corner, you're out looking — and taking a called third strike with runners on feels terrible. Be ready to swing at anything close and protect the plate.

While you need to protect the zone, swinging at 'anything near the zone' leads to chasing bad pitches. There's a difference between protecting the plate with a short swing and flailing at everything. Stay disciplined but aggressive.
scenarios.concept: In a 1-2 count with the game on the line, choke up and battle. Foul off tough pitches and wait for your opportunity — never give in to the pitcher.

scenarios.title: First Pitch Fastball
scenarios.description: You step into the batter's box for your first at bat. This pitcher throws a first-pitch fastball to almost every batter — you noticed while watching from the dugout. The pitcher winds up and here comes the first pitch. It's a fastball right down the middle!
options: Swing! If you know a first-pitch fastball is coming, be ready to attack it
Take it for a strike to see the pitcher's speed and get comfortable
Bunt it — surprise the defense on the very first pitch
Foul it off on purpose to see more pitches
explanations: Yes! If you've done your homework and know the pitcher likes to throw first-pitch fastballs, being ready to attack it is a huge advantage. The first pitch is often the best pitch you'll see in an at bat because the pitcher is trying to get ahead. A fastball down the middle on the first pitch is a gift — don't let it go!

Seeing a pitch to get comfortable sounds smart, but you just gave up a meatball down the middle. Sometimes the first pitch IS the best pitch. If you know it's coming, attack it! You don't get that pitch back.

Bunting a pitch right down the middle that you knew was coming wastes a great opportunity. You could have driven it for extra bases. Bunting has its place, but not when you're ambushing a predictable fastball.
Fouling off a pitch on purpose is a waste of a hittable pitch. That fastball down the middle might be the best pitch you see all at bat. Don't play games — when you get your pitch, swing at it!
scenarios.concept: Do your homework! Watch the pitcher from the dugout. If you know a first-pitch fastball is coming, be aggressive and ready to attack.

scenarios.title: 2-0 Sitting Fastball
scenarios.description: You're at the plate and the count is 2-0 after two balls off the plate. The pitcher needs to throw a strike to avoid falling behind 3-0. You're a good hitter having a great game. Your coach gave you the green light to swing. Statistics show pitchers throw fastballs about 70% of the time on 2-0 counts.
options: Look for a fastball in your favorite zone — if you get it, drive it hard
Swing at any strike — you need to be aggressive on 2-0
Take the pitch to try to get to 3-0 and a guaranteed fastball
Look for the breaking ball since the pitcher might try to trick you
explanations: Perfect approach! At 2-0, you can be selective AND aggressive. Pick one zone (like middle-in or middle-out) and one pitch (fastball). If you get exactly what you're looking for, swing hard. If not, take it — you're still ahead in the count. This is how smart hitters do damage on 2-0 counts.

Being aggressive is right, but swinging at ANY strike is too loose. At 2-0, you have the luxury of being picky. You don't have to swing at a borderline strike or a pitch in a tough location. Be aggressive only on YOUR pitch.

While taking to 3-0 isn't terrible, you're giving up one of the best hitting counts in baseball. Pitchers throw fastballs 70% of the time at 2-0 because they need a strike. That's a perfect time to sit on one and punish it.
At 2-0, pitchers overwhelmingly throw fastballs because they need to get back in the count. Looking breaking ball on a 2-0 count is overthinking it. Play the percentages — sit fastball and react.
scenarios.concept: A 2-0 count is a great opportunity. Look for a fastball in YOUR zone — if you get it, swing hard. If not, you're still ahead in the count.

scenarios.title: Full Count Two Outs
scenarios.description: Full count, 3-2, with two outs. The runners are going on the pitch because any walk or hit puts them in motion. You're at the plate against a tired reliever in the 8th inning. Your team trails by two runs with runners on first and second.
options: Protect the plate — fight off anything close and don't go down looking
Sit on one specific pitch and take a huge swing at it
Take the pitch hoping for ball four
Close your eyes and swing as hard as you can
explanations: On a 3-2 count with two outs, the worst thing you can do is take a called strike three. Expand your zone slightly, shorten your swing, and fight off borderline pitches. The runners are moving, so even a single could score both runs. Just put the ball in play and let good things happen.

Sitting on one specific pitch in a 3-2 two-out count is too risky. If you guess wrong, the pitch might catch the corner and you're out looking. You need to be ready for anything in the zone and protect the plate.

Taking a 3-2 pitch hoping for ball four is extremely risky with two outs. If it catches the corner, you struck out looking with runners on and your team needed you. You can't leave the bat on your shoulder here — protect the plate!

Closing your eyes and swinging is not a strategy — it's giving up. Keep your eyes on the ball, look for something in the zone, shorten your swing, and compete. Seeing the ball is literally the most important part of hitting.
scenarios.concept: On a full count with two outs, protect the plate! Shorten your swing and don't go down looking. Put the ball in play and make the defense work.

scenarios.title: 0-2 Waste Pitch
scenarios.description: You're pitching with an 0-2 count on the batter. You've got him in a deep hole with two quick strikes. Your catcher puts down the sign. What's the best strategy when you're ahead 0-2?
